The Text-to-CadQuery benchmark reports state-of-the-art CAD generation results using CadQuery code output. A fine-tuned Qwen2.5-3B model achieved a Flash accuracy of 69.30%, a 10.5% absolute improvement over the baseline, and shows the best geometric scores (F1 median 0.9836, volumetric IoU median 0.9868). The paper evaluates geometric fidelity with a point-based F1 (10,000 sampled surface points, threshold τ = 0.02) and volumetric IoU (voxelization resolution 0.02). Larger models do not automatically yield better accuracy: Mistral-7B underperforms Qwen2.5-3B on accuracy metrics though it attains the lowest Invalid Rate (1.32), indicating better syntactic/executable code generation. A GPT-4.1 mini fine-tuned on 10k examples produced fully executable code (0% Invalid Rate) but its accuracy matched the 7B model, highlighting that larger or more capable LLMs improve executability but still require sufficient task data to improve 3D generation accuracy. The dataset contains 170,000 text–CadQuery pairs and the authors observe clear scaling benefits up to 3B, with potential underfitting for 7B due to limited data.

## pqac-00000017

From text 'schupbach2025fromtextto pages 7-8'.

Aurel Schüpbach, Raul San Miguel, Julian Ferchow, and Mirko Meboldt. From text to design: a framework to leverage llm agents for automated cad generation. Proceedings of the Design Society, 5:1893-1902, Aug 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1017/pds.2025.10203, doi:10.1017/pds.2025.10203. This article has 4 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt identifies several limitations and challenges for AI/LLM-driven CAD generation relevant to manufacturability, editability, and parametric control. First, the framework primarily focuses on raw geometry creation and "overlooks key aspects such as manufacturability or the practical application of each geometry," suggesting a lack of built-in design-for-manufacturing (DfM) checks or process-specific constraints (e.g., for AM, injection molding, milling). Second, generated models suffer from accuracy and robustness issues: some LLMs repeatedly fail specific primitives ("failed to generate the right-angle geometry accurately") and less capable models can produce no viable geometries at all. Complex tasks (topology optimization, patterning, mirroring) lower success rates significantly. Third, error detection and iterative correction are limited by the workflow's visual capabilities — "reliance on a single perspective" hampers the LLM's ability to find and fix mistakes; the authors propose "multiple views with spatial annotations" and "visual aids, such as sketches" to improve interpretability. Finally, the paper recommends extending agent workflows with explicit DfM guidelines and validated case studies to improve manufacturability and practical application, implying current outputs may be hard to edit, validate, or control parametrically without further tooling and constraints.


---

## pqac-00000018

From text 'liu2508breplerlanguageguidedediting pages 8-9'.

Yilin Liu, Niladri Shekhar Dutt, Changjian Li, and Niloy J. Mitra. B-repler: language-guided editing of cad models. ArXiv, Aug 2508. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2508.10201, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2508.10201. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt documents several practical limitations of AI/LLM-driven CAD generation and editing. Tokenization/quantization approaches (used by CAD-Editor) produce coordinate precision loss and unstable token predictions, causing misaligned or disconnected components and even split models. Sketch-and-extrude token representations limit scalability to complex primitives and datasets lacking construction history. Modeling B-rep latent spaces is challenging because they are variable-length; padding/unpadding introduces noise that degrades performance. The methods do not explicitly capture or enforce high-level geometric constraints (e.g., symmetry, orthogonality, parallelism), so edits can break design intent and reduce generalization to real-world CAD workflows. Models can also misinterpret textual prompts (notably counting-based instructions). Finally, current approaches are purely text- and geometry-driven and lack physical or functional validation — the model “reasons about geometry only, without validation from a physical simulation,” which limits confidence for functional/manufacturability requirements. Together these issues point to challenges in manufacturability, editability, parametric control, and downstream functional validation of generated CAD models.


---

## pqac-00000019

From text 'noever2512prompttopartsgenerativeai pages 16-18'.

David A. Noever. Prompt-to-parts: generative ai for physical assembly and scalable instructions. ArXiv, Dec 2512. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2512.15743, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2512.15743. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt identifies several limitations and challenges for AI/LLM-driven CAD and generative-3D systems relevant to manufacturability, editability, and parametric control. Many systems optimize visual fidelity over physical constructability, producing models that look plausible but may not be buildable. Earlier algorithmic approaches required a full target geometry as input, preventing open-ended language-driven generation. Mixed manual workflows (e.g., faBrickation) remained non-generative, indicating limited automation. Hierarchical and structure-aware methods improve coherence but often target continuous 3D representations rather than discrete part assemblies, so translating outputs into manufacturable, discrete component inventories is unresolved. More broadly, the field faces brittle spatial reasoning in LLMs, a disconnect between instruction generation and execution verification (so generated CAD may lack actionable, verifiable build instructions), and a shortage of systematic benchmarks for constrained physical generation. The text notes that programmatic intermediate representations (like LDraw) are more reliable than end-to-end approaches, but also highlights that CAD systems operate in continuous parameter spaces—implying difficulties mapping to discrete parts, enforceable tolerances, and parametric editability for manufacturing constraints.


---

## pqac-00000020

From text 'yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 1-2'.

Nomi Yu, Md Ferdous Alam, A. John Hart, and Faez Ahmed. Gencad-3d: cad program generation using multimodal latent space alignment and synthetic dataset balancing. Journal of Mechanical Design, pages 1-17, Jul 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1115/1.4069276, doi:10.1115/1.4069276. This article has 6 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt highlights several limitations and challenges relevant to AI/LLM-driven CAD generation. Many generative models output non-parametric representations ("non-parametric data such as point clouds and meshes") or other free-form shapes that lack CAD’s parametric structure and editability, so such outputs often "require manual adjustments to meet exact design intent," which is especially problematic where strict tolerances and precise fits are needed. Creating and editing CAD programs remains a specialist skill ("manually creating CAD programs is itself a complex skill that typically takes years to master"), and existing reverse-engineering tools still demand substantial expert input, "making the process expensive and time-consuming." A core data limitation is dataset imbalance: "High-complexity CAD programs... are underrepresented, causing models trained on this data to excel on simple shapes but struggle with more intricate ones." Standard evaluation metrics "are dominated by results on simple examples," which can mask failures on complex, high-complexity geometries. Together these factors lead to generated models that can be non-editable, insufficiently parametric for engineering needs, error-prone for complex designs, and often require human post-processing to be manufacturable or fit for downstream use.

## pqac-00000021

From text 'schupbach2025fromtextto pages 1-2'.

Aurel Schüpbach, Raul San Miguel, Julian Ferchow, and Mirko Meboldt. From text to design: a framework to leverage llm agents for automated cad generation. Proceedings of the Design Society, 5:1893-1902, Aug 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1017/pds.2025.10203, doi:10.1017/pds.2025.10203. This article has 4 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The paper highlights several limitations of AI/LLM-driven CAD generation relevant to manufacturability, editability, and parametric control. Diffusion- and LLM-based approaches "have limited ability to produce exact CAD models from engineering constraints" and "still struggle to adhere to precise requirements such as dimensional constraints or accurate positions of features," undermining reliable parametric controllability and tight tolerances needed for manufacturability. Generated geometry often "lack[s] typical features like design history or CAD interoperability," which reduces editability, downstream modification, and integration with CAD toolchains. Broader challenges include "model hallucination" and the "limited availability of specialized engineering training data," causing incorrect or fabricated features that can break manufacturability and require human correction. The reliance on text-only I/O and weaknesses in spatial reasoning ("current LLMs primarily rely on text input and output"; "limitations in spatial reasoning") further limit the system's ability to capture 3D constraints and produce robust, editable parametric models. The authors also note "prompt dependency" and "workflow adaptability" as constraints, and recommend future work on improving "design-for-manufacturing capabilities, visual tools, and evaluation benchmarking."


---

## pqac-00000022

From text 'liao2025automatedcadmodeling pages 1-2'.

Jianxing Liao, Junyan Xu, Yatao Sun, Maowen Tang, Sicheng He, Jingxian Liao, Shui Yu, Yun Li, and Hongguan Xiao. Automated cad modeling sequence generation from text descriptions via transformer-based large language models. ArXiv, May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.19490, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.19490. This article has 8 citations.

The excerpt identifies several limitations and challenges of AI/LLM-driven CAD generation relevant to manufacturability, editability, and parametric control. Generated design data often lacks sufficient accuracy, making conversion into high-quality, editable CAD models difficult—especially where industrial precision is required. LLMs are computationally expensive and inefficient, and designers find it hard to guide them with simple text or visual prompts toward reasonable, manufacturable designs. Prior methods focus on data synthesis rather than producing robust modeling histories or parametric control; available datasets often lack modeling-process information and natural-language annotations needed for editable, parametric models. Specific model behaviors can harm editability and parametric controllability: for example, DeepCAD generates sketches before extrusion parameters, producing disconnected sequences and only supporting basic operations. The paper suggests hybrid approaches—using finely tuned small generative models to provide accurate task-level guidance combined with large models—to mitigate reasoning uncertainty, but overall challenges remain in ensuring manufacturability, maintaining parametric editability, and producing precise, connected modeling sequences that are practical for industrial workflows.


---

## pqac-00000023

From text 'heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 21-22'.

Negar Heidari and Alexandros Iosifidis. Geometric deep learning for computer-aided design: a survey. IEEE Access, 13:119305-119334, Feb 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2402.17695, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2402.17695. This article has 47 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt highlights multiple practical limitations and challenges for AI-driven CAD generation that affect manufacturability, editability and parametric control. Methods that infer parametric primitives (e.g., extrusion parameters) or reconstruct CAD by searching constrained-sketch repositories can yield parametric CAD outputs, but their robustness is limited: point-cloud-based approaches struggle with noisy or distorted inputs, fail to recover sharp edges and only provide approximate 3D representations. While some approaches produce structured B-Rep outputs, broader limitations come from data: there is a shortage of large, diverse, well-annotated B-Rep datasets (many datasets exist only as meshes), annotation of mechanical models requires domain expertise, and surface/face classes are inconsistent across datasets, hindering transfer learning. Assembly-level information (joints, hierarchical subassemblies) is often not annotated in B-Rep data, making edit operations like replacing parts while preserving connections and functional/material considerations difficult. Together, these factors reduce confidence in manufacturability checks, limit fine-grained parametric controllability, and make subsequent editable, semantically-correct CAD model refinement and assembly-aware edits challenging.


---

## pqac-00000024

From text 'yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 14-15'.

Nomi Yu, Md Ferdous Alam, A. John Hart, and Faez Ahmed. Gencad-3d: cad program generation using multimodal latent space alignment and synthetic dataset balancing. Journal of Mechanical Design, pages 1-17, Jul 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1115/1.4069276, doi:10.1115/1.4069276. This article has 6 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The paper notes several limitations relevant to AI/LLM-driven CAD generation. First, synthetic augmentation (SynthBal) improves performance on existing benchmarks but "synthetic data may not fully capture the complexities inherent in industrial designs, potentially limiting generalization." Second, the training/evaluation data are narrow: "the current DeepCAD dataset only contains Sketch-and-Extrude CAD programs, a small subset of engineering geometries," which restricts exposure to commands, feature types, and real-world part variability. Third, practical parametric and editable CAD requires handling of more diverse CAD vocabularies and specialized program constructs: "it is important to collect and experiment on datasets with larger CAD vocabularies, and perhaps develop architectures better suited to handling special commands such as those that require references to compiled B-Rep geometries." Overall, the authors emphasize that despite performance gains, "certain limitations remain," pointing to dataset realism, limited CAD program types, and the need for architectures that support B-Rep references and richer CAD operations for better generalization, parametric controllability, and practical editability.

What are the benefits and limitations identified?", "text_name_focus_list": ["peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 1-3", "peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 8-9", "peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 20-21", "peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 28-29"]}

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000025

From text 'yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 10-11'.

Nomi Yu, Md Ferdous Alam, A. John Hart, and Faez Ahmed. Gencad-3d: cad program generation using multimodal latent space alignment and synthetic dataset balancing. Journal of Mechanical Design, pages 1-17, Jul 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1115/1.4069276, doi:10.1115/1.4069276. This article has 6 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

Table 4 ("Quality and Accuracy of Multimodal Generative Models") includes a row for SkexGen [25] labeled as "Uncond." Many columns for SkexGen are marked "-" ("-" indicates that the metric is not relevant), meaning most of the table's metrics were not reported for SkexGen. The table header shows the last three reported columns as "cmd (↑) 𝜇SLparam (↑) 𝑀SLCD" and the SkexGen row lists values only for these three: 83.6, 1.48, and 0.81 respectively. In other words, within this table SkexGen's entry provides only the three metrics shown in the header (cmd = 83.6, 𝜇SLparam = 1.48, 𝑀SLCD = 0.81) while leaving the other listed metrics unreported for that model.


---

## pqac-00000026

From text 'xu2023hierarchicalneuralcoding pages 6-7'.

Xiang Xu, Pradeep Kumar Jayaraman, Joseph G. Lambourne, Karl D. D. Willis, and Yasutaka Furukawa. Hierarchical neural coding for controllable cad model generation. ArXiv, Jun 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2307.00149, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2307.00149. This article has 117 citations.

The excerpt reports benchmark results comparing SkexGen on unconditional sketch-and-extrude CAD generation. Table 1 gives SkexGen: Coverage (COV) = 84.74%, Minimum Matching Distance (MMD) = 1.02, Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD) = 0.90, Novel = 99.1%, Unique = 99.8%, and human-perceived Realism = 46.9. The paper defines the evaluation metrics: point-cloud metrics (COV, MMD, JSD) assessing diversity/quality of generated shapes, token metrics for sequences (Novel and Unique), and a human evaluation for realism. The excerpt does not provide separate, explicit metric values labeled specifically for a “disentangled latent space” nor distinct quantitative breakdowns separately for sketch vs. extrusion quality—only a pointer that sketch generation results are available in Appendix C. Thus, from this excerpt the reported metrics for SkexGen are the point-cloud (COV/MMD/JSD), token (Novel/Unique) scores, and human realism score as listed.


---

## pqac-00000027

From text 'xu2023hierarchicalneuralcoding pages 13-20'.

Xiang Xu, Pradeep Kumar Jayaraman, Joseph G. Lambourne, Karl D. D. Willis, and Yasutaka Furukawa. Hierarchical neural coding for controllable cad model generation. ArXiv, Jun 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2307.00149, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2307.00149. This article has 117 citations.

The excerpt reports unconditional sketch-generation benchmarks where SkexGen achieves FID=18.56, Unique=96.02%, and Novel=83.54% (Table 2). The paper computes Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) using features from a ResNet-18 pre-trained on human sketch classification, following SkexGen’s evaluation protocol. In addition to automated sketch metrics (FID, uniqueness, novelty), the paper describes a human two-alternative forced-choice evaluation of realism (AMT), run on 950 image pairs per generative method with 7 workers per pair (19,950 annotations total). For disentangled latent/code evaluation, the excerpt presents qualitative code-to-data mappings showing that "data assigned to the same code also have similar design patterns," indicating qualitative clustering of profile/loop geometry, but no numerical disentanglement metric is reported in these pages. The excerpt does not provide explicit benchmark numbers for SkexGen on full autoregressive CAD construction-sequence generation or on extrusion-generation quality; only sketch-generation metrics and qualitative code-mapping observations are present.

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000028

From text 'peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 26-28'.

Owen Peckham, Jonathan Raines, Erik Bulsink, Mark Goudswaard, James Gopsill, David Barton, Aydin Nassehi, and Ben Hicks. Artificial intelligence in generative design: a structured review of trends and opportunities in techniques and applications. Designs, 9:79, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/designs9040079, doi:10.3390/designs9040079. This article has 26 citations.

The review of 88 articles reports two main roles for AI in generative design (GD): surrogate models to speed up optimisation workflows, and direct design generation. There has been rapid recent growth (72.7% of papers since 2021) and a shift from classic MLPs (still most common at 34%) toward modern generative architectures such as GANs (23.4%) and CNNs (19.1%) over the last five years — indicating increasing use of GANs for direct generation tasks. Surrogate models offer acceleration benefits but face substantial limitations: high data generation and training costs (reported in 56.8% of articles), and model accuracy concerns (30%), requiring trade-offs between training expense and performance. Identified opportunities include training foundation surrogate models that generalise across scenarios to reduce cost and improve accuracy, developing GenAI systems that synthesize using structured engineering data, exploring unsupervised methods to capture physical meaning in designs, and improving manufacturability of GD outcomes. The excerpt does not discuss diffusion models or genetic algorithms, and notes the review only covers academic literature (limiting insight into proprietary industrial tools such as Autodesk’s Dreamcatcher).


---

## pqac-00000029

From text 'peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 1-3'.

Owen Peckham, Jonathan Raines, Erik Bulsink, Mark Goudswaard, James Gopsill, David Barton, Aydin Nassehi, and Ben Hicks. Artificial intelligence in generative design: a structured review of trends and opportunities in techniques and applications. Designs, 9:79, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/designs9040079, doi:10.3390/designs9040079. This article has 26 citations.

The review reports that Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) are being used for direct design generation in engineering CAD/GD workflows, while genetic algorithms remain widely used and surrogate models (e.g., Convolutional Neural Networks and Multilayer Perceptrons) are employed to manage the high computational cost of simulation-heavy design tasks. Structural and aerodynamic problems are the dominant application areas. Identified benefits include improved computational efficiency, greater design diversity, and substantial performance gains in light-weighting studies (>40–60% mass savings versus expert designs). Limitations and barriers highlighted are data-generation costs, model accuracy, and interpretability; manufacturability constraints are noted as an under-addressed area. The review points to opportunities to develop generalisable foundation surrogate models and to integrate emerging generative methods such as diffusion models and large language models into generative design pipelines.


---

## pqac-00000030

From text 'peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 20-21'.

Owen Peckham, Jonathan Raines, Erik Bulsink, Mark Goudswaard, James Gopsill, David Barton, Aydin Nassehi, and Ben Hicks. Artificial intelligence in generative design: a structured review of trends and opportunities in techniques and applications. Designs, 9:79, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/designs9040079, doi:10.3390/designs9040079. This article has 26 citations.

The excerpt reports several survey findings relevant to generative design techniques. Conditional GANs have been used to incorporate physical/design parameters (e.g., initial stress fields, angle of attack, Mach number) into generation, helping ensure more physically informed outputs. However, GANs are being superseded in image domains by diffusion models; recent work applies diffusion models to topology optimisation (TO) and even to non-pixel representations—Xu et al. used diffusion to predict hierarchical tree node properties to generate B-rep CAD models. Surrogate modelling is discussed as applicable to multi-physics structural design, with accuracy as the primary concern. Broad limitations across AI/ML methods include that models learn “what” rather than “why,” trained designs lack context and physical derivation, and models typically output a single solution whereas engineering often requires Pareto fronts for multi-objective trade-offs. LLMs are notably under-represented in the corpus, possibly due to sequence representation and numeric-precision challenges. Explainability appears rarely addressed despite its importance for generated-design rationale, particularly in safety-critical domains. The excerpt does not explicitly discuss genetic algorithms.


---

## pqac-00000031

From text 'peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 24-26'.

Owen Peckham, Jonathan Raines, Erik Bulsink, Mark Goudswaard, James Gopsill, David Barton, Aydin Nassehi, and Ben Hicks. Artificial intelligence in generative design: a structured review of trends and opportunities in techniques and applications. Designs, 9:79, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/designs9040079, doi:10.3390/designs9040079. This article has 26 citations.

The review notes a trend toward greater maturity and reliability of generative models and predicts diffusion models will likely be adopted in engineering generative design. However, a core limitation is representational mismatch: many modern tools (GANs, diffusion models, transformers) are built for images/voxels or sequences, formats that "are not commonly used to represent designs in industry," creating a need for methods to translate "neural/pixel-based representations and engineering-specific formats" or to develop diffusion models tailored to engineering data. Neural-guided search is highlighted as promising for addressing combinatorial complexity when adapting diffusion models and transformers to structures. Sequence-based models and LLMs can be applied via a "program-centric" route—having LLMs "generate code that describes a parametric design"—and fine-tuning (including RL) could help models learn the "why as well as the what," but integration with simulation or CAM tools is likely required. Genetic algorithms are referenced historically (e.g., GA used to search for proofs). Practical limitations include the "real-world feasibility" of GD outputs and the difficulty of applying manufacturing constraints; the paper suggests surrogate-style AI tools ("MLPs") could act as manufacturability checkers integrated into late-stage GD optimisation to improve producibility.


---

## pqac-00000032

From text 'peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 23-24'.

Owen Peckham, Jonathan Raines, Erik Bulsink, Mark Goudswaard, James Gopsill, David Barton, Aydin Nassehi, and Ben Hicks. Artificial intelligence in generative design: a structured review of trends and opportunities in techniques and applications. Designs, 9:79, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/designs9040079, doi:10.3390/designs9040079. This article has 26 citations.

The reviewed excerpt reports that GANs are the most common architecture for direct design generation (9/14 studies), motivated by a desire for greater design variety and originally applied to aesthetics. GAN outputs are generally used as starting points for downstream processing rather than final geometries. More recently, GANs have been applied to physics-informed designs by representing properties (pressure, stress, strain) as images, and have expanded into constrained domains (structural and aerodynamic) since 2021. Image-based GAN outputs suffer from pixelation, aliasing and fuzzy edges; remedies include post-processing or switching to alternate geometric representations (e.g., vectors of 2D points, tensor representations) to produce smooth, parametric-compatible geometry. Constraint handling via a second discriminator can reward physical plausibility but may introduce competing loss signals that hurt other design qualities. For surrogate models, CNNs dominate across many engineering fields; MLPs and VAEs are used where appropriate (e.g., MLPs for approximating costly CFD). PINNs can embed PDEs to avoid labelled data but can be far more computationally expensive (reported 34×). The review emphasizes the critical role of training-data quality and coverage, recommending DOE methods such as Latin hypercube sampling and the D-optimal method. The excerpt does not discuss diffusion models or genetic algorithms.


---

## pqac-00000033

From text 'peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 21-23'.

Owen Peckham, Jonathan Raines, Erik Bulsink, Mark Goudswaard, James Gopsill, David Barton, Aydin Nassehi, and Ben Hicks. Artificial intelligence in generative design: a structured review of trends and opportunities in techniques and applications. Designs, 9:79, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/designs9040079, doi:10.3390/designs9040079. This article has 26 citations.

The surveyed corpus identifies two common technique–function pairings: genetic algorithms (GAs) paired with surrogate models, and GANs used for design generation. Surrogate models are valued because they can be orders of magnitude faster than traditional solvers, reducing the computational burden of GA fitness evaluations. However, researchers report notable limitations: reduced surrogate accuracy (reported in 9 of 28 studies), the cost of generating training data (often needing runs of traditional solvers), and training costs (20 of 28 studies mentioned this), making a complex trade-off over whether to invest in surrogate training. To address generalisability and fidelity, some studies use ML to accelerate finite element methods (performing FEM at lower resolution and upscaling) or embed physics via PINNs (incorporating PDEs into the loss) to improve results. GANs are cited as a primary tool for generating design variants, though the excerpt does not detail specific GAN benefits or failure modes beyond their role in generation. Explainability and confidence are under-addressed: only one paper covered explainable AI, and current techniques lack measures of prediction confidence. The excerpt does not mention diffusion models.


---

## pqac-00000034

From text 'peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 32-33'.

Owen Peckham, Jonathan Raines, Erik Bulsink, Mark Goudswaard, James Gopsill, David Barton, Aydin Nassehi, and Ben Hicks. Artificial intelligence in generative design: a structured review of trends and opportunities in techniques and applications. Designs, 9:79, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/designs9040079, doi:10.3390/designs9040079. This article has 26 citations.

The provided pages are the reference list from a 2025 survey on AI in generative design. They cite foundational and recent works across GANs, diffusion models, genetic algorithms, and surrogate models applied to CAD and engineering design. Examples include Wasserstein GAN and application papers such as ShipHullGAN (parametric ship-hull modelling) and several diffusion-model works (e.g., Latent Diffusion Models for Structural Component Design; BrepGen for B-rep geometry). Classic evolutionary approaches are represented by a genetic-algorithms overview, and acceleration/approximation approaches appear via multi-stage DNN-accelerated topology optimization and deep-learning-based surrogate models for fast multi-material topology optimization. There are also citations for CAD-focused generative approaches (CAD-LLM) and other generative methods for analog IC topology and airfoil inverse design. The excerpt does not contain the survey’s narrative findings, explicit benefits, or limitations; however, the cited titles imply research trends: GANs and diffusion models for generative geometry, genetic algorithms for global/combinatorial search, and surrogate/DNN models to speed optimization. Direct statements about advantages (e.g., quality, fidelity, speed) or limitations (e.g., mode collapse, computation cost, generalization) are not present on these pages.


---

## pqac-00000035

From text 'souvanhnakhoomman2025acomprehensivereview pages 2-3'.

Sane Souvanhnakhoomman and Alvin Chua. A comprehensive review of generative design applications in unmanned aerial vehicles. ASEAN Engineering Journal, 15:163-175, Feb 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.11113/aej.v15.21286, doi:10.11113/aej.v15.21286. This article has 4 citations.

The excerpt reports survey findings that highlight the integration of AI into generative design for CAD/engineering. Genetic algorithms (GAs) have been applied in industrial case studies to evolve parametric CAD models, often combined with rigid-body simulation and finite element analysis to meet engineering requirements. Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) have been paired with topology optimization to generate multiple design variants (example: wheel rims) that satisfy both engineering and aesthetic criteria; GANs were also used as a filter under the assumption that existing designs are more aesthetically acceptable than arbitrary topology-optimization outputs. The field has moved from seeking a single “perfect” design toward producing a set of diverse alternatives. Benefits noted include practical industrial applicability, the ability to generate multiple viable solutions, and tighter integration of AI with topology optimization and CAD tools. Limitations and caveats in the excerpt include reliance on aesthetic assumptions for filtering, the lack of a universally agreed definition of generative design, and earlier limited practical implementations. The excerpt does not discuss diffusion models or surrogate models, so no findings about those methods are reported here.

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-01)

| Tool Name | Vendor | Core Method (TO/GD/both) | Key Features | Manufacturing Constraints Support | Output Format | Notable Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Autodesk Fusion Generative Design | Autodesk | Both | Generates 3D part forms from geometric + force parameters; explores many alternatives; supports simulation-based evaluation and filtering of outcomes; widely used for lightweighting and AM-oriented redesign (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000003) | Strong relative to mainstream CAD GD tools; users can apply manufacturing constraints to bias solutions toward manufacturable outputs, especially AM processes (pqac-00000003) | Native Fusion geometry; exportable CAD/B-rep workflows | Outputs often skew toward AM-style organic forms; many alternatives still require manual interpretation and downstream styling; engineer-centric workflow (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003) |
| nTopology / nTop | nTop | Both | Field-driven implicit modeling; strong lattice generation/manipulation; integrates FEA-informed lattice workflows; well suited to AM and performance-driven geometry (pqac-00000002) | Strong for AM/DFAM, especially lattice and powder-bed workflows (pqac-00000002) | Implicit/mesh/B-rep export depending workflow | Best fit is advanced AM rather than conventional subtractive manufacturing; requires expertise to convert performance forms into broader production-ready product geometry (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002) |
| Siemens NX Topology Optimizer (+ Simcenter HEEDS) | Siemens | Both | NX geometry + Simcenter/HEEDS can automatically generate and compare hundreds to thousands of optimized options; integrates CAD and CAE; targeted at high-end engineering workflows and AM-ready exploration (pqac-00000000) | Good support through NX/Simcenter manufacturing-aware setup and AM-targeted workflows (pqac-00000000) | NX CAD geometry / CAE-linked optimized models | Higher workflow complexity and enterprise cost; still subject to classic TO sensitivity to loads/boundary conditions and post-processing burden for manufacturable CAD (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001) |
| PTC Creo Generative Design | PTC | Both | Integrated parametric CAD + simulation-driven concept generation; intended to produce editable Creo-native concepts and support engineering redesign | Moderate-to-strong in principle via process constraints, preserved geometry, loads, materials, and manufacturing method setup | Creo-native parametric geometry / exportable CAD | Fewer independently reported benchmark-style public results than Autodesk/Altair; manufacturability and editability depend heavily on problem setup and post-processing; organic outputs still need engineering cleanup (pqac-00000001) |
| ANSYS Discovery | ANSYS | Both | Real-time simulation-guided design exploration; topology optimization linked to fast physics feedback; strong CAE-centric concept screening | Good for simulation-constrained design; manufacturability can be introduced through setup constraints, but typically less design-history-centric than full parametric CAD | Simulation-derived geometry / CAD handoff | More analysis-centric than end-to-end editable CAD authoring; translating optimized shapes into robust production CAD can require extra remodeling (pqac-00000001) |
| Altair Inspire (OptiStruct) | Altair | Both | Early and influential commercial TO platform; removes material from a 3D CAD model to meet goals such as maximum stiffness with minimum mass; strong concept-lightweighting workflow (pqac-00000003) | Moderate; supports engineering constraints and process-aware setup, but manufacturable finalization often needs redesign/refinement (pqac-00000003) | Optimized concept geometry / export to downstream CAD | TO outputs can be hard to convert directly into final parametric CAD; workflows are predominantly engineer-focused; raw results may require significant remodeling and styling (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000003) |
| Rhino + Grasshopper | Robert McNeel + Associates | GD-oriented (with TO via plugins) | Flexible visual parametric/algorithmic modeling; strong for custom rule-based exploration, geometry scripting, and integration with plugins such as Kangaroo, Karamba3D, Millipede, Wallacei, and AM workflows | Variable; can encode manufacturing rules and custom constraints, but depends on user-authored definitions/plugins rather than turnkey solver workflows | NURBS/mesh/parametric Grasshopper definitions; broad export support | Powerful but less turnkey for certified engineering optimization; requires substantial user expertise, plugin selection, and manual validation; manufacturability and performance assurance are not automatic (pqac-00000001) |


*Table: This table compares major commercial topology-optimization and generative-design platforms on method, workflow strengths, manufacturing support, outputs, and practical limitations. It is useful for distinguishing simulation-centric optimization tools from more editable parametric or algorithmic design environments.*

function.arguments: {"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a comparison table of parametric/code-based CAD frameworks. Columns: Framework, Language, License/Type, Kernel, GUI Required?, Python Ecosystem Integration, CI/CD Suitability, Key Strengths, Key Limitations. Rows for: CadQuery, OpenSCAD, build123d, Onshape FeatureScript, JSCAD. Use evidence from pqac-00000008 showing CadQuery uses Python with no separate software needed, excellent Jupyter/scripting integration; OpenSCAD uses its own DSL, requires separate software, no Python integration. Supplement with domain knowledge for build123d (Python, OCCT kernel, successor to CadQuery), FeatureScript (Onshape's proprietary language, cloud-only), and JSCAD (JavaScript-based CSG)."}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-02)

| Framework | Language | License/Type | Kernel | GUI Required? | Python Ecosystem Integration | CI/CD Suitability | Key Strengths | Key Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CadQuery | Python | Open-source library | OpenCascade (via OCCT/OCP) | No | Excellent; strong Jupyter and scripting workflow, with no separate CAD software required (pqac-00000008) | High | Parametric, programmable solid modeling; readable Python API; good for automation, testing, batch generation, and export pipelines; strong fit for reproducible engineering code (pqac-00000008) | Less interactive than mainstream GUI CAD; advanced surfacing/assemblies can require more coding expertise; manufacturability rules are not automatic |
| OpenSCAD | OpenSCAD DSL | Open-source application/language | CGAL-based CSG pipeline | Typically yes for normal use; headless CLI possible | None/very limited; not Python-native and requires separate software (pqac-00000008) | Medium | Extremely deterministic and reproducible; simple declarative CSG; excellent for scripted fixtures, enclosures, and parameterized hobbyist/mechanical parts | Weaker ecosystem interoperability; limited direct Python integration; less suited to complex fillets/freeform industrial geometry; editability is code-centric rather than feature-tree-centric (pqac-00000008) |
| build123d | Python | Open-source library | OpenCascade (OCCT) | No | Excellent | High | Modern Pythonic parametric CAD; close to CadQuery but with explicit algebra/mode-building APIs; strong type hints and testability; good for CI/CD and reusable CAD components | Smaller ecosystem/community than older tools; still code-first, so discoverability and interactive editing are weaker than GUI CAD |
| Onshape FeatureScript | FeatureScript | Proprietary cloud CAD language/platform | Onshape Parasolid-based cloud kernel | Yes, via Onshape cloud environment | Limited/indirect; accessible through APIs rather than native Python modeling | Medium-High | Native extension mechanism inside a production CAD system; robust parametric features; strong collaboration, versioning, and enterprise governance; good for custom feature authoring in real engineering workflows | Cloud-only and vendor-tied; proprietary language/platform; less suitable for offline/local builds; CI/CD depends on SaaS/API workflows rather than pure local execution |
| JSCAD | JavaScript | Open-source library/toolchain | CSG/B-rep-style JS geometry engine | No | Limited direct Python integration; strong JavaScript/Node ecosystem instead | High for JS-centric teams | Browser- and Node-friendly; easy web deployment; good for lightweight parametric CSG and custom configurators; accessible for full-stack developers | Less mature for heavy mechanical CAD than OCCT-based tools; complex fillets/shells/industrial geometry can be harder; manufacturability and engineering semantics are mostly user-enforced |


*Table: This table compares major parametric and code-driven CAD frameworks on language, kernel, scripting ergonomics, and CI/CD suitability. It is useful for distinguishing pure code-first CAD stacks from cloud-native feature authoring and browser-oriented geometry tools.*

Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: [artifact-00, artifact-01, artifact-02]

Context IDs that may be cited: [pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033, pqac-00000034, pqac-00000035]

info.name: patent_search
info.description:
Search for patents to increase the patent count.

Repeat previous calls with the same query to continue a search.
Only repeat a maximum of three times.
This tool can be called concurrently.
This tool introduces novel patents, so invoke it when just beginning
or when unsatisfied with the current evidence.

query.description:
A search query for patents (using Google Patents). Never include temporal
filters here (e.g. specific years or dates); instead use the `after` and `before`
arguments. Never include author filters here; instead use the `inventor` argument.
Supports the following syntax, but they can be brittle and are not preferred; try
simple queries where possible:
- Boolean operators: AND (default), OR, and - (NOT)
- Exact phrases: "quoted text"
- Proximity: NEAR/x, ADJ/x (ordered), WITH (20 words), SAME (200 words)
- Field search: TI=(title), AB=(abstract), CL=(claims)
- CPC codes: cpc:A01B or CPC=B60R22/low (includes children)
- Wildcards: ? (0-1 char), * (0+ chars), # (exactly 1 char)
- Chemistry: exact molecule names, SSS=molecule (substructure),
  ~molecule (similar), SMILES strings, SMARTS=pattern, InChI keys,
  CL=~molecule (chemistry in claims)
query.title: Query

after.description:
Minimum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20150101' for patents after Jan 1, 2015.
after.title: After

before.description:
Maximum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20200101' for patents before Jan 1, 2020.
before.title: Before

date_type.description:
Type of date that after and before refer to. Must be either 'publication'
or 'filing'. Only necessary to set if after and/or before are set.
date_type.title: Date Type

inventor.description: Filter by inventor name(s). Comma-separated for multiple.
inventor.title: Inventor

info.name: create_plan
info.description:
Create a plan for to construct a thorough and accurate answer.

Decompose the input task into a set of sub-objectives that need to
be met in order to form the answer. Subsequently, when working on solving
the query, you can focus on these objectives one by one. Note that the
`update_plan` tool allows you to change the objectives if needed.

In addition to the objectives you deem necessary, add one final objective
to call the `answer` tool with your final answer, so that you do not
forget to submit your answer.

plan_json.description:
A JSON string representing the plan. It should be a dictionary
with top-level key "objectives", whose value is a list of objectives.
Each objective should be a dictionary with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id for the plan objective
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
plan_json.title: Plan Json

info.name: update_plan
info.description:
Update the plan for a specific or new objective using a JSON string to create or
replace the existing objective.

The updated_json should be a JSON string representing a single plan objective object.

You may use this tool to update an objective, create a new one, or change
strategy if the current plan is not working. Only change strategy significantly
as a last resort, after trying very hard to make the current plan work.

Only mark an objective as completed if you are absolutely sure that it has been fully
met. If it is only partially met, you may add additional objectives to the plan for
what is missing.

plan_objective_id.description: The id of the objective to update.
plan_objective_id.title: Plan Objective Id

updated_plan_objective_json.description:
A JSON string representing the updated objective.
It should be a dictionary with the with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id matching plan_objective_id, if not found, a new objective is created.
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
updated_plan_objective_json.title: Updated Plan Objective Json

info.name: read_text
info.description:
Given a list of text names, read their contents.

This tool is valuable to read the full contents of specific parts of documents,
complementing the `gather_evidence` tool's indirect approach.
This can be an especially useful tool if `gather_evidence` does not find evidence,
but you think it is very likely that specific documents are still relevant to the question.

text_name_list.description:
A list of up to 10 text names to read.
These text names can be any valid text names retrieved by other tools.
text_name_list.title: Text Name List

info.name: clinical_trials_search
info.description:
Search for clinical trials, with support for repeated calls and concurrent execution.

Will add new clinical trials to the state, and return metadata about the number of trials found.

query.description:
The search query string. Supports complex boolean expressions, field-specific
searches, and query modifiers through operators. All configuration is done through
operators in the query string.
Query Syntax:
    Basic Search:
        Simple text automatically uses default EXPANSION[Relaxation] and COVERAGE[Contains]
        >>> "heart attack"

    Modified Search:
        Use operators to modify search behavior:
        >>> 'EXPANSION[None]COVERAGE[FullMatch]"exact phrase"'
        >>> 'EXPANSION[Concept]heart attack'

    Field Search:
        Specify fields using AREA operator:
        >>> 'AREA[InterventionName]aspirin'
        >>> 'AREA[Phase]PHASE3'

    Location Search:
        Use SEARCH operator for compound location queries:
        >>> 'cancer AND SEARCH[Location](AREA[LocationCity]Boston AND AREA[LocationState]Massachusetts)'

    Complex Boolean:
        Combine terms with AND, OR, NOT and parentheses:
        >>> '(cancer OR tumor) AND NOT (EXPANSION[None]pediatric OR AREA[StdAge]CHILD)'

    Date Ranges:
        Use RANGE to specify date ranges with formats like "yyyy-MM" or "yyyy-MM-dd".
        Note that MIN and MAX can be used for open-ended ranges:
        >>> AREA[ResultsFirstPostDate]RANGE[2015-01-01, MAX]

Operators:
    EXPANSION[type]: Controls term expansion
        - None: Exact match only, case and accent sensitive
        - Term: Includes lexical variants (plurals, spellings)
        - Concept: Includes UMLS synonyms
        - Relaxation: Relaxes adjacency requirements (default)
        - Lossy: Allows missing partial terms

    COVERAGE[type]: Controls text matching
        - FullMatch: Must match entire field
        - StartsWith: Must match beginning of field
        - EndsWith: Must match end of field
        - Contains: Must match part of field (default)

    AREA[field]: Specifies field to search
        - See Field Reference for available fields

    SEARCH[type]: Groups field searches
        - Location: Groups location-related fields
        - Study: Groups study-related fields

Usage Notes:
    - All search expressions are implicitly OR expressions
    - Operator precedence (highest to lowest): terms/source operators, NOT/context operators, AND, OR
    - Use quotes for exact phrase matching: "heart attack"
    - Use parentheses for grouping: (heart OR cardiac) AND attack
    - Use backslash to escape operators: \AND
    - Default expansion is EXPANSION[Relaxation]
    - Default coverage is COVERAGE[Contains]

Field Reference:
    High Priority Fields (weight >= 0.8):
        - NCTId (1.0): Trial identifier
        - Acronym (1.0): Study acronym
        - BriefTitle (0.89): Short title
        - OfficialTitle (0.85): Full official title
        - Condition (0.81): Medical condition
        - InterventionName (0.8): Primary intervention name
        - OverallStatus: Trial status

    Medium Priority Fields (0.5-0.79):
        - InterventionOtherName (0.75): Alternative intervention names
        - Phase (0.65): Trial phase
        - StdAge (0.65): Standard age groups
        - Keyword (0.6): Study keywords
        - BriefSummary (0.6): Short description
        - SecondaryOutcomeMeasure (0.5): Secondary outcomes

    Low Priority Fields (< 0.5):
        - DesignPrimaryPurpose (0.3): Primary purpose of study
        - StudyType (0.3)
        - Various descriptive, location, and administrative fields

Supported Enums:
    Phase:
        - EARLY_PHASE1: Early Phase 1
        - PHASE1: Phase 1
        - PHASE2: Phase 2
        - PHASE3: Phase 3
        - PHASE4: Phase 4
        - NA: Not Applicable

    StandardAge:
        - CHILD: Child
        - ADULT: Adult
        - OLDER_ADULT: Older Adult

    Status:
        - RECRUITING: Currently recruiting participants
        - ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING: Active but not recruiting
        - COMPLETED: Study completed
        - ENROLLING_BY_INVITATION: Enrolling by invitation only
        - NOT_YET_RECRUITING: Not yet recruiting
        - SUSPENDED: Study suspended
        - TERMINATED: Study terminated
        - WITHDRAWN: Study withdrawn
        - AVAILABLE: Available
        - NO_LONGER_AVAILABLE: No longer available
        - TEMPORARILY_NOT_AVAILABLE: Temporarily not available
        - APPROVED_FOR_MARKETING: Approved for marketing
        - WITHHELD: Withheld
        - UNKNOWN: Unknown status

    StudyType:
        - INTERVENTIONAL: Interventional studies
        - OBSERVATIONAL: Observational studies
        - EXPANDED_ACCESS: Expanded access studies

    PrimaryPurpose:
        - TREATMENT: Treatment
        - PREVENTION: Prevention
        - DIAGNOSTIC: Diagnostic
        - ECT: Educational/Counseling/Training
        - SUPPORTIVE_CARE: Supportive Care
        - SCREENING: Screening
        - HEALTH_SERVICES_RESEARCH: Health Services Research
        - BASIC_SCIENCE: Basic Science
        - DEVICE_FEASIBILITY: Device Feasibility
        - OTHER: Other

    InterventionType:
        - BEHAVIORAL: Behavioral interventions
        - BIOLOGICAL: Biological interventions
        - COMBINATION_PRODUCT: Combination product interventions
        - DEVICE: Device interventions
        - DIAGNOSTIC_TEST: Diagnostic test interventions
        - DIETARY_SUPPLEMENT: Dietary supplement interventions
        - DRUG: Drug interventions
        - GENETIC: Genetic interventions
        - PROCEDURE: Procedure interventions
        - RADIATION: Radiation interventions
        - OTHER: Other interventions

    DesignAllocation:
        - RANDOMIZED: Randomized allocation
        - NON_RANDOMIZED: Non-randomized allocation
        - NA: Not applicable

    InterventionalAssignment:
        - SINGLE_GROUP: Single group assignment
        - PARALLEL: Parallel assignment
        - CROSSOVER: Crossover assignment
        - FACTORIAL: Factorial assignment
        - SEQUENTIAL: Sequential assignment

    ObservationalModel:
        - COHORT: Cohort
        - CASE_CONTROL: Case-Control
        - CASE_ONLY: Case-Only
        - CASE_CROSSOVER: Case-Crossover
        - ECOLOGIC_OR_COMMUNITY: Ecologic or Community
        - FAMILY_BASED: Family-Based
        - DEFINED_POPULATION: Defined Population
        - NATURAL_HISTORY: Natural History
        - OTHER: Other

    DesignMasking:
        - NONE: None (Open Label)
        - SINGLE: Single
        - DOUBLE: Double
        - TRIPLE: Triple
        - QUADRUPLE: Quadruple

    WhoMasked:
        - PARTICIPANT: Participant
        - CARE_PROVIDER: Care Provider
        - INVESTIGATOR: Investigator
        - OUTCOMES_ASSESSOR: Outcomes Assessor
query.title: Query

info.name: view_images
info.description:
Retrieve images from a document.

Use this tool when you need to see figures, tables, or other visual content.
The tool works by asking a weaker LLM agent to scan through the document, looking for
visual content matching the query you provide. When the agent finds matches, it will
crop the page to the relevant region. It can be tasked to return multiple images,
if desired. Note that the weaker LLM may be worse at *understanding* images than
you, so double-check its conclusions about the returned images. It is also stateless,
i.e. it will not remember what you have asked it to find in the past.

Use this tool carefully. It is difficult to retrieve images, and the images
themselves will fill up your context window quickly. Here are some guidelines:
1. ALWAYS use this tool if the question explicitly mentions 'Table X' or 'Figure Y'.
   These questions require visual content and cannot be answered with text alone.
2. Use this tool as a fallback when gather_evidence fails repeatedly (2+ times
   returning 'No relevant evidence'). This suggests the answer may be in visual content.
3. When gather_evidence returns text mentioning 'see Figure X' or 'shown in Table Y',
   you should call this tool to examine that specific visual reference.
4. Only call this tool after directly inspecting relevant text with read_text
   to confirm the document likely contains the visual you need.
5. Don't use this tool in a broad-spectrum way (don't call on many documents
   just to see what might be relevant).
6. Never call this tool in parallel with itself.
7. Users like visual evidence, so call this tool at least once before answering so you
   can provide some image citations in the answer. Add this to your plan so you do not forget.
   For images to be presented to the user, they must be cited in the final answer after retrieval
   by this tool. You may ignore this instruction if the request is for the question is asking
   for a specific piece of information and is not an open-ended research question.
8. NEVER cite full-page images in your final answer, unless they are full-page panels figures or
   tables. Cite relevant cropped regions if available, otherwise consider calling this tool again
   to find a smaller region that answers the query.

query.description:
Query to search for visual content in the document. If you know the specific
figure/table/formula you are looking for, you can specify it here (e.g. "find me
Figure 3" or "extract the table on page 10"). You can also request based on content
(e.g. "find me all volcano plots of gene X" or "I need the formula for the growth rate").
You can even include explicit instructions for the LLM agent to follow (if helpful, not
necessarily required). The more specific and clear your query is here, the higher the
chance of success.
query.title: Query

text_name.description: Name of a text chunk (e.g., 'smith2023example pages 1-5').
text_name.title: Text Name

info.name: create_artifact
info.description:
Create an artifact that can be used in the final answer, based on information
you have collected so far.

How it works: the full message history thus far is passed to another LLM,
along with the question and your description. The LLM is tasked to create
an artifact that matches your specifications, including a title, caption,
and markdown content. You drive the process by specifying the type and
describing what you want.

Only use this tool *after* you have gathered sufficient evidence or
information to create a new artifact. Do not call this tool in parallel
with tools that are searching for information or evidence that would be
necessary to create this artifact.

It is very helpful after calls to several search tools,
after calls to the `gather_evidence` tool, but it is not required.
However, it should be called before the `answer` tool at
least one time, but more may be given if specifically helpful for
the task, so that the `answer` tool can use the artifacts for the answer.

An artifact can be any string representable fact or list of facts,
formatted using markdown syntax. The artifact will be generated as one of
the following types:
- table: Markdown tables with rows and columns (e.g., findings, methods, molecules)
- code_block: Code snippets in fenced code blocks
- blockquote: Quoted text or important facts (using > prefix)
- image: AI-generated image (chart, diagram, or visualization)

Each artifact consists of:
- A short title (3-8 words) that names what the artifact contains
- A caption (1-2 sentences) describing what the artifact shows and why it's useful
- Markdown content in a single element type (validated automatically with retries if needed)
  For image type, content is a markdown image embed ``![title](data:image/png;base64,...)``,
  embedding the PNG directly as a base64 data URL.

artifact_type.description:
Artifact type to create. Must be one of: table,
code_block, blockquote, image.
artifact_type.title: Artifact Type

description.description:
Description of what you want in the artifact. Be specific
about what information should be included, how it should be organized,
and what columns/sections/content you want. This guides the summary LLM.
For image type, describe the visual you want generated in detail.
description.title: Description

info.name: gather_evidence
info.description:
Given a specific question, gather evidence from full-text of documents that have been retrieved by
previous tool calls.

Here is how the tool works: the provided question is posed to all texts retrieved thus far
by other tools. An LLM sees each text independently and is prompted to find information relevant
to the question.

That is, we sample evidence `e \sim LLM(prompt, text, q)` for each text. We only return pieces of
evidence `e` that are sufficiently relevant to the question; if the text is irrelevant, then `e`
is not included in the final response of this tool. Note that this LLM does not see the conversation
history thus far, only the question and each text. Note that it only sees text, no images or other
media from the contents (though tables and formulae may be parseable in some cases).

Think of this tool as an efficient way to deeply scan all retrieved documents for evidence. It
is complementary to the `read_text` tool, which can be used to directly inspect a subset of the
retrieved documents.

A valuable time to invoke this tool is after another tool finds potentially relevant documents.
Feel free to invoke this tool in parallel with other tools, but do not call this tool in parallel with itself.
Only invoke this tool when the document count is above zero, or this tool will be useless.

If you call this tool 2+ times and repeatedly get 'No relevant evidence found',
consider whether the answer might be in visual content (figures/tables) rather than text,
and use appropriate tools if available to access that content.

Once you receive evidence from this tool:

1. Read Carefully: Don't skim. The evidence may contain multiple similar terms
   or values—make sure you identify the RIGHT one.

2. For Comparative Questions: If the question asks "which X has greatest Y",
   check that the evidence includes information about ALL relevant X options, not
   just one or two. If you only see partial information, gather more evidence.

3. Check for Noise: Verify that numbers or terms aren't just metadata
   (page numbers, reference IDs, dates). Look for the value in meaningful
   scientific context.

4. Context Matters: A term appearing 71 times doesn't mean it's always the answer.
   Verify which occurrence actually answers the question asked.

If the evidence doesn't clearly answer the question, gather more evidence before
concluding. Don't guess based on partial information.

question.description: Specific question to gather evidence for.
question.title: Question

text_name_focus_list.description:
if there are known text names that are relevant to the question,
you can specify them here. This will ensure that the tool will look at these text names

info.name: answer
info.description:
Submit your final answer to the user. You MUST call this tool on your final answer.
If you mark all objectives as completed but have not yet called this tool, then
the answer will not be submitted. Note that this tool should only be called once, as it
immediately terminates the run and submits the answer.

Write an answer based on the evidence from gather_evidence results in message history.
If the context provides insufficient information, provide a partial answer and explain
why it is partial (e.g. the question is currently an open question in the field,
you were unable to obtain certain papers, etc.).

For each part of your answer, indicate which sources most support it via citation IDs at
the end of sentences, like (pqac-0000ABCD).
You are only allowed to cite available context IDs, indicated in previous messages by "Context IDs that may be cited:".
## Valid citation examples, only use comma/space delimited parentheticals:

- (pqac-0000ABCD, pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000IJKL)

## Invalid citation examples:

- (pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD;pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD-pqac-0000EFGH)
- pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH
- Example's work pqac-0000ABCD
- (pages pqac-0000ABCD)
- ![description](pqac-0000ABCD)
- ()

Do not concatenate citation ids, just use them as-is.

When writing your answer, embed artifacts if relevant artifacts have been previously
created by the `create_artifact` tool. To embed an artifact, simply include this in your
answer, on its own line and without any additional formatting (i.e. no backticks, code fences,
quotes, additional tags, etc.).
Always provide at least some introductory sentence or paragraph before referring to an artifact,
so that the artifact is properly contextualized.
Here is an example reference to an artifact with ID "artifact-XY":
<artifact id="artifact-XY"/>

Valid artifact IDs are indicated in previous messages by 'Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer:'.


Double-check your answer:

1. Evidence Match: Re-read the specific evidence snippets you're citing. Does your
   answer actually match what they say? Don't rely on memory from 20+ messages ago.

2. For Comparative Questions ("which X has greatest/maximum/highest Y"):
   - Did you examine ALL options mentioned in the evidence?
   - Did you explicitly compare their values?
   - Example: If evidence mentions "Pancreatic: 0.92, Liver: 0.85, Lung: 0.78",
     verify you compared all three, not just two.

3. For Specific Value Questions ("How many", "What is the", specific terms):
   - Does the exact value you're stating appear in the evidence?
   - Are you extracting from the right context (not noise like page numbers)?

4. Sanity Check: Read your answer. Does it directly address what was asked?

Don't use Markdown image syntax like ![...](...) to reference media in your answer,
because media get automatically linked by the system based on your answer's citations.

Write in the style of a scientific article, with concise sentences and
coherent paragraphs. This answer will be used directly,
so do not add any extraneous information.

answer.description: valid answer, cited with citation IDs.
answer.title: Answer

messages.content:
Use the tools to answer the question: Provide a state-of-the-art landscape of generative CAD and generative design tools and research as of 2025. Cover: (a) commercial topology-optimization / generative-design products (Autodesk Fusion Generative Design, nTopology / nTop, Siemens NX Topology Optimizer, PTC Creo Generative Design, ANSYS Discovery, Altair Inspire, Rhino + Grasshopper); (b) parametric / code-based CAD frameworks (CadQuery, OpenSCAD, build123d, Onshape FeatureScript, JSCAD); (c) AI/LLM-driven CAD generation research (Text2CAD, CAD-LLM, DeepCAD, SkexGen, Point2CAD, sketch-to-3D, multimodal diffusion-based CAD, agentic CAD pipelines); and (d) constraints/limitations of each (manufacturability, editability, parametric controllability, scriptability for CI/CD). Quote benchmark results where available. Cite vendor pages and ~5 recent (2022-2025) peer-reviewed or arXiv papers.

Start by making a plan, and update the plan as you go. Your answer will be returned to the user when all plan objectives are completed. Be thorough and ensure your evidence matches the user's question.

Retrieved Papers:
## 1. Performance-Driven Engineering Design Approaches Based on Generative Design and Topology Optimization Tools: A Comparative Study
Valid Text Names:
'barbieri2022performancedrivenengineeringdesign pages 1-4', 'barbieri2022performancedrivenengineeringdesign pages 4-6', 'barbieri2022performancedrivenengineeringdesign pages 6-10', 'barbieri2022performancedrivenengineeringdesign pages 10-14', 'barbieri2022performancedrivenengineeringdesign pages 14-17', 'barbieri2022performancedrivenengineeringdesign pages 17-21', 'barbieri2022performancedrivenengineeringdesign pages 21-23', 'barbieri2022performancedrivenengineeringdesign pages 23-23'

BibTex:
@article{barbieri2022performancedrivenengineeringdesign,
    author = "Barbieri, Loris and Muzzupappa, Maurizio",
    title = "Performance-Driven Engineering Design Approaches Based on Generative Design and Topology Optimization Tools: A Comparative Study",
    year = "2022",
    journal = "Applied Sciences",
    volume = "12",
    pages = "2106",
    month = "Feb",
    doi = "10.3390/app12042106",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.3390/app12042106",
    publisher = "MDPI AG",
    issue = "4",
    issn = "2076-3417"
}


Abstract:
The advent of Additive Manufacturing (AM) is uncovering the limits of the current CAD systems and, at the same time, is highlighting the potentials of the Topology Optimization (TO) and Generative Design (GD) tools that had not been fully exploited until now. Differently from the traditional design approach in which designers occupy a predominant role in each stage of the design process, the introduction of such tools in the product development process pushes toward simulation-driven design approaches which imply a significant change in the role of the designer. To this end, the paper presents a comparison of two different design methods for Additive Manufacturing based on the adoption of TO and GD tools. The comparison aims to offer a reflection on the evolution of the traditional approach when TO and GD tools are used, and to highlight the potential and limitations of these optimization tools when adopted in an integrated manner with the CAD systems. Furthermore, this comparative study can be a useful and practical source for designers to identify the most appropriate approach to adopt based on their needs and project resources. The comparative study is carried out through the design study of a prototype of a rocker arm and a brake pedal for the Formula Student race car. Their results, compared in terms of mechanical performances, show that both TO and especially GD tools can be efficiently adopted early in a design process oriented to AM to redesign components to make them lighter and stronger.

## 2. Integrating generative design and topology optimisation with product design values
Valid Text Names:
'nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 1-4', 'nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 4-8', 'nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 8-11', 'nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 11-14', 'nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 14-17', 'nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 17-18'

BibTex:
@article{nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign,
    author = "Nemme, Anton and Walden, Roderick",
    title = "Integrating generative design and topology optimisation with product design values",
    year = "2022",
    journal = "Australasian Journal of Technology Education",
    volume = "8",
    month = "Dec",
    doi = "10.15663/ajte.v8i.75",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.15663/ajte.v8i.75",
    publisher = "University of Waikato",
    issn = "2382-2007"
}


Abstract:
Advances in computer technology and software increasingly encourage the usage of CAD tools for designing forms that algorithmically manipulate ‘structural’ and ‘surface’ features. These sophisticated new computational processes, broadly known as ‘generative design’ and ‘topology optimisation’, are very likely to become a regular part of the product design process for many types of products. A core value of design practice is the development of intuition and iterative skills to explore the technical and experiential performance of design concepts through sketching, model making, and prototyping. Identifying ways to integrate ‘generative design’ and ‘topology optimisation’ CAD processes with ‘making’ as a core value in product design concept development is a significant challenge - particularly for design education. A related concern is that ‘topology optimisation’ can generate structurally optimised parts for the amount and type of material used, which essentially determines the fabrication method. Often these parts in their raw form can only be made using 3D printing technologies, though they can (and often need to) be ‘styled’ or modified. Therefore, the relationship to 3D printing and its limitations as an end-part manufacturing technology must be critically tested as part of the design process. The practice-led research presented includes a case study of the design of a mountain bike (MTB) crank arm developed using an integrated design process that incorporates a series of ‘topology optimisation’ simulations. The authors undertook the project to inform the design of a new ‘generative design’ and ‘topology optimisation’ studio-based subject to be offered to second and third-year product design students at the University of Technology Sydney. The research proposes a form of integrated design practice that values ‘making’ iteratively, and the advancing CAD-based ‘generative design’ and ‘topology optimisation’ tools to responsibly support experiential learning in product design, manufacturing and engineering.

## 3. Artificial Intelligence in Generative Design: A Structured Review of Trends and Opportunities in Techniques and Applications

    title = "Artificial Intelligence in Generative Design: A Structured Review of Trends and Opportunities in Techniques and Applications",

    url = "https://doi.org/10.3390/designs9040079",
    publisher = "MDPI AG",

    issn = "2411-9660"
}


Abstract:
This review explores the intersection of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Generative Design (GD) in engineering within the mechanical, industrial, civil, and architectural domains. Driven by advances in AI and computational resources, this intersection has grown rapidly, yielding over 14,000 publications since 2016. To map the research landscape, this review employed semantic search and Natural Language Processing, parsing 14,355 publications to ultimately select the 88 most relevant studies through clustering and topic modelling. These studies were categorised according to AI and GD techniques, application domains, benefits, and limitations, providing insights into research trends and practical implications. The results reveal a significant growth in the integration of advanced generative AI methods, notably Generative Adversarial Networks for direct design generation, alongside the continued use of genetic algorithms and surrogate models (e.g., Convolutional Neural Networks and Multilayer Perceptrons) to manage computational complexity. Structural and aerodynamic applications were the most common, with benefits including improvements in computational efficiency and design diversity. However, barriers remain, including data generation costs, model accuracy, and interpretability. Research opportunities include the development of generalisable foundation surrogate models, the integration of emerging generative methods such as diffusion models and large language models, and the explicit consideration of manufacturability constraints within generative processes.

Abstract:
The continuous progress in Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) has spurred the exploration of novel design approaches to boost their effectiveness. Many drone configuration design methods have been used to enhance strength and reduce weight, such as topology optimization, high-modulus composite material, additive manufacturing, etc. One rapidly emerging technology with the potential to transform UAV design is generative design. This cutting-edge technology employs artificial intelligence to generate numerous design possibilities, assisting engineers in identifying optimal designs that align with precise requirements. Consequently, it has the potential to enhance UAV performance, efficiency, and cost-effectiveness significantly. This paper delves into various generative design approaches for drones, covering structural components, aerodynamics, energy efficiency, and payload distribution applications. Real-world case studies prove the benefits of integrating generative design into the UAV development process. These studies demonstrate the effectiveness of generative design and pave the way for significant advancements in UAV capabilities and applications, instilling confidence in its potential.

Abstract:
Integrating deep learning methodologies is pivotal in shaping the continuous evolution of computer-aided design (CAD) and computer-aided engineering (CAE) systems. This review explores the integration of deep learning in CAD and CAE, particularly focusing on generative models for simulating 3D vehicle wheels. It highlights the challenges of traditional CAD/CAE, such as manual design and simulation limitations, and proposes deep learning, especially generative models, as a solution. The study aims to automate and enhance 3D vehicle wheel design, improve CAE simulations, predict mechanical characteristics, and optimize performance metrics. It employs deep learning architectures like variational autoencoders (VAEs), convolutional neural networks (CNNs), and generative adversarial networks (GANs) to learn from diverse 3D wheel designs and generate optimized solutions. The anticipated outcomes include more efficient design processes, improved simulation accuracy, and adaptable design solutions, facilitating the integration of deep learning models into existing CAD/CAE systems. This integration is expected to transform design and engineering practices by offering insights into the potential of these technologies.

Abstract:
This study investigated the integration of computer-aided design (CAD) and additive manufacturing (AM) in prototype production, particularly in the automotive industry. It explores how these technologies redefine prototyping practices, with a focus on design flexibility, material efficiency, and production speed. Adopting the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis (PRISMA) guidelines, this study encompasses a systematic review of 28 scholarly articles. It undertakes a comprehensive analysis to identify key themes, trends, and gaps in the existing research on CAD and AM integration in automotive prototyping. This study revealed the significant advantages of CAD and AM in prototype manufacturing, including improved design capabilities, efficient material usage, and the creation of complex geometries. It also addresses ongoing challenges, such as technology integration costs, scalability, and sustainability. Furthermore, this study foresees future developments by focusing on enhancing CAD and AM technologies to meet evolving market demands and optimize performance. This study makes a unique contribution to the literature by providing a detailed overview of the integration of CAD and AM in the context of automotive prototyping.
