## 7. Generative design of space frames for additive manufacturing technology
Valid Text Names:
'watson2023generativedesignof pages 1-2', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 2-4', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 4-6', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 6-8', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 8-10', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 10-12', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 12-14', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 14-17', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 17-19', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 19-20', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 20-21', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 21-21'

BibTex:
@article{watson2023generativedesignof,
    author = "Watson, Marcus and Leary, Martin and Downing, David and Brandt, Milan",
    title = "Generative design of space frames for additive manufacturing technology",
    year = "2023",
    journal = "The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology",
    volume = "127",
    pages = "4619-4639",
    month = "Jun",
    doi = "10.1007/s00170-023-11691-9",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1007/s00170-023-11691-9",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "9-10",
    issn = "0268-3768"
}


Abstract:
A generative design methodology is presented that solves for minimum volume and compliance space-frame systems, with consideration of stress and buckling constraints. The solution space is explored using formal topology optimisation routines. A parameterisation method converts voxelised topology optimisation solutions into skeletonised connectivity representations. An inequality constrained gradient descent optimisation method optimises and defines cross-sectional geometry. This enables fast and automatic solution generation, providing designers with sets of high-performing problem solutions. Skeleton representations provide an inexpensive modelling tool for parallel analysis of physical problems difficult to model using topology optimisation. Geometry is represented using traditional engineering cross-sections with well understood behaviour. This improves certainty in the performance of solutions, simplifying certification processes. The generative design of a structural aerospace bracket for additive manufacture has been used as a case study within this research. A design of experiments produced 360 topology optimisation results, altering input variables and discretisation resolution to identify their effect on solution outcomes and the performance of parameterisation. The proposed method was found to robustly generate high-performing solutions utilising low-resolution topology optimisation. Additionally, 6 high-performing topologies were identified, providing designers with a set of solutions to select from. Limitations on the parameterisation process were identified, with topology optimisation solutions with volume fractions above 0.2 not parameterising successfully, and simulations with a resolution of 5 mm element size and below generating low performing skeletonised topologies.

    year = "2505",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2505.17702",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2505.17702",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.17702"
}


Abstract:
The advent of Computer-Aided Design (CAD) generative modeling will significantly transform the design of industrial products. The recent research endeavor has extended into the realm of Large Language Models (LLMs). In contrast to fine-tuning methods, training-free approaches typically utilize the advanced closed-source LLMs, thereby offering enhanced flexibility and efficiency in the development of AI agents for generating CAD parametric models. However, the substantial cost and limitations of local deployment of the top-tier closed-source LLMs pose challenges in practical applications. The Seek-CAD is the pioneer exploration of locally deployed open-source inference LLM DeepSeek-R1 for CAD parametric model generation with a training-free methodology. This study is the first investigation to incorporate both visual and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) feedback within the self-refinement mechanism for generating CAD models. Specifically, the initial generated parametric CAD model is rendered into a sequence of step-wise perspective images, which are subsequently processed by a Vision Language Model (VLM) alongside the corresponding CoTs derived from DeepSeek-R1 to assess the CAD model generation. Then, the feedback is utilized by DeepSeek-R1 to refine the initial generated model for the next round of generation. Moreover, we present an innovative 3D CAD model dataset structured around the SSR (Sketch, Sketch-based feature, and Refinements) triple design paradigm. This dataset encompasses a wide range of CAD commands, thereby aligning effectively with industrial application requirements and proving suitable for the generation of LLMs. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of Seek-CAD under various metrics.

## 2. CADCrafter: Generating Computer-Aided Design Models from Unconstrained Images
Valid Text Names:
'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 1-2', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 2-3', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 3-4', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 4-6', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 6-7', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 7-8', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 8-9', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 9-11', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 11-12', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 12-13'

    author = "Chen, Cheng and Wei, Jiacheng and Chen, Tianrun and Zhang, Chi and Yang, Xiaofeng and Zhang, Shangzhan and Yang, Bingchen and Foo, Chuan-Sheng and Lin, Guosheng and Huang, Qixing and Liu, Fayao",
    title = "CADCrafter: Generating Computer-Aided Design Models from Unconstrained Images",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "2025 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)",
    pages = "11073-11082",
    month = "Apr",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2504.04753",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2504.04753"
}


Abstract:
Creating CAD digital twins from the physical world is crucial for manufacturing, design, and simulation. However, current methods typically rely on costly 3D scanning with labor-intensive post-processing. To provide a user-friendly design process, we explore the problem of reverse engineering from unconstrained real-world CAD images that can be easily captured by users of all experiences. However, the scarcity of real-world CAD data poses challenges in directly training such models. To tackle these challenges, we propose CADCrafter, an image-to-parametric CAD model generation framework that trains solely on synthetic textureless CAD data while testing on real-world images. To bridge the significant representation disparity between images and parametric CAD models, we introduce a geometry encoder to accurately capture diverse geometric features. Moreover, the texture-invariant properties of the geometric features can also facilitate the generalization to real-world scenarios. Since compiling CAD parameter sequences into explicit CAD models is a non-differentiable process, the network training inherently lacks explicit geometric supervision. To impose geometric validity constraints, we employ direct preference optimization (DPO) to fine-tune our model with the automatic code checker feedback on CAD sequence quality. Furthermore, we collected a real-world dataset, comprised of multi-view images and corresponding CAD command sequence pairs, to evaluate our method. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach can robustly handle real unconstrained CAD images, and even generalize to unseen general objects.

Abstract:
Geometric Deep Learning techniques have become a transformative force in the field of Computer-Aided Design (CAD), and have the potential to revolutionize how designers and engineers approach and enhance the design process. By harnessing the power of machine learning-based methods, CAD designers can optimize their workflows, save time and effort while making better informed decisions, and create designs that are both innovative and practical. The ability to process the CAD designs represented by geometric data and to analyze their encoded features enables the identification of similarities among diverse CAD models, the proposition of alternative designs and enhancements, and even the generation of novel design alternatives. This survey offers a comprehensive overview of learning-based methods in computer-aided design across various categories, including similarity analysis and retrieval, 2D and 3D CAD model synthesis, and CAD generation from point clouds, and single/multi-view images. Additionally, it provides a complete list of benchmark datasets and their characteristics, along with open-source codes that have propelled research in this domain. The final discussion delves into the challenges prevalent in this field, followed by potential future research directions in this rapidly evolving field.

Abstract:
This paper presents a novel generative model for Computer Aided Design (CAD) that 1) represents high-level design concepts of a CAD model as a three-level hierarchical tree of neural codes, from global part arrangement down to local curve geometry; and 2) controls the generation or completion of CAD models by specifying the target design using a code tree. Concretely, a novel variant of a vector quantized VAE with"masked skip connection"extracts design variations as neural codebooks at three levels. Two-stage cascaded auto-regressive transformers learn to generate code trees from incomplete CAD models and then complete CAD models following the intended design. Extensive experiments demonstrate superior performance on conventional tasks such as random generation while enabling novel interaction capabilities on conditional generation tasks. The code is available at https://github.com/samxuxiang/hnc-cad.

## 6. GenCAD-3D: CAD Program Generation using Multimodal Latent Space Alignment and Synthetic Dataset Balancing

Abstract:

 CAD programs, structured as parametric sequences of commands that compile into precise 3D geometries, are fundamental to accurate and efficient engineering design processes. Generating these programs from nonparametric data such as point clouds and meshes remains a crucial yet challenging task, typically requiring extensive manual intervention. Current deep generative models aimed at automating CAD generation are significantly limited by imbalanced and insufficiently large datasets, particularly those lacking representation for complex CAD programs. To address this, we introduce GenCAD-3D, a multimodal generative framework utilizing contrastive learning for aligning latent embeddings between CAD and geometric encoders, combined with latent diffusion models for CAD sequence generation and retrieval. Additionally, we present SynthBal, a synthetic data augmentation strategy specifically designed to balance and expand datasets, notably enhancing representation of complex CAD geometries. Our experiments show that SynthBal significantly boosts reconstruction accuracy, reduces the generation of invalid CAD models, and markedly improves performance on high-complexity geometries, surpassing existing benchmarks. These advancements hold substantial implications for streamlining reverse engineering and enhancing automation in engineering design.

## 7. GenCAD-Self-Repairing: Feasibility Enhancement for 3D CAD Generation
Valid Text Names:

    year = "2025",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2505.23287",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2505.23287",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.23287"
}


Abstract:
With the advancement of generative AI, research on its application to 3D model generation has gained traction, particularly in automating the creation of Computer-Aided Design (CAD) files from images. GenCAD is a notable model in this domain, leveraging an autoregressive transformer-based architecture with a contrastive learning framework to generate CAD programs. However, a major limitation of GenCAD is its inability to consistently produce feasible boundary representations (B-reps), with approximately 10% of generated designs being infeasible. To address this, we propose GenCAD-Self-Repairing, a framework that enhances the feasibility of generative CAD models through diffusion guidance and a self-repairing pipeline. This framework integrates a guided diffusion denoising process in the latent space and a regression-based correction mechanism to refine infeasible CAD command sequences while preserving geometric accuracy. Our approach successfully converted two-thirds of infeasible designs in the baseline method into feasible ones, significantly improving the feasibility rate while simultaneously maintaining a reasonable level of geometric accuracy between the point clouds of ground truth models and generated models. By significantly improving the feasibility rate of generating CAD models, our approach helps expand the availability of high-quality training data and enhances the applicability of AI-driven CAD generation in manufacturing, architecture, and product design.

## 8. Generating CAD Code with Vision-Language Models for 3D Designs

    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2410.05340",

    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
Generative AI has transformed the fields of Design and Manufacturing by providing efficient and automated methods for generating and modifying 3D objects. One approach involves using Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate Computer- Aided Design (CAD) scripting code, which can then be executed to render a 3D object; however, the resulting 3D object may not meet the specified requirements. Testing the correctness of CAD generated code is challenging due to the complexity and structure of 3D objects (e.g., shapes, surfaces, and dimensions) that are not feasible in code. In this paper, we introduce CADCodeVerify, a novel approach to iteratively verify and improve 3D objects generated from CAD code. Our approach works by producing ameliorative feedback by prompting a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to generate and answer a set of validation questions to verify the generated object and prompt the VLM to correct deviations. To evaluate CADCodeVerify, we introduce, CADPrompt, the first benchmark for CAD code generation, consisting of 200 natural language prompts paired with expert-annotated scripting code for 3D objects to benchmark progress. Our findings show that CADCodeVerify improves VLM performance by providing visual feedback, enhancing the structure of the 3D objects, and increasing the success rate of the compiled program. When applied to GPT-4, CADCodeVerify achieved a 7.30% reduction in Point Cloud distance and a 5.0% improvement in success rate compared to prior work

Abstract:
Computer-aided design (CAD) is fundamental to modern engineering and manufacturing, but creating CAD models still requires expert knowledge and specialized software. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) open up the possibility of generative CAD, where natural language is directly translated into parametric 3D models. However, most existing methods generate task-specific command sequences that pretrained models cannot directly handle. These sequences must be converted into CAD representations such as CAD vectors before a 3D model can be produced, which requires training models from scratch and adds unnecessary complexity. To tackle this issue, we propose generating CadQuery code directly from text, leveraging the strengths of pretrained LLMs to produce 3D models without intermediate representations, using this Python-based scripting language. Since LLMs already excel at Python generation and spatial reasoning, fine-tuning them on Text-to-CadQuery data proves highly effective. Given that these capabilities typically improve with scale, we hypothesize that larger models will perform better after fine-tuning. To enable this, we augment the Text2CAD dataset with 170,000 CadQuery annotations. We fine-tune six open-source LLMs of varying sizes and observe consistent improvements. Our best model achieves a top-1 exact match of 69.3%, up from 58.8%, and reduces Chamfer Distance by 48.6%. Project page: https://github.com/Text-to-CadQuery/Text-to-CadQuery.

Abstract:
Computer-aided design (CAD) is a way to digitally create 2D drawings and 3D models of real-world products. Traditional CAD typically relies on hand-drawing by experts or modifications of existing library files, which doesn't allow for rapid personalization. With the emergence of generative artificial intelligence, convenient and efficient personalized CAD generation has become possible. However, existing generative methods typically produce outputs that lack interactive editability and geometric annotations, limiting their practical applications in manufacturing. To enable interactive generative CAD, we propose CAD-Coder, a framework that transforms natural language instructions into CAD script codes, which can be executed in Python environments to generate human-editable CAD files (.Dxf). To facilitate the generation of editable CAD sketches with annotation information, we construct a comprehensive dataset comprising 29,130 Dxf files with their corresponding script codes, where each sketch preserves both editability and geometric annotations. We evaluate CAD-Coder on various 2D/3D CAD generation tasks against existing methods, demonstrating superior interactive capabilities while uniquely providing editable sketches with geometric annotations.

## 11. A Survey on Deep Learning in 3D CAD Reconstruction
Valid Text Names:
'lin2025asurveyon pages 1-3', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 3-4', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 4-6', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 6-7', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 7-9', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 9-11', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 11-13', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 13-14', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 14-16', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 16-18', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 18-19', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 19-21', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 21-22', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 22-24', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 24-25'

    issn = "2076-3417"
}


Abstract:
Three-dimensional CAD reconstruction is a long-standing and important task in fields such as industrial manufacturing, architecture, medicine, film and television, research, and education. Reconstructing CAD models remains a persistent challenge in machine learning. There have been many studies on deep learning in the field of 3D reconstruction. In recent years, with the release of CAD datasets, there have been more and more studies on 3D CAD reconstruction using deep learning. With the continuous deepening of research, deep learning has significantly improved the performance of tasks in the field of CAD reconstruction. However, this task remains challenging due to data scarcity and labeling difficulties, model complexity, and lack of generality and adaptability. This paper reviews both classic and recent research results on 3D CAD reconstruction tasks based on deep learning. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first investigation focusing on the CAD reconstruction task in the field of deep learning. Since there are relatively few studies related to 3D CAD reconstruction, we also investigate the reconstruction and generation of 2D CAD sketches. According to the different input data, we divide all investigations into the following categories: point cloud input to 3D CAD models, sketch input to 3D CAD models, other input to 3D CAD models, reconstruction and generation of 2D sketches, characterization of CAD data, CAD datasets, and related evaluation indicators. Commonly used datasets are outlined in our taxonomy. We provide a brief overview of the current research background, challenges, and recent results. Finally, future research directions are discussed.

## 12. Revisiting CAD Model Generation by Learning Raster Sketch

    issue = "5",
    issn = "2374-3468"
}


Abstract:
The integration of deep generative networks into generating Computer-Aided Design (CAD) models has garnered increasing attention over recent years. Traditional methods often rely on discrete sequences of parametric line/curve segments to represent sketches. Differently, we introduce RECAD, a novel framework that generates Raster sketches and 3D Extrusions for CAD models. Representing sketches as raster images offers several advantages over discrete sequences: 1) it breaks the limitations on the types and numbers of lines/curves, providing enhanced geometric representation capabilities; 2) it enables interpolation within a continuous latent space; and 3) it allows for more intuitive user control over the output. Technically, RECAD employs two diffusion networks: the first network generates extrusion boxes conditioned on the number and types of extrusions, while the second network produces sketch images conditioned on these extrusion boxes. By combining these two networks, RECAD effectively generates sketch-and-extrude CAD models, offering a more robust and intuitive approach to CAD model generation. Experimental results indicate that RECAD achieves strong performance in unconditional generation, while also demonstrating effectiveness in conditional generation and output editing.

Abstract:
Designing complex computer-aided design (CAD) models is often time-consuming due to challenges such as computational inefficiency and the difficulty of generating precise models. We propose a novel language-guided framework for industrial design automation to address these issues, integrating large language models (LLMs) with computer-automated design (CAutoD).Through this framework, CAD models are automatically generated from parameters and appearance descriptions, supporting the automation of design tasks during the detailed CAD design phase. Our approach introduces three key innovations: (1) a semi-automated data annotation pipeline that leverages LLMs and vision-language large models (VLLMs) to generate high-quality parameters and appearance descriptions; (2) a Transformer-based CAD generator (TCADGen) that predicts modeling sequences via dual-channel feature aggregation; (3) an enhanced CAD modeling generation model, called CADLLM, that is designed to refine the generated sequences by incorporating the confidence scores from TCADGen. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms traditional methods in both accuracy and efficiency, providing a powerful tool for automating industrial workflows and generating complex CAD models from textual prompts. The code is available at https://jianxliao.github.io/cadllm-page/

Abstract:
<jats:p>Boundary representation (B-rep) is the de facto standard for CAD model representation in modern industrial design. The intricate coupling between geometric and topological elements in B-rep structures has forced existing generative methods to rely on cascaded multi-stage networks, resulting in error accumulation and computational inefficiency. We present BrepGPT, a single-stage autoregressive framework for B-rep generation. Our key innovation lies in the Voronoi Half-Patch (VHP) representation, which decomposes B-reps into unified local units by assigning geometry to nearest half-edges and sampling their next pointers. Unlike hierarchical representations that require multiple distinct encodings for different structural levels, our VHP representation facilitates unifying geometric attributes and topological relations in a single, coherent format. We further leverage dual VQ-VAEs to encode both vertex topology and Voronoi Half-Patches into vertex-based tokens, achieving a more compact sequential encoding. A decoder-only Transformer is then trained to autoregressively predict these tokens, which are subsequently mapped to vertex-based features and decoded into complete B-rep models. Experiments demonstrate that BrepGPT achieves state-of-the-art performance in unconditional B-rep generation. The framework also exhibits versatility in various applications, including conditional generation from category labels, point clouds, text descriptions, and images, as well as B-rep autocompletion and interpolation.</jats:p>

## 1. LLM4CAD: Multi-Modal Large Language Models for 3D Computer-Aided Design Generation
Valid Text Names:
'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 1-2', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 3-4', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 3-3', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 4-5', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 4-4', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 5-6', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 5-5', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 6-6', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 7-7', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 7-8', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 8-8', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 9-9', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 9-10', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 10-11', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 10-10', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 11-11', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 11-12', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 12-12', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 13-13'

@article{li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge,
    author = "Li, Xingang and Sun, Yuewan and Sha, Zhenghui",
    title = "LLM4CAD: Multi-Modal Large Language Models for 3D Computer-Aided Design Generation",
    year = "2024",
    journal = "Volume 6: 36th International Conference on Design Theory and Methodology (DTM)",
    month = "Aug",
    doi = "10.1115/detc2024-143740",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1115/detc2024-143740",
    publisher = "American Society of Mechanical Engineers"
}


Abstract:

 The evolution of multimodal large language models (LLMs) capable of processing diverse input modalities (e.g., text and images) holds new prospects for their application in engineering design, such as the generation of 3D computer-aided design (CAD) models. However, little is known about the ability of multimodal LLMs to generate 3D design objects, and there is a lack of quantitative assessment. In this study, we develop an approach to enable two LLMs, GPT-4 and GPT-4V, to generate 3D CAD models (i.e., LLM4CAD) and perform experiments to evaluate their efficacy. To address the challenge of data scarcity for multimodal LLM studies, we created a data synthesis pipeline to generate CAD models, sketches, and image data of typical mechanical components (e.g., gears and springs) and collect their natural-language descriptions with dimensional information using Amazon Mechanical Turk. We positioned the CAD program (programming script for CAD design) as a bridge, facilitating the conversion of LLMs’ textual output into tangible CAD design objects. We focus on two critical capabilities: the generation of syntactically correct CAD programs (Cap1) and the accuracy of the parsed 3D shapes (Cap2) quantified by intersection over union. The results show that both GPT-4 and GPT-4V demonstrate potential in 3D CAD generation. Specifically, on average, GPT-4V outperforms when processing only text-based input, exceeding the results obtained using multimodal inputs, such as text with image, for Cap 1 and Cap 2. However, when examining category-specific results of mechanical components, while the same trend still holds for Cap 2, the prominence of multimodal inputs is increasingly evident for more complex geometries (e.g., springs and gears) in Cap 1. The potential of multimodal LLMs in enhancing 3D CAD generation is clear, but their application must be carefully calibrated to the complexity of the target CAD models to be generated.

Abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT:</jats:title><jats:p>Design generation using traditional Computer-Aided Design (CAD) tools remains a labor-intensive and manual task. This paper introduces a framework for automating CAD geometry generation using Large Language Models (LLMs) with function calling and agent workflows. The framework enables both expert and novice designers to use textual prompts to automatically generate CAD code. We evaluate it with five LLMs and four agent workflows. The agent workflow incorporating automated visual feedback outperforms the others, especially with multimodal LLMs like ChatGPT-4o. A case study shows its use in topology optimization and additive manufacturing with minimal human input. Remaining challenges include limitations in spatial reasoning, prompt dependency, and workflow adaptability. Future work should focus on improving design-for-manufacturing capabilities, visual tools, and evaluation benchmarking.</jats:p>

## 6. Foundation Model Driven Robotics: A Comprehensive Review
Valid Text Names:
'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 1-1', 'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 1-3', 'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 3-4', 'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 4-5', 'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 5-6', 'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 6-7', 'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 7-9', 'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 9-10', 'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 10-11', 'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 11-12', 'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 12-13', 'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 13-13', 'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 13-14', 'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 14-15', 'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 15-16', 'khan2507foundationmodeldriven pages 16-16'

    author = "Khan, Muhammad Tayyab and Waheed, Ammar",
    title = "Foundation Model Driven Robotics: A Comprehensive Review",
    year = "2507",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2507.10087",
    month = "Jul",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2507.10087",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2507.10087"
}


Abstract:
The rapid emergence of foundation models, particularly Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs), has introduced a transformative paradigm in robotics. These models offer powerful capabilities in semantic understanding, high-level reasoning, and cross-modal generalization, enabling significant advances in perception, planning, control, and human-robot interaction. This critical review provides a structured synthesis of recent developments, categorizing applications across simulation-driven design, open-world execution, sim-to-real transfer, and adaptable robotics. Unlike existing surveys that emphasize isolated capabilities, this work highlights integrated, system-level strategies and evaluates their practical feasibility in real-world environments. Key enabling trends such as procedural scene generation, policy generalization, and multimodal reasoning are discussed alongside core bottlenecks, including limited embodiment, lack of multimodal data, safety risks, and computational constraints. Through this lens, this paper identifies both the architectural strengths and critical limitations of foundation model-based robotics, highlighting open challenges in real-time operation, grounding, resilience, and trust. The review concludes with a roadmap for future research aimed at bridging semantic reasoning and physical intelligence through more robust, interpretable, and embodied models.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000000

From text 'souvanhnakhoomman2025acomprehensivereview pages 5-6'.

Sane Souvanhnakhoomman and Alvin Chua. A comprehensive review of generative design applications in unmanned aerial vehicles. ASEAN Engineering Journal, 15:163-175, Feb 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.11113/aej.v15.21286, doi:10.11113/aej.v15.21286. This article has 4 citations.

The excerpt describes capabilities, workflows, and limitations for several commercial generative-design tools. Fusion 360 uses topology optimization to identify optimal material distribution, generates many design iterations that users can visualize and validate via simulation, and in case studies achieved significant mass reduction (e.g., 705 g to 586 g). CogniCAD (introduced 2018) is focused on GD/AM generation but cannot create geometries natively—users must import STEP files from an external CAD system and configure loads/grid settings. Solid Edge’s generative tools are limited in the student edition; full functionality requires a licensed version and workflows typically start from a main-body sketch with geometry-preservation areas and cut-outs. Siemens NX, combined with Simcenter HEEDS, can automatically generate and compare hundreds to thousands of optimized options and integrate NX geometry with Simcenter CAE for solutions targeted at additive manufacturing. The table of algorithms highlights trade-offs: methods like genetic algorithms suit complex multi-objective optimization but can be computationally expensive and slow to converge; swarm approaches have fast convergence but risk local optima; AI approaches can learn and optimize complex dynamic systems yet demand significant compute and large datasets. These points summarize practical strengths and limitations observable in commercial GD/topology tools in the excerpt.


---

## pqac-00000001

From text 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 2-4'.

Marcus Watson, Martin Leary, David Downing, and Milan Brandt. Generative design of space frames for additive manufacturing technology. The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology, 127:4619-4639, Jun 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s00170-023-11691-9, doi:10.1007/s00170-023-11691-9. This article has 43 citations.

The excerpt outlines general capabilities, common features and key limitations of generative design (GD) and topology optimisation (TO) approaches relevant to commercial tools. GD methods encode rules to generate many candidate forms, helping avoid designer fixation and producing multiple embodiments rapidly. TO is performance-driven (often using FEM) and includes material-distribution methods such as SIMP and BESO; it excels at structural light-weighting but tends to produce voxelised density fields. Major limitations include sensitivity to input definitions (loads/boundaries), local optima, high computational cost for high-resolution problems, and difficulty capturing complex, multi-case design requirements within a single TO run. A practical challenge is translating voxel TO outputs into manufacturable parametric CAD (B‑splines, contours, polygons, or fitting to control points). For additive manufacturing, DFAM constraints must be integrated early, and GD/TO are recommended as exploratory tools to survey solution spaces rather than as turnkey net‑design solvers.

From text 'nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 8-11'.

Anton Nemme and Roderick Walden. Integrating generative design and topology optimisation with product design values. Australasian Journal of Technology Education, Dec 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.15663/ajte.v8i.75, doi:10.15663/ajte.v8i.75. This article has 6 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes capabilities and some practical constraints of topology optimisation and generative-design tools relevant to product design education. Tools like nTopology and Materialise 3-Matic can generate and manipulate lattice structures driven by FEA data, which is beneficial for powder-based additive manufacturing. Autodesk Fusion 360 was chosen for the authors' topology optimisation experiment because it is accessible to students and offers practical training; SolidWorks is noted as another viable option. Typical solver behaviour requires user inputs (e.g., a mass reduction percentage, often 30–40%) and then removes material from a shape envelope to maximize stiffness. Multiple design alternatives can be produced by varying envelope size, symmetry, force location/magnitude, and mesh density. Limitations highlighted include the need for manual interpretation of many generated alternatives for manufacturability and performance, the potential for designer fixation, and that most tools are primarily engineer-focused rather than tailored for product-design education. The excerpt does not provide specific feature-by-feature comparisons for Siemens NX, PTC Creo, ANSYS Discovery, Altair Inspire, or Rhino+Grasshopper.

## pqac-00000003

From text 'nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 4-8'.

Anton Nemme and Roderick Walden. Integrating generative design and topology optimisation with product design values. Australasian Journal of Technology Education, Dec 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.15663/ajte.v8i.75, doi:10.15663/ajte.v8i.75. This article has 6 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes key capabilities and some limitations of contemporary generative design (GD) and topology optimisation (TO) tools. Autodesk Fusion 360's Generative Design module can "generate the three-dimensional form of a part given a set of geometric and force parameters," supports designer-guided filtering of results, and lets operators apply manufacturing constraints so outputs are more manufacturable; its exuberant results are often better suited to additive manufacturing (AM). GD more broadly enables users to "generate and explore (a multitude of) alternative design proposals" and to "analyse and evaluate them," and increasingly the software tools can be "relocated at the centre of the design process." Common GD algorithms include L-systems, shape grammars, cellular automata, genetic algorithms and swarm intelligence. On TO, Altair's Optistruct was an early commercial product; TO workflows typically "remove material volume from a three-dimensional CAD model until a goal is reached," e.g., to "find the stiffest possible design using the least material." The literature notes many TO tools exist but are predominantly engineer-focused. The authors also distinguish TO for efficiency goals from topology manipulation for aesthetic changes, and observe AM remains best suited to small-batch or niche production—limiting practical manufacturing options for some GD/TO outputs.

Hu et al. (CVPR’23) for reconstruction from three orthographic views reports Precision 84.12%, Recall 82.05%, F1 82.62%. ## pqac-00000009

From text 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 1-3'.

Haoyang Xie and Feng Ju. Text-to-cadquery: a new paradigm for cad generation with scalable large model capabilities. ArXiv, May 2505. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507. This article has 29 citations.

The paper introduces Text-to-CadQuery, a paradigm that directly generates executable CadQuery Python code from natural language, removing intermediate command sequences. The authors expanded the Text2CAD/DeepCAD data to create roughly 170,000 text–CadQuery pairs for supervised fine-tuning. They fine-tuned six open-source LLMs spanning 124M to 7B parameters (CodeGPT-small 124M; GPT-2 medium 355M; GPT-2 large 774M; Gemma3-1B; Qwen2.5-3B; Mistral-7B with LoRA). Across these models they report consistent improvements and a clear scaling trend: larger models yield higher generation accuracy. Quantitatively, their best fine-tuned model attains a top-1 exact match of 69.3% (up from 58.8%) and reduces Chamfer Distance by 48.6%. They also note efficiency benefits: even the smallest model reached higher CAD generation accuracy with only 58 minutes of training compared to 2 days reported for Text2CAD. The authors attribute gains to leveraging pretrained models’ Python-generation and spatial reasoning strengths when trained on code-level CAD supervision.


---

## pqac-00000010

From text 'he2505cadcodertextguidedcadfiles pages 11-12'.

Changqi He, Shuhan Zhang, Liguo Zhang, and Jiajun Miao. Cad-coder:text-guided cad files code generation. ArXiv, May 2505. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.08686, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.08686. This article has 5 citations.

The excerpt reports benchmark and ablation results for a Text-to-CadQuery/CAD-Coder setup. Evaluation uses pass@k, APR, ACC-G/ACC-P/ACC-F and Chamfer Distance (CD) computed after converting DXF to point clouds (Comparecloud) and measuring CD with Open3D. Fine-tuning details: multi-round fine-tuning of DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B on 29,130 samples (~2.27M tokens) on one V100 (lr=0.0002, batch=4, seq=1048, 2 epochs). LoRA is described (layer increment equations shown) and the authors state: “we conclude that the LoRA method is more suitable for our task, as our dataset is not very large, and the LoRA method helps reduce the occurrence of overfitting.” Quantitative results show Chamfer distance improvements for the fine-tuned CAD-Coder variants: CAD-Coder with comments CD=0.88 vs without comments CD=1.31 (better when annotated); adding 3D training produced CD=0.72 in one variant. Compared to baselines, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B yields CD=0.88, Llama baseline CD=1.20, CodeLlama 1.76, Qwen-Coder 2.10, indicating Chamfer distance reduction after LLM fine-tuning and dataset/annotation choices. Pass@k and APR also improve with annotations and fine-tuning.


---

## pqac-00000011

From text 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 8-10'.

Haoyang Xie and Feng Ju. Text-to-cadquery: a new paradigm for cad generation with scalable large model capabilities. ArXiv, May 2505. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507. This article has 29 citations.

The Text-to-CadQuery benchmark reports state-of-the-art CAD generation results using CadQuery code output. A fine-tuned Qwen2.5-3B model achieved a Flash accuracy of 69.30%, a 10.5% absolute improvement over the baseline, and shows the best geometric scores (F1 median 0.9836, volumetric IoU median 0.9868). The paper evaluates geometric fidelity with a point-based F1 (10,000 sampled surface points, threshold τ = 0.02) and volumetric IoU (voxelization resolution 0.02). Larger models do not automatically yield better accuracy: Mistral-7B underperforms Qwen2.5-3B on accuracy metrics though it attains the lowest Invalid Rate (1.32), indicating better syntactic/executable code generation. A GPT-4.1 mini fine-tuned on 10k examples produced fully executable code (0% Invalid Rate) but its accuracy matched the 7B model, highlighting that larger or more capable LLMs improve executability but still require sufficient task data to improve 3D generation accuracy. The dataset contains 170,000 text–CadQuery pairs and the authors observe clear scaling benefits up to 3B, with potential underfitting for 7B due to limited data.


---

## pqac-00000012

From text 'liao2025automatedcadmodeling pages 7-8'.

Jianxing Liao, Junyan Xu, Yatao Sun, Maowen Tang, Sicheng He, Jingxian Liao, Shui Yu, Yun Li, and Hongguan Xiao. Automated cad modeling sequence generation from text descriptions via transformer-based large language models. ArXiv, May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.19490, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.19490. This article has 8 citations.

The excerpt reports benchmark results for text-to-CAD sequence generation showing large benefits from combining transformer predictors with an LLM-based refinement (CADLLM). Command-level prediction (Table 1) shows TCADGen+CADLLM achieving very high metrics (e.g., row: "TCADGen+CADLLM (Ours)  0.966  0.947  0.962 ..."). For sequence-level generation (Table 2), CADLLM + TCADGen attains accuracy 0.864 and average LCSratio 0.983, outperforming prompt-only and non-LLM combinations. Fine-tuning the Llama 3.2 3B model (SFT) improves performance versus prompt-only (0.621 vs 0.328 accuracy; LCSratio 0.883 vs 0.688). Chamfer Distance (Table 3) is dramatically reduced when adding CADLLM: baseline Transformer methods have CDs of 120.99–169.93, CADFusion 45.67, while LLM-refined combinations yield CD ≈ 3–4 (e.g., "TCADGen+CADLLM(ours)  3.12 (↓117.87)"). MMD and JSD also drop substantially with CADLLM. The excerpt does not explicitly report an "exact match" metric, but reports accuracy, F1, AUC, LCSratio, and geometric distances (CD/MMD/JSD) illustrating that LLM fine-tuning/refinement yields large improvements in both symbolic command accuracy and geometric fidelity.

## pqac-00000013

From text 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 6-8'.

Haoyang Xie and Feng Ju. Text-to-cadquery: a new paradigm for cad generation with scalable large model capabilities. ArXiv, May 2505. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507. This article has 29 citations.

The excerpt reports Text-to-CadQuery evaluation setup and quantitative results for multiple fine-tuned LLMs. Training used token-level cross-entropy loss and a 1024-token context window; Mistral-7B was adapted with LoRA (4-bit quantization, rank r=16, α=32; ∆W = AB low-rank updates). Evaluation metrics are Chamfer Distance (CD), Invalid Rate (IR), and Gemini 2.0 Flash. The baseline Text2CAD Transformer has median CD 0.370 and mean CD 26.417 (×10^3), Gemini 58.80%, IR 3.5. Fine-tuned CadQuery-output models improve substantially: CodeGPT-small (124M, SFT) median CD 0.234, mean 13.520, Gemini 60.28%; Qwen2.5-3B (3B, SFT) achieves the best reported median CD 0.191 and mean 10.229, Gemini 69.30%, IR 6.5. Mistral-7B fine-tuned with LoRA reports median CD 0.219, mean 11.835, IR 1.32, Gemini 65.38%. The authors note CodeGPT-small’s advantage likely stems from Python-code pretraining. The excerpt does not report an "exact match" metric for generated code; geometry-focused CD, execution IR, and perceptual Gemini scores are provided instead.

## pqac-00000014

From text 'alrashedy2024generatingcadcode pages 7-9'.

Kamel Alrashedy, Pradyumna Tambwekar, Zulfiqar Zaidi, Megan Langwasser, Wei Xu, and Matthew Gombolay. Generating cad code with vision-language models for 3d designs. ArXiv, Oct 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2410.05340, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2410.05340. This article has 63 citations.

The paper evaluates CAD-code generation using three geometric metrics: a point-cloud distance defined in Equation 8 (a Chamfer-style bidirectional nearest-neighbor average), Hausdorff distance, and Intersection over Ground Truth (IoGT). Generated meshes are aligned via ICP and normalized to fit in a unit cube; failures to compile are penalized by assigning the maximum point distance (√3) and IoGT = 0. Experiments use zero-shot and few-shot (five demonstrations) protocols across GPT-4, Gemini 1.5 Pro, and CodeLlama. Table 2 reports median Point Cloud (Chamfer-like) distances and success rates: GPT-4 few-shot with CADCodeVerify achieves 0.127 median point-cloud distance and 96.5% success; GPT-4 zero-shot CADCodeVerify 0.132 and 94.0% success. Gemini and CodeLlama show higher point-cloud distances and lower success rates (e.g., Gemini few-shot CADCodeVerify 0.147 / 85.0%; CodeLlama few-shot CADCodeVerify 0.185 / 73.5%). The geometric solver (accessing ground truth) is presented as an upper-bound for refinement. The excerpt does not report explicit "exact match" metrics nor any experiments involving fine-tuning of LLMs for CAD code generation; models were used via API in zero-/few-shot setups.

## pqac-00000015

From text 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 4-6'.

Haoyang Xie and Feng Ju. Text-to-cadquery: a new paradigm for cad generation with scalable large model capabilities. ArXiv, May 2505. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507. This article has 29 citations.

The excerpt details the Text-to-CadQuery data annotation and model fine-tuning pipeline but does not report explicit benchmark metrics such as exact-match rates or Chamfer distance reductions. It describes a semi-automatic annotation pipeline that converted minimal JSON descriptions into CadQuery using Gemini 2.0 Flash, with a self-correction feedback loop that increased code execution success from 53% to 85%. The team annotated 170,000 CadQuery samples; GPT-4o produced >90% executable outputs on first attempt but was costlier (roughly 20× the API cost of Gemini 2.0 Flash). Gemini 2.0 Flash was chosen for annotation because it offered competitive quality, much lower cost, higher throughput (up to 2000 RPM and 4M tokens/min), and lower latency, allowing the full annotation to finish in ~9 hours for $170. For model selection and fine-tuning, Text2CAD Transformer architecture is described (340M encoder + 23M decoder = 363M total), and a range of decoder-only LLMs from 124M to 7B parameters were evaluated. Data splits were 90/5/5, and training used full-parameter SFT for small models and PEFT (LoRA with 4-bit quantization, r=16, α=32) for large models. Training times ranged from under an hour (124M) to ~33 hours (Mistral-7B) on available hardware. No explicit exact-match, Chamfer reduction, or LLM fine-tuning performance numbers are given in this excerpt.

## pqac-00000016

From text 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 3-4'.

Haoyang Xie and Feng Ju. Text-to-cadquery: a new paradigm for cad generation with scalable large model capabilities. ArXiv, May 2505. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507. This article has 29 citations.

The excerpt describes the Text-to-CadQuery work: the authors build on large foundational CAD datasets (DeepCAD: 178,238 command-sequence models; Text2CAD extends DeepCAD with multi-level natural language annotations, producing ~170k models and >600k prompts). They adopt text as the sole input modality and use CadQuery as the high-level output representation. The methodology centers on fine-tuning pretrained large foundation models (varying model scales) to generate executable CadQuery code. The paper claims that larger model capacity improves symbolic 3D generation and that they systematically evaluate models of different scales, with Section 4 covering evaluation metrics and experimental results. Prior symbolic-CAD datasets and baselines noted include Query2CAD (57 examples) and LLM4CAD (~5k CadQuery samples), motivating the need for a larger, diverse dataset. The excerpt, however, does not provide numeric benchmark results (no exact-match rates, Chamfer distance reductions, or concrete fine-tuning performance numbers). For precise values (exact match, Chamfer reduction, quantified effects of LLM fine-tuning), the full paper's results section (Section 4) must be consulted.

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000017

From text 'schupbach2025fromtextto pages 7-8'.

Aurel Schüpbach, Raul San Miguel, Julian Ferchow, and Mirko Meboldt. From text to design: a framework to leverage llm agents for automated cad generation. Proceedings of the Design Society, 5:1893-1902, Aug 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1017/pds.2025.10203, doi:10.1017/pds.2025.10203. This article has 4 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt identifies several limitations and challenges for AI/LLM-driven CAD generation relevant to manufacturability, editability, and parametric control. First, the framework primarily focuses on raw geometry creation and "overlooks key aspects such as manufacturability or the practical application of each geometry," suggesting a lack of built-in design-for-manufacturing (DfM) checks or process-specific constraints (e.g., for AM, injection molding, milling). Second, generated models suffer from accuracy and robustness issues: some LLMs repeatedly fail specific primitives ("failed to generate the right-angle geometry accurately") and less capable models can produce no viable geometries at all. Complex tasks (topology optimization, patterning, mirroring) lower success rates significantly. Third, error detection and iterative correction are limited by the workflow's visual capabilities — "reliance on a single perspective" hampers the LLM's ability to find and fix mistakes; the authors propose "multiple views with spatial annotations" and "visual aids, such as sketches" to improve interpretability. Finally, the paper recommends extending agent workflows with explicit DfM guidelines and validated case studies to improve manufacturability and practical application, implying current outputs may be hard to edit, validate, or control parametrically without further tooling and constraints.


---

## pqac-00000018

From text 'liu2508breplerlanguageguidedediting pages 8-9'.

Yilin Liu, Niladri Shekhar Dutt, Changjian Li, and Niloy J. Mitra. B-repler: language-guided editing of cad models. ArXiv, Aug 2508. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2508.10201, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2508.10201. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt documents several practical limitations of AI/LLM-driven CAD generation and editing. Tokenization/quantization approaches (used by CAD-Editor) produce coordinate precision loss and unstable token predictions, causing misaligned or disconnected components and even split models. Sketch-and-extrude token representations limit scalability to complex primitives and datasets lacking construction history. Modeling B-rep latent spaces is challenging because they are variable-length; padding/unpadding introduces noise that degrades performance. The methods do not explicitly capture or enforce high-level geometric constraints (e.g., symmetry, orthogonality, parallelism), so edits can break design intent and reduce generalization to real-world CAD workflows. Models can also misinterpret textual prompts (notably counting-based instructions). Finally, current approaches are purely text- and geometry-driven and lack physical or functional validation — the model “reasons about geometry only, without validation from a physical simulation,” which limits confidence for functional/manufacturability requirements. Together these issues point to challenges in manufacturability, editability, parametric control, and downstream functional validation of generated CAD models.


---

## pqac-00000019

From text 'noever2512prompttopartsgenerativeai pages 16-18'.

David A. Noever. Prompt-to-parts: generative ai for physical assembly and scalable instructions. ArXiv, Dec 2512. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2512.15743, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2512.15743. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt identifies several limitations and challenges for AI/LLM-driven CAD and generative-3D systems relevant to manufacturability, editability, and parametric control. Many systems optimize visual fidelity over physical constructability, producing models that look plausible but may not be buildable. Earlier algorithmic approaches required a full target geometry as input, preventing open-ended language-driven generation. Mixed manual workflows (e.g., faBrickation) remained non-generative, indicating limited automation. Hierarchical and structure-aware methods improve coherence but often target continuous 3D representations rather than discrete part assemblies, so translating outputs into manufacturable, discrete component inventories is unresolved. More broadly, the field faces brittle spatial reasoning in LLMs, a disconnect between instruction generation and execution verification (so generated CAD may lack actionable, verifiable build instructions), and a shortage of systematic benchmarks for constrained physical generation. The text notes that programmatic intermediate representations (like LDraw) are more reliable than end-to-end approaches, but also highlights that CAD systems operate in continuous parameter spaces—implying difficulties mapping to discrete parts, enforceable tolerances, and parametric editability for manufacturing constraints.


---

## pqac-00000020

From text 'yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 1-2'.

Nomi Yu, Md Ferdous Alam, A. John Hart, and Faez Ahmed. Gencad-3d: cad program generation using multimodal latent space alignment and synthetic dataset balancing. Journal of Mechanical Design, pages 1-17, Jul 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1115/1.4069276, doi:10.1115/1.4069276. This article has 6 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt highlights several limitations and challenges relevant to AI/LLM-driven CAD generation. Many generative models output non-parametric representations ("non-parametric data such as point clouds and meshes") or other free-form shapes that lack CAD’s parametric structure and editability, so such outputs often "require manual adjustments to meet exact design intent," which is especially problematic where strict tolerances and precise fits are needed. Creating and editing CAD programs remains a specialist skill ("manually creating CAD programs is itself a complex skill that typically takes years to master"), and existing reverse-engineering tools still demand substantial expert input, "making the process expensive and time-consuming." A core data limitation is dataset imbalance: "High-complexity CAD programs... are underrepresented, causing models trained on this data to excel on simple shapes but struggle with more intricate ones." Standard evaluation metrics "are dominated by results on simple examples," which can mask failures on complex, high-complexity geometries. Together these factors lead to generated models that can be non-editable, insufficiently parametric for engineering needs, error-prone for complex designs, and often require human post-processing to be manufacturable or fit for downstream use.


---

## pqac-00000021

From text 'schupbach2025fromtextto pages 1-2'.

Aurel Schüpbach, Raul San Miguel, Julian Ferchow, and Mirko Meboldt. From text to design: a framework to leverage llm agents for automated cad generation. Proceedings of the Design Society, 5:1893-1902, Aug 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1017/pds.2025.10203, doi:10.1017/pds.2025.10203. This article has 4 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The paper highlights several limitations of AI/LLM-driven CAD generation relevant to manufacturability, editability, and parametric control. Diffusion- and LLM-based approaches "have limited ability to produce exact CAD models from engineering constraints" and "still struggle to adhere to precise requirements such as dimensional constraints or accurate positions of features," undermining reliable parametric controllability and tight tolerances needed for manufacturability. Generated geometry often "lack[s] typical features like design history or CAD interoperability," which reduces editability, downstream modification, and integration with CAD toolchains. Broader challenges include "model hallucination" and the "limited availability of specialized engineering training data," causing incorrect or fabricated features that can break manufacturability and require human correction. The reliance on text-only I/O and weaknesses in spatial reasoning ("current LLMs primarily rely on text input and output"; "limitations in spatial reasoning") further limit the system's ability to capture 3D constraints and produce robust, editable parametric models. The authors also note "prompt dependency" and "workflow adaptability" as constraints, and recommend future work on improving "design-for-manufacturing capabilities, visual tools, and evaluation benchmarking."


---

## pqac-00000022

From text 'liao2025automatedcadmodeling pages 1-2'.

Jianxing Liao, Junyan Xu, Yatao Sun, Maowen Tang, Sicheng He, Jingxian Liao, Shui Yu, Yun Li, and Hongguan Xiao. Automated cad modeling sequence generation from text descriptions via transformer-based large language models. ArXiv, May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.19490, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.19490. This article has 8 citations.

The excerpt identifies several limitations and challenges of AI/LLM-driven CAD generation relevant to manufacturability, editability, and parametric control. Generated design data often lacks sufficient accuracy, making conversion into high-quality, editable CAD models difficult—especially where industrial precision is required. LLMs are computationally expensive and inefficient, and designers find it hard to guide them with simple text or visual prompts toward reasonable, manufacturable designs. Prior methods focus on data synthesis rather than producing robust modeling histories or parametric control; available datasets often lack modeling-process information and natural-language annotations needed for editable, parametric models. Specific model behaviors can harm editability and parametric controllability: for example, DeepCAD generates sketches before extrusion parameters, producing disconnected sequences and only supporting basic operations. The paper suggests hybrid approaches—using finely tuned small generative models to provide accurate task-level guidance combined with large models—to mitigate reasoning uncertainty, but overall challenges remain in ensuring manufacturability, maintaining parametric editability, and producing precise, connected modeling sequences that are practical for industrial workflows.


---

## pqac-00000023

From text 'heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 21-22'.

Negar Heidari and Alexandros Iosifidis. Geometric deep learning for computer-aided design: a survey. IEEE Access, 13:119305-119334, Feb 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2402.17695, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2402.17695. This article has 47 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt highlights multiple practical limitations and challenges for AI-driven CAD generation that affect manufacturability, editability and parametric control. Methods that infer parametric primitives (e.g., extrusion parameters) or reconstruct CAD by searching constrained-sketch repositories can yield parametric CAD outputs, but their robustness is limited: point-cloud-based approaches struggle with noisy or distorted inputs, fail to recover sharp edges and only provide approximate 3D representations. While some approaches produce structured B-Rep outputs, broader limitations come from data: there is a shortage of large, diverse, well-annotated B-Rep datasets (many datasets exist only as meshes), annotation of mechanical models requires domain expertise, and surface/face classes are inconsistent across datasets, hindering transfer learning. Assembly-level information (joints, hierarchical subassemblies) is often not annotated in B-Rep data, making edit operations like replacing parts while preserving connections and functional/material considerations difficult. Together, these factors reduce confidence in manufacturability checks, limit fine-grained parametric controllability, and make subsequent editable, semantically-correct CAD model refinement and assembly-aware edits challenging.


---

## pqac-00000024

From text 'yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 14-15'.

Nomi Yu, Md Ferdous Alam, A. John Hart, and Faez Ahmed. Gencad-3d: cad program generation using multimodal latent space alignment and synthetic dataset balancing. Journal of Mechanical Design, pages 1-17, Jul 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1115/1.4069276, doi:10.1115/1.4069276. This article has 6 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The paper notes several limitations relevant to AI/LLM-driven CAD generation. First, synthetic augmentation (SynthBal) improves performance on existing benchmarks but "synthetic data may not fully capture the complexities inherent in industrial designs, potentially limiting generalization." Second, the training/evaluation data are narrow: "the current DeepCAD dataset only contains Sketch-and-Extrude CAD programs, a small subset of engineering geometries," which restricts exposure to commands, feature types, and real-world part variability. Third, practical parametric and editable CAD requires handling of more diverse CAD vocabularies and specialized program constructs: "it is important to collect and experiment on datasets with larger CAD vocabularies, and perhaps develop architectures better suited to handling special commands such as those that require references to compiled B-Rep geometries." Overall, the authors emphasize that despite performance gains, "certain limitations remain," pointing to dataset realism, limited CAD program types, and the need for architectures that support B-Rep references and richer CAD operations for better generalization, parametric controllability, and practical editability.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000025

From text 'yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 10-11'.

Nomi Yu, Md Ferdous Alam, A. John Hart, and Faez Ahmed. Gencad-3d: cad program generation using multimodal latent space alignment and synthetic dataset balancing. Journal of Mechanical Design, pages 1-17, Jul 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1115/1.4069276, doi:10.1115/1.4069276. This article has 6 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

Table 4 ("Quality and Accuracy of Multimodal Generative Models") includes a row for SkexGen [25] labeled as "Uncond." Many columns for SkexGen are marked "-" ("-" indicates that the metric is not relevant), meaning most of the table's metrics were not reported for SkexGen. The table header shows the last three reported columns as "cmd (↑) 𝜇SLparam (↑) 𝑀SLCD" and the SkexGen row lists values only for these three: 83.6, 1.48, and 0.81 respectively. In other words, within this table SkexGen's entry provides only the three metrics shown in the header (cmd = 83.6, 𝜇SLparam = 1.48, 𝑀SLCD = 0.81) while leaving the other listed metrics unreported for that model.


---

## pqac-00000026

From text 'xu2023hierarchicalneuralcoding pages 6-7'.

Xiang Xu, Pradeep Kumar Jayaraman, Joseph G. Lambourne, Karl D. D. Willis, and Yasutaka Furukawa. Hierarchical neural coding for controllable cad model generation. ArXiv, Jun 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2307.00149, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2307.00149. This article has 117 citations.

The excerpt reports benchmark results comparing SkexGen on unconditional sketch-and-extrude CAD generation. Table 1 gives SkexGen: Coverage (COV) = 84.74%, Minimum Matching Distance (MMD) = 1.02, Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD) = 0.90, Novel = 99.1%, Unique = 99.8%, and human-perceived Realism = 46.9. The paper defines the evaluation metrics: point-cloud metrics (COV, MMD, JSD) assessing diversity/quality of generated shapes, token metrics for sequences (Novel and Unique), and a human evaluation for realism. The excerpt does not provide separate, explicit metric values labeled specifically for a “disentangled latent space” nor distinct quantitative breakdowns separately for sketch vs. extrusion quality—only a pointer that sketch generation results are available in Appendix C. Thus, from this excerpt the reported metrics for SkexGen are the point-cloud (COV/MMD/JSD), token (Novel/Unique) scores, and human realism score as listed.


---

## pqac-00000027

From text 'xu2023hierarchicalneuralcoding pages 13-20'.

Xiang Xu, Pradeep Kumar Jayaraman, Joseph G. Lambourne, Karl D. D. Willis, and Yasutaka Furukawa. Hierarchical neural coding for controllable cad model generation. ArXiv, Jun 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2307.00149, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2307.00149. This article has 117 citations.

The excerpt reports unconditional sketch-generation benchmarks where SkexGen achieves FID=18.56, Unique=96.02%, and Novel=83.54% (Table 2). The paper computes Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) using features from a ResNet-18 pre-trained on human sketch classification, following SkexGen’s evaluation protocol. In addition to automated sketch metrics (FID, uniqueness, novelty), the paper describes a human two-alternative forced-choice evaluation of realism (AMT), run on 950 image pairs per generative method with 7 workers per pair (19,950 annotations total). For disentangled latent/code evaluation, the excerpt presents qualitative code-to-data mappings showing that "data assigned to the same code also have similar design patterns," indicating qualitative clustering of profile/loop geometry, but no numerical disentanglement metric is reported in these pages. The excerpt does not provide explicit benchmark numbers for SkexGen on full autoregressive CAD construction-sequence generation or on extrusion-generation quality; only sketch-generation metrics and qualitative code-mapping observations are present.

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000028

From text 'peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 26-28'.

Owen Peckham, Jonathan Raines, Erik Bulsink, Mark Goudswaard, James Gopsill, David Barton, Aydin Nassehi, and Ben Hicks. Artificial intelligence in generative design: a structured review of trends and opportunities in techniques and applications. Designs, 9:79, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/designs9040079, doi:10.3390/designs9040079. This article has 26 citations.

The review of 88 articles reports two main roles for AI in generative design (GD): surrogate models to speed up optimisation workflows, and direct design generation. There has been rapid recent growth (72.7% of papers since 2021) and a shift from classic MLPs (still most common at 34%) toward modern generative architectures such as GANs (23.4%) and CNNs (19.1%) over the last five years — indicating increasing use of GANs for direct generation tasks. Surrogate models offer acceleration benefits but face substantial limitations: high data generation and training costs (reported in 56.8% of articles), and model accuracy concerns (30%), requiring trade-offs between training expense and performance. Identified opportunities include training foundation surrogate models that generalise across scenarios to reduce cost and improve accuracy, developing GenAI systems that synthesize using structured engineering data, exploring unsupervised methods to capture physical meaning in designs, and improving manufacturability of GD outcomes. The excerpt does not discuss diffusion models or genetic algorithms, and notes the review only covers academic literature (limiting insight into proprietary industrial tools such as Autodesk’s Dreamcatcher).


---

## pqac-00000029

From text 'peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 1-3'.

Owen Peckham, Jonathan Raines, Erik Bulsink, Mark Goudswaard, James Gopsill, David Barton, Aydin Nassehi, and Ben Hicks. Artificial intelligence in generative design: a structured review of trends and opportunities in techniques and applications. Designs, 9:79, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/designs9040079, doi:10.3390/designs9040079. This article has 26 citations.

The review reports that Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) are being used for direct design generation in engineering CAD/GD workflows, while genetic algorithms remain widely used and surrogate models (e.g., Convolutional Neural Networks and Multilayer Perceptrons) are employed to manage the high computational cost of simulation-heavy design tasks. Structural and aerodynamic problems are the dominant application areas. Identified benefits include improved computational efficiency, greater design diversity, and substantial performance gains in light-weighting studies (>40–60% mass savings versus expert designs). Limitations and barriers highlighted are data-generation costs, model accuracy, and interpretability; manufacturability constraints are noted as an under-addressed area. The review points to opportunities to develop generalisable foundation surrogate models and to integrate emerging generative methods such as diffusion models and large language models into generative design pipelines.


---

## pqac-00000030

From text 'peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 20-21'.

Owen Peckham, Jonathan Raines, Erik Bulsink, Mark Goudswaard, James Gopsill, David Barton, Aydin Nassehi, and Ben Hicks. Artificial intelligence in generative design: a structured review of trends and opportunities in techniques and applications. Designs, 9:79, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/designs9040079, doi:10.3390/designs9040079. This article has 26 citations.

The excerpt reports several survey findings relevant to generative design techniques. Conditional GANs have been used to incorporate physical/design parameters (e.g., initial stress fields, angle of attack, Mach number) into generation, helping ensure more physically informed outputs. However, GANs are being superseded in image domains by diffusion models; recent work applies diffusion models to topology optimisation (TO) and even to non-pixel representations—Xu et al. used diffusion to predict hierarchical tree node properties to generate B-rep CAD models. Surrogate modelling is discussed as applicable to multi-physics structural design, with accuracy as the primary concern. Broad limitations across AI/ML methods include that models learn “what” rather than “why,” trained designs lack context and physical derivation, and models typically output a single solution whereas engineering often requires Pareto fronts for multi-objective trade-offs. LLMs are notably under-represented in the corpus, possibly due to sequence representation and numeric-precision challenges. Explainability appears rarely addressed despite its importance for generated-design rationale, particularly in safety-critical domains. The excerpt does not explicitly discuss genetic algorithms.


---

## pqac-00000031

From text 'peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 24-26'.

Owen Peckham, Jonathan Raines, Erik Bulsink, Mark Goudswaard, James Gopsill, David Barton, Aydin Nassehi, and Ben Hicks. Artificial intelligence in generative design: a structured review of trends and opportunities in techniques and applications. Designs, 9:79, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/designs9040079, doi:10.3390/designs9040079. This article has 26 citations.

The review notes a trend toward greater maturity and reliability of generative models and predicts diffusion models will likely be adopted in engineering generative design. However, a core limitation is representational mismatch: many modern tools (GANs, diffusion models, transformers) are built for images/voxels or sequences, formats that "are not commonly used to represent designs in industry," creating a need for methods to translate "neural/pixel-based representations and engineering-specific formats" or to develop diffusion models tailored to engineering data. Neural-guided search is highlighted as promising for addressing combinatorial complexity when adapting diffusion models and transformers to structures. Sequence-based models and LLMs can be applied via a "program-centric" route—having LLMs "generate code that describes a parametric design"—and fine-tuning (including RL) could help models learn the "why as well as the what," but integration with simulation or CAM tools is likely required. Genetic algorithms are referenced historically (e.g., GA used to search for proofs). Practical limitations include the "real-world feasibility" of GD outputs and the difficulty of applying manufacturing constraints; the paper suggests surrogate-style AI tools ("MLPs") could act as manufacturability checkers integrated into late-stage GD optimisation to improve producibility.


---

## pqac-00000032

From text 'peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 23-24'.

Owen Peckham, Jonathan Raines, Erik Bulsink, Mark Goudswaard, James Gopsill, David Barton, Aydin Nassehi, and Ben Hicks. Artificial intelligence in generative design: a structured review of trends and opportunities in techniques and applications. Designs, 9:79, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/designs9040079, doi:10.3390/designs9040079. This article has 26 citations.

The reviewed excerpt reports that GANs are the most common architecture for direct design generation (9/14 studies), motivated by a desire for greater design variety and originally applied to aesthetics. GAN outputs are generally used as starting points for downstream processing rather than final geometries. More recently, GANs have been applied to physics-informed designs by representing properties (pressure, stress, strain) as images, and have expanded into constrained domains (structural and aerodynamic) since 2021. Image-based GAN outputs suffer from pixelation, aliasing and fuzzy edges; remedies include post-processing or switching to alternate geometric representations (e.g., vectors of 2D points, tensor representations) to produce smooth, parametric-compatible geometry. Constraint handling via a second discriminator can reward physical plausibility but may introduce competing loss signals that hurt other design qualities. For surrogate models, CNNs dominate across many engineering fields; MLPs and VAEs are used where appropriate (e.g., MLPs for approximating costly CFD). PINNs can embed PDEs to avoid labelled data but can be far more computationally expensive (reported 34×). The review emphasizes the critical role of training-data quality and coverage, recommending DOE methods such as Latin hypercube sampling and the D-optimal method. The excerpt does not discuss diffusion models or genetic algorithms.


---

## pqac-00000033

From text 'peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 21-23'.

Owen Peckham, Jonathan Raines, Erik Bulsink, Mark Goudswaard, James Gopsill, David Barton, Aydin Nassehi, and Ben Hicks. Artificial intelligence in generative design: a structured review of trends and opportunities in techniques and applications. Designs, 9:79, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/designs9040079, doi:10.3390/designs9040079. This article has 26 citations.

The surveyed corpus identifies two common technique–function pairings: genetic algorithms (GAs) paired with surrogate models, and GANs used for design generation. Surrogate models are valued because they can be orders of magnitude faster than traditional solvers, reducing the computational burden of GA fitness evaluations. However, researchers report notable limitations: reduced surrogate accuracy (reported in 9 of 28 studies), the cost of generating training data (often needing runs of traditional solvers), and training costs (20 of 28 studies mentioned this), making a complex trade-off over whether to invest in surrogate training. To address generalisability and fidelity, some studies use ML to accelerate finite element methods (performing FEM at lower resolution and upscaling) or embed physics via PINNs (incorporating PDEs into the loss) to improve results. GANs are cited as a primary tool for generating design variants, though the excerpt does not detail specific GAN benefits or failure modes beyond their role in generation. Explainability and confidence are under-addressed: only one paper covered explainable AI, and current techniques lack measures of prediction confidence. The excerpt does not mention diffusion models.

From text 'peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 32-33'.

Owen Peckham, Jonathan Raines, Erik Bulsink, Mark Goudswaard, James Gopsill, David Barton, Aydin Nassehi, and Ben Hicks.
