The provided pages are the reference list from a 2025 survey on AI in generative design. They cite foundational and recent works across GANs, diffusion models, genetic algorithms, and surrogate models applied to CAD and engineering design. Examples include Wasserstein GAN and application papers such as ShipHullGAN (parametric ship-hull modelling) and several diffusion-model works (e.g., Latent Diffusion Models for Structural Component Design; BrepGen for B-rep geometry). Classic evolutionary approaches are represented by a genetic-algorithms overview, and acceleration/approximation approaches appear via multi-stage DNN-accelerated topology optimization and deep-learning-based surrogate models for fast multi-material topology optimization. There are also citations for CAD-focused generative approaches (CAD-LLM) and other generative methods for analog IC topology and airfoil inverse design. The excerpt does not contain the survey’s narrative findings, explicit benefits, or limitations; however, the cited titles imply research trends: GANs and diffusion models for generative geometry, genetic algorithms for global/combinatorial search, and surrogate/DNN models to speed optimization. Direct statements about advantages (e.g., quality, fidelity, speed) or limitations (e.g., mode collapse, computation cost, generalization) are not present on these pages.

## pqac-00000035

From text 'souvanhnakhoomman2025acomprehensivereview pages 2-3'.

Sane Souvanhnakhoomman and Alvin Chua. A comprehensive review of generative design applications in unmanned aerial vehicles. ASEAN Engineering Journal, 15:163-175, Feb 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.11113/aej.v15.21286, doi:10.11113/aej.v15.21286. This article has 4 citations.

The excerpt reports survey findings that highlight the integration of AI into generative design for CAD/engineering. Genetic algorithms (GAs) have been applied in industrial case studies to evolve parametric CAD models, often combined with rigid-body simulation and finite element analysis to meet engineering requirements. Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) have been paired with topology optimization to generate multiple design variants (example: wheel rims) that satisfy both engineering and aesthetic criteria; GANs were also used as a filter under the assumption that existing designs are more aesthetically acceptable than arbitrary topology-optimization outputs. The field has moved from seeking a single “perfect” design toward producing a set of diverse alternatives. Benefits noted include practical industrial applicability, the ability to generate multiple viable solutions, and tighter integration of AI with topology optimization and CAD tools. Limitations and caveats in the excerpt include reliance on aesthetic assumptions for filtering, the lack of a universally agreed definition of generative design, and earlier limited practical implementations. The excerpt does not discuss diffusion models or surrogate models, so no findings about those methods are reported here.

### Artifact (ID artifact-01)

| Tool Name | Vendor | Core Method (TO/GD/both) | Key Features | Manufacturing Constraints Support | Output Format | Notable Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Autodesk Fusion Generative Design | Autodesk | Both | Generates 3D part forms from geometric + force parameters; explores many alternatives; supports simulation-based evaluation and filtering of outcomes; widely used for lightweighting and AM-oriented redesign (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000003) | Strong relative to mainstream CAD GD tools; users can apply manufacturing constraints to bias solutions toward manufacturable outputs, especially AM processes (pqac-00000003) | Native Fusion geometry; exportable CAD/B-rep workflows | Outputs often skew toward AM-style organic forms; many alternatives still require manual interpretation and downstream styling; engineer-centric workflow (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003) |
| nTopology / nTop | nTop | Both | Field-driven implicit modeling; strong lattice generation/manipulation; integrates FEA-informed lattice workflows; well suited to AM and performance-driven geometry (pqac-00000002) | Strong for AM/DFAM, especially lattice and powder-bed workflows (pqac-00000002) | Implicit/mesh/B-rep export depending workflow | Best fit is advanced AM rather than conventional subtractive manufacturing; requires expertise to convert performance forms into broader production-ready product geometry (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002) |
| Siemens NX Topology Optimizer (+ Simcenter HEEDS) | Siemens | Both | NX geometry + Simcenter/HEEDS can automatically generate and compare hundreds to thousands of optimized options; integrates CAD and CAE; targeted at high-end engineering workflows and AM-ready exploration (pqac-00000000) | Good support through NX/Simcenter manufacturing-aware setup and AM-targeted workflows (pqac-00000000) | NX CAD geometry / CAE-linked optimized models | Higher workflow complexity and enterprise cost; still subject to classic TO sensitivity to loads/boundary conditions and post-processing burden for manufacturable CAD (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001) |
| PTC Creo Generative Design | PTC | Both | Integrated parametric CAD + simulation-driven concept generation; intended to produce editable Creo-native concepts and support engineering redesign | Moderate-to-strong in principle via process constraints, preserved geometry, loads, materials, and manufacturing method setup | Creo-native parametric geometry / exportable CAD | Fewer independently reported benchmark-style public results than Autodesk/Altair; manufacturability and editability depend heavily on problem setup and post-processing; organic outputs still need engineering cleanup (pqac-00000001) |
| ANSYS Discovery | ANSYS | Both | Real-time simulation-guided design exploration; topology optimization linked to fast physics feedback; strong CAE-centric concept screening | Good for simulation-constrained design; manufacturability can be introduced through setup constraints, but typically less design-history-centric than full parametric CAD | Simulation-derived geometry / CAD handoff | More analysis-centric than end-to-end editable CAD authoring; translating optimized shapes into robust production CAD can require extra remodeling (pqac-00000001) |
| Altair Inspire (OptiStruct) | Altair | Both | Early and influential commercial TO platform; removes material from a 3D CAD model to meet goals such as maximum stiffness with minimum mass; strong concept-lightweighting workflow (pqac-00000003) | Moderate; supports engineering constraints and process-aware setup, but manufacturable finalization often needs redesign/refinement (pqac-00000003) | Optimized concept geometry / export to downstream CAD | TO outputs can be hard to convert directly into final parametric CAD; workflows are predominantly engineer-focused; raw results may require significant remodeling and styling (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000003) |
| Rhino + Grasshopper | Robert McNeel + Associates | GD-oriented (with TO via plugins) | Flexible visual parametric/algorithmic modeling; strong for custom rule-based exploration, geometry scripting, and integration with plugins such as Kangaroo, Karamba3D, Millipede, Wallacei, and AM workflows | Variable; can encode manufacturing rules and custom constraints, but depends on user-authored definitions/plugins rather than turnkey solver workflows | NURBS/mesh/parametric Grasshopper definitions; broad export support | Powerful but less turnkey for certified engineering optimization; requires substantial user expertise, plugin selection, and manual validation; manufacturability and performance assurance are not automatic (pqac-00000001) |


*Table: This table compares major commercial topology-optimization and generative-design platforms on method, workflow strengths, manufacturing support, outputs, and practical limitations. It is useful for distinguishing simulation-centric optimization tools from more editable parametric or algorithmic design environments.*

### Artifact (ID artifact-02)

| Framework | Language | License/Type | Kernel | GUI Required? | Python Ecosystem Integration | CI/CD Suitability | Key Strengths | Key Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CadQuery | Python | Open-source library | OpenCascade (via OCCT/OCP) | No | Excellent; strong Jupyter and scripting workflow, with no separate CAD software required (pqac-00000008) | High | Parametric, programmable solid modeling; readable Python API; good for automation, testing, batch generation, and export pipelines; strong fit for reproducible engineering code (pqac-00000008) | Less interactive than mainstream GUI CAD; advanced surfacing/assemblies can require more coding expertise; manufacturability rules are not automatic |
| OpenSCAD | OpenSCAD DSL | Open-source application/language | CGAL-based CSG pipeline | Typically yes for normal use; headless CLI possible | None/very limited; not Python-native and requires separate software (pqac-00000008) | Medium | Extremely deterministic and reproducible; simple declarative CSG; excellent for scripted fixtures, enclosures, and parameterized hobbyist/mechanical parts | Weaker ecosystem interoperability; limited direct Python integration; less suited to complex fillets/freeform industrial geometry; editability is code-centric rather than feature-tree-centric (pqac-00000008) |
| build123d | Python | Open-source library | OpenCascade (OCCT) | No | Excellent | High | Modern Pythonic parametric CAD; close to CadQuery but with explicit algebra/mode-building APIs; strong type hints and testability; good for CI/CD and reusable CAD components | Smaller ecosystem/community than older tools; still code-first, so discoverability and interactive editing are weaker than GUI CAD |
| Onshape FeatureScript | FeatureScript | Proprietary cloud CAD language/platform | Onshape Parasolid-based cloud kernel | Yes, via Onshape cloud environment | Limited/indirect; accessible through APIs rather than native Python modeling | Medium-High | Native extension mechanism inside a production CAD system; robust parametric features; strong collaboration, versioning, and enterprise governance; good for custom feature authoring in real engineering workflows | Cloud-only and vendor-tied; proprietary language/platform; less suitable for offline/local builds; CI/CD depends on SaaS/API workflows rather than pure local execution |
| JSCAD | JavaScript | Open-source library/toolchain | CSG/B-rep-style JS geometry engine | No | Limited direct Python integration; strong JavaScript/Node ecosystem instead | High for JS-centric teams | Browser- and Node-friendly; easy web deployment; good for lightweight parametric CSG and custom configurators; accessible for full-stack developers | Less mature for heavy mechanical CAD than OCCT-based tools; complex fillets/shells/industrial geometry can be harder; manufacturability and engineering semantics are mostly user-enforced |

function.arguments:
{"answer": "# State-of-the-Art Landscape of Generative CAD and Generative Design Tools and Research (2025)

## 1. Introduction

The convergence of topology optimization (TO), generative design (GD), parametric code-based CAD, and AI/LLM-driven generation is reshaping how engineers and designers create 3D models. This landscape review covers four major pillars: (a) commercial TO/GD products, (b) parametric/code-based CAD frameworks, (c) AI/LLM-driven CAD generation research, and (d) cross-cutting constraints and limitations. A structured review of over 14,000 publications found that 72.7% of relevant AI-in-GD papers appeared after 2021, reflecting rapid growth driven by advances in generative AI and computational resources (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029).

---

## 2. Commercial Topology-Optimization and Generative-Design Products

Commercial GD and TO tools encode physical rules and optimization algorithms to generate many candidate forms from user-specified constraints, helping avoid designer fixation and producing multiple design embodiments rapidly (pqac-00000001). The following table summarizes the major platforms:

<artifact id="artifact-01"/>

**Autodesk Fusion Generative Design** generates 3D part forms from geometric and force parameters, exploring many alternatives that users can visualize, simulate, and filter. In case studies it achieved significant mass reductions (e.g., 705 g to 586 g on drone components). Users can apply manufacturing constraints to bias solutions toward AM or subtractive processes (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000003).

**nTopology (nTop)** employs field-driven implicit modeling and FEA-informed lattice structures, particularly suited to powder-based AM workflows. It can generate and manipulate lattice structures driven by simulation data, providing strong DFAM (Design for Additive Manufacturing) support (pqac-00000002).

**Siemens NX + Simcenter HEEDS** automatically generates and compares hundreds to thousands of optimized options, integrating NX CAD geometry with Simcenter CAE for solutions targeted at additive manufacturing. The combined workflow enables large-scale design-space exploration but carries higher enterprise workflow complexity (pqac-00000000).

**Altair Inspire (OptiStruct)** is among the earliest commercial TO platforms. It removes material from a 3D CAD model to meet goals such as maximum stiffness with minimum mass. Practical challenges include translating voxelized TO outputs into manufacturable parametric CAD (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000003).

**PTC Creo Generative Design** and **ANSYS Discovery** offer integrated simulation-driven concept generation, with PTC targeting Creo-native parametric geometry and ANSYS providing real-time physics feedback. Both face the common limitation that optimized shapes often require significant remodeling for production readiness (pqac-00000001).

**Rhino + Grasshopper** provides a flexible visual parametric/algorithmic modeling environment with plugins for structural analysis (Karamba3D, Kangaroo) and evolutionary optimization (Wallacei, Galapagos). While extremely versatile, it requires substantial user expertise and does not provide turnkey certified engineering optimization (pqac-00000001).

Across all commercial TO/GD tools, common limitations include sensitivity to input definitions (loads/boundaries), risk of converging to local optima, high computational cost for high-resolution problems, and difficulty translating voxel-based density fields into manufacturable parametric CAD (B-splines, contours) (pqac-00000001). Additive manufacturing remains best suited to small-batch or niche production, which limits practical manufacturing options for some GD/TO outputs (pqac-00000003). A structured review found that light-weighting studies using GD/TO report >40–60% mass savings versus expert designs (pqac-00000029).

---

## 3. Parametric / Code-Based CAD Frameworks

Code-based CAD frameworks enable programmatic, reproducible, and version-controllable 3D modeling, making them particularly valuable for CI/CD pipelines and automated design workflows. The following table compares the major frameworks:

<artifact id="artifact-02"/>

**CadQuery** is a Python library for parametric 3D modeling built on the OpenCascade (OCCT) kernel. It requires no separate software, uses pure Python, has low modeling difficulty, and offers "excellent" integration with the Python ecosystem (Jupyter, scripting). Its headless operation makes it highly suited for CI/CD pipelines and automated batch geometry generation (pqac-00000008). Recent work on Text-to-CadQuery has demonstrated that CadQuery provides the best trade-off between usability and flexibility for programmatic shape synthesis, and is "well-suited for programmatic shape synthesis" (pqac-00000008).

**OpenSCAD** uses its own domain-specific language (DSL) with a CGAL-based CSG pipeline. It requires separate software for execution, has medium modeling difficulty, and shows no direct Python ecosystem integration (pqac-00000008). While deterministic and reproducible, its limited interoperability constrains its use in modern Python-centric automation workflows.

**build123d** is a modern Python library also built on the OCCT kernel. It serves as a spiritual successor to CadQuery with explicit algebra/mode-building APIs and strong type hints, making it well-suited for testable, CI/CD-integrated CAD generation.

**Onshape FeatureScript** is a proprietary language embedded within the Onshape cloud CAD platform. It enables custom feature authoring within a production-grade parametric system with robust collaboration and versioning. However, it is cloud-only and vendor-tied, limiting offline/local CI/CD workflows.

**JSCAD** provides JavaScript-based parametric CSG modeling that runs in browsers and Node.js environments, making it accessible for web-based configurators and full-stack teams. It is less mature for heavy mechanical CAD than OCCT-based tools.

Key limitations across code-based frameworks include: (i) manufacturability rules are not automatically enforced—users must encode process constraints manually; (ii) interactive visual editing is weaker compared to GUI CAD systems; and (iii) complex freeform/NURBS surfacing can require substantial coding expertise.

---

## 4. AI/LLM-Driven CAD Generation Research

The past three years have seen an explosion of research applying deep learning, LLMs, and diffusion models to automated CAD generation. The following table summarizes benchmark results for representative methods:

<artifact id="artifact-00"/>

### 4.1 Foundational Generative CAD Models

**DeepCAD** (ICCV 2021) pioneered autoregressive deep generative networks for sketch-and-extrude CAD program synthesis. On unconditional generation benchmarks, DeepCAD achieved Coverage (COV) = 65.46%, MMD = 1.29, JSD = 1.67, with 87.4% novel and 89.3% unique outputs, but only 46.1% of generated models were valid sealed B-rep solids. In autoencoder reconstruction, it reports command accuracy (ACCcmd) = 99.36 and parameter accuracy (ACCparam) = 97.47 with a median Chamfer Distance (CD) of 0.787 and an invalid ratio of 3.30% (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006).

**SkexGen** (2022) introduced autoregressive generation of CAD construction sequences with a disentangled latent space separating sketch and extrusion properties. It substantially improved upon DeepCAD with COV = 84.74%, MMD = 1.02, JSD = 0.90, 99.1% novel and 99.8% unique outputs, and a human-perceived realism score of 46.9. For sketch generation, it achieved FID = 18.56 (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027).

**HNC-CAD** (2023) extended controllable generation via hierarchical neural coding with VQ-VAE and cascaded autoregressive transformers, achieving CD = 1.09 (versus DeepCAD's 4.25) and an invalid ratio of only 0.32 in reconstruction benchmarks, enabling novel interaction capabilities for conditional generation and completion tasks (pqac-00000005).

**BrepGen** (CVPR 2024) addressed direct B-rep generation with COV = 71.26%, MMD = 1.04, JSD = 0.09, and 62.9% valid outputs—substantially improving validity over DeepCAD while maintaining 99.8% novelty and 99.7% uniqueness (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000006). More recent work by **BrepGPT** (2025) introduced a single-stage autoregressive framework using Voronoi Half-Patch representation for unified B-rep generation.

### 4.2 Text-to-CAD and LLM-Driven Generation

**Text-to-CadQuery** (2025) proposed generating executable CadQuery Python code directly from natural language, bypassing intermediate command sequences. The best fine-tuned model (Qwen2.5-3B) achieved 69.3% accuracy (up from 58.8% baseline), reduced Chamfer Distance by 48.6%, and attained a volumetric IoU median of 0.9868 and F1 median of 0.9836. The work demonstrated clear scaling benefits: even the smallest model (124M parameters) reached higher accuracy with only 58 minutes of training compared to 2 days for the Text2CAD baseline. A GPT-4.1 mini fine-tuned on 10k examples produced 0% invalid rate but did not exceed accuracy of the 3B model, highlighting that larger LLMs improve executability but require sufficient task-specific data to improve 3D generation accuracy (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000013).

**TCADGen + CADLLM** (2025) combined a Transformer-based CAD sequence generator with LLM-based refinement (Llama 3.2 3B), achieving a sequence-level accuracy of 0.864 and dramatically reducing Chamfer Distance from 120.99–169.93 (baseline transformers) down to approximately 3.12, representing orders-of-magnitude geometric improvement (pqac-00000012).

**CADCodeVerify** (2025) introduced iterative visual verification for LLM-generated CAD code. GPT-4 with few-shot prompting and CADCodeVerify achieved a median point-cloud distance of 0.127 and 96.5% success rate, outperforming Gemini 1.5 Pro (0.147, 85.0%) and CodeLlama (0.185, 73.5%) (pqac-00000014).

**Seek-CAD** (2025) pioneered the use of locally deployed open-source inference LLMs (DeepSeek-R1) for training-free CAD parametric model generation with visual and chain-of-thought feedback in a self-refinement loop.

**CAD-Coder** (2025) fine-tuned DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B to generate CAD script codes with geometric annotations, achieving CD = 0.88 with comments versus 1.31 without, demonstrating the importance of annotation quality for LLM-based CAD generation (pqac-00000010).

### 4.3 Multimodal and Diffusion-Based Approaches

Diffusion models are increasingly applied to CAD generation. **RECAD** (AAAI 2025) employs dual diffusion networks generating raster sketches and extrusion boxes, offering advantages in continuous latent space interpolation and intuitive user control. **GenCAD-3D** (2025) uses multimodal latent space alignment with contrastive learning and latent diffusion models, augmented by synthetic dataset balancing (SynthBal), to improve reconstruction accuracy on high-complexity geometries (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000024).

A structured review identified GANs as the most common architecture for direct design generation (9/14 studies in a systematic sample), with conditional GANs incorporating physical parameters (stress fields, aerodynamic conditions) to produce more physically informed outputs. However, GAN outputs suffer from pixelation, aliasing, and fuzzy edges, and are generally used as starting points for downstream processing rather than final geometries (pqac-00000030, pqac-00000032). Diffusion models are predicted to be adopted in engineering GD as they mature, though a representational mismatch persists between neural pixel/voxel formats and engineering-standard CAD formats (pqac-00000031).

### 4.4 Agentic CAD Pipelines

A framework for automating CAD generation using LLM agents with function calling and agent workflows evaluated five LLMs across four agent workflows. The agent workflow incorporating automated visual feedback outperformed others, especially with multimodal LLMs like ChatGPT-4o. A case study demonstrated its application to topology optimization and additive manufacturing with minimal human input (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000021).

---

## 5. Constraints and Limitations

### 5.1 Manufacturability

Across all approaches, manufacturability remains a critical under-addressed challenge. Commercial TO/GD tools can encode manufacturing constraints (e.g., AM build directions, minimum feature sizes), but outputs typically require extensive post-processing before they are production-ready (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000003). AI-generated CAD models fundamentally "overlook key aspects such as manufacturability or the practical application of each geometry," with frameworks that focus on raw geometry creation lacking built-in DfM checks (pqac-00000017). Current AI approaches "reason about geometry only, without validation from a physical simulation," limiting confidence for functional and manufacturing requirements (pqac-00000018). Research suggests integrating AI-based manufacturability checkers (e.g., surrogate models acting as MLP-based DfM filters) into late-stage optimization to improve producibility (pqac-00000031).

### 5.2 Editability and Parametric Controllability

A fundamental tension exists between AI-generated outputs and traditional parametric CAD workflows. Many generative models produce non-parametric representations (point clouds, meshes) that "require manual adjustments to meet exact design intent" and lack design history or CAD interoperability features essential for downstream modification (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021). Tokenization and quantization approaches used in sequence-based CAD generation produce coordinate precision loss and unstable predictions, causing misaligned or disconnected components (pqac-00000018). Current methods do not explicitly capture high-level geometric constraints such as symmetry, orthogonality, or parallelism, so edits can break design intent (pqac-00000018). The code-generation paradigm (e.g., Text-to-CadQuery) partially addresses editability by outputting executable parametric scripts, but this approach is currently limited to the sketch-and-extrude subset of CAD operations (pqac-00000024).

### 5.3 Scriptability and CI/CD Integration

Code-based frameworks like CadQuery and build123d offer the strongest CI/CD integration due to their Python-native, headless operation and excellent scripting ecosystem support (pqac-00000008). OpenSCAD provides deterministic reproducibility but limited Python integration. Commercial TO/GD tools are largely GUI-bound with API access varying by vendor. The emerging Text-to-CadQuery paradigm bridges AI generation with CI/CD-friendly code output, leveraging the fact that LLMs already excel at Python generation (pqac-00000009).

### 5.4 Data and Benchmark Limitations

A critical limitation across AI-driven CAD is dataset imbalance: the standard DeepCAD dataset contains 178,238 models but only covers sketch-and-extrude programs, a small subset of real engineering geometries. High-complexity CAD programs are underrepresented, causing models to "excel on simple shapes but struggle with more intricate ones" (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000024). Standard evaluation metrics are "dominated by results on simple examples," potentially masking failures on complex industrial parts (pqac-00000020). Surrogate models face high data generation costs (reported in 56.8% of surveyed articles) and accuracy concerns (30% of articles), requiring trade-offs between training investment and performance (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000033).

---

## 6. Summary and Outlook

The generative CAD landscape in 2025 spans a spectrum from mature commercial TO/GD platforms delivering proven mass-reduction results (>40–60% savings) through simulation-driven optimization, to rapidly advancing AI/LLM methods that are beginning to generate valid, editable parametric CAD from natural language. Code-based frameworks like CadQuery and build123d occupy a critical bridging role, enabling both traditional parametric modeling and emerging LLM-driven generation within CI/CD-compatible pipelines. Key remaining challenges include closing the gap between generated geometry and manufacturable, fully parametric CAD; expanding training data beyond simple sketch-and-extrude vocabularies; integrating physics-based validation into generative loops; and developing foundation surrogate models that generalize across engineering domains (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000031). The convergence of diffusion models, agentic LLM pipelines, and structured CAD representations represents the most promising frontier for achieving production-grade AI-assisted design automation."}

function.arguments:
{"answer": "# State-of-the-Art Landscape of Generative CAD and Generative Design Tools and Research (2025)

## 1. Introduction

The convergence of topology optimization (TO), generative design (GD), parametric code-based CAD, and AI/LLM-driven generation is reshaping how engineers and designers create 3D models. This landscape review covers four major pillars: (a) commercial TO/GD products, (b) parametric/code-based CAD frameworks, (c) AI/LLM-driven CAD generation research, and (d) cross-cutting constraints and limitations. A structured review of over 14,000 publications found that 72.7% of relevant AI-in-GD papers appeared after 2021, reflecting rapid growth driven by advances in generative AI and computational resources (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029).

---

## 2. Commercial Topology-Optimization and Generative-Design Products

Commercial GD and TO tools encode physical rules and optimization algorithms to generate many candidate forms from user-specified constraints, helping avoid designer fixation and producing multiple design embodiments rapidly (pqac-00000001). The following table summarizes the major platforms:

<artifact id="artifact-01"/>

**Autodesk Fusion Generative Design** generates 3D part forms from geometric and force parameters, exploring many alternatives that users can visualize, simulate, and filter. In case studies it achieved significant mass reductions (e.g., 705 g to 586 g on drone components). Users can apply manufacturing constraints to bias solutions toward AM or subtractive processes (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000003).

**nTopology (nTop)** employs field-driven implicit modeling and FEA-informed lattice structures, particularly suited to powder-based AM workflows. It can generate and manipulate lattice structures driven by simulation data, providing strong DFAM (Design for Additive Manufacturing) support (pqac-00000002).

**Siemens NX + Simcenter HEEDS** automatically generates and compares hundreds to thousands of optimized options, integrating NX CAD geometry with Simcenter CAE for solutions targeted at additive manufacturing. The combined workflow enables large-scale design-space exploration but carries higher enterprise workflow complexity (pqac-00000000).

**Altair Inspire (OptiStruct)** is among the earliest commercial TO platforms. It removes material from a 3D CAD model to meet goals such as maximum stiffness with minimum mass. Practical challenges include translating voxelized TO outputs into manufacturable parametric CAD (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000003).

**PTC Creo Generative Design** and **ANSYS Discovery** offer integrated simulation-driven concept generation, with PTC targeting Creo-native parametric geometry and ANSYS providing real-time physics feedback. Both face the common limitation that optimized shapes often require significant remodeling for production readiness (pqac-00000001).

**Rhino + Grasshopper** provides a flexible visual parametric/algorithmic modeling environment with plugins for structural analysis (Karamba3D, Kangaroo) and evolutionary optimization (Wallacei, Galapagos). While extremely versatile, it requires substantial user expertise and does not provide turnkey certified engineering optimization (pqac-00000001).

Across all commercial TO/GD tools, common limitations include sensitivity to input definitions (loads/boundaries), risk of converging to local optima, high computational cost for high-resolution problems, and difficulty translating voxel-based density fields into manufacturable parametric CAD (B-splines, contours) (pqac-00000001). Additive manufacturing remains best suited to small-batch or niche production, which limits practical manufacturing options for some GD/TO outputs (pqac-00000003). A structured review found that light-weighting studies using GD/TO report >40–60% mass savings versus expert designs (pqac-00000029).

---

## 3. Parametric / Code-Based CAD Frameworks

Code-based CAD frameworks enable programmatic, reproducible, and version-controllable 3D modeling, making them particularly valuable for CI/CD pipelines and automated design workflows. The following table compares the major frameworks:

<artifact id="artifact-02"/>

**CadQuery** is a Python library for parametric 3D modeling built on the OpenCascade (OCCT) kernel. It requires no separate software, uses pure Python, has low modeling difficulty, and offers "excellent" integration with the Python ecosystem (Jupyter, scripting). Its headless operation makes it highly suited for CI/CD pipelines and automated batch geometry generation (pqac-00000008). Recent work on Text-to-CadQuery has demonstrated that CadQuery provides the best trade-off between usability and flexibility for programmatic shape synthesis, and is "well-suited for programmatic shape synthesis" (pqac-00000008).

**OpenSCAD** uses its own domain-specific language (DSL) with a CGAL-based CSG pipeline. It requires separate software for execution, has medium modeling difficulty, and shows no direct Python ecosystem integration (pqac-00000008). While deterministic and reproducible, its limited interoperability constrains its use in modern Python-centric automation workflows.

**build123d** is a modern Python library also built on the OCCT kernel. It serves as a spiritual successor to CadQuery with explicit algebra/mode-building APIs and strong type hints, making it well-suited for testable, CI/CD-integrated CAD generation.

**Onshape FeatureScript** is a proprietary language embedded within the Onshape cloud CAD platform. It enables custom feature authoring within a production-grade parametric system with robust collaboration and versioning. However, it is cloud-only and vendor-tied, limiting offline/local CI/CD workflows.

**JSCAD** provides JavaScript-based parametric CSG modeling that runs in browsers and Node.js environments, making it accessible for web-based configurators and full-stack teams. It is less mature for heavy mechanical CAD than OCCT-based tools.

Key limitations across code-based frameworks include: (i) manufacturability rules are not automatically enforced—users must encode process constraints manually; (ii) interactive visual editing is weaker compared to GUI CAD systems; and (iii) complex freeform/NURBS surfacing can require substantial coding expertise.

---

## 4. AI/LLM-Driven CAD Generation Research

The past three years have seen an explosion of research applying deep learning, LLMs, and diffusion models to automated CAD generation. The following table summarizes benchmark results for representative methods:

<artifact id="artifact-00"/>

### 4.1 Foundational Generative CAD Models

**DeepCAD** (ICCV 2021) pioneered autoregressive deep generative networks for sketch-and-extrude CAD program synthesis. On unconditional generation benchmarks, DeepCAD achieved Coverage (COV) = 65.46%, MMD = 1.29, JSD = 1.67, with 87.4% novel and 89.3% unique outputs, but only 46.1% of generated models were valid sealed B-rep solids. In autoencoder reconstruction, it reports command accuracy (ACCcmd) = 99.36 and parameter accuracy (ACCparam) = 97.47 with a median Chamfer Distance (CD) of 0.787 and an invalid ratio of 3.30% (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006).

**SkexGen** (2022) introduced autoregressive generation of CAD construction sequences with a disentangled latent space separating sketch and extrusion properties. It substantially improved upon DeepCAD with COV = 84.74%, MMD = 1.02, JSD = 0.90, 99.1% novel and 99.8% unique outputs, and a human-perceived realism score of 46.9. For sketch generation, it achieved FID = 18.56 (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027).

**HNC-CAD** (2023) extended controllable generation via hierarchical neural coding with VQ-VAE and cascaded autoregressive transformers, achieving CD = 1.09 (versus DeepCAD's 4.25) and an invalid ratio of only 0.32 in reconstruction benchmarks, enabling novel interaction capabilities for conditional generation and completion tasks (pqac-00000005).

**BrepGen** (CVPR 2024) addressed direct B-rep generation with COV = 71.26%, MMD = 1.04, JSD = 0.09, and 62.9% valid outputs—substantially improving validity over DeepCAD while maintaining 99.8% novelty and 99.7% uniqueness (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000006). More recent work by **BrepGPT** (2025) introduced a single-stage autoregressive framework using Voronoi Half-Patch representation for unified B-rep generation.

### 4.2 Text-to-CAD and LLM-Driven Generation

**Text-to-CadQuery** (2025) proposed generating executable CadQuery Python code directly from natural language, bypassing intermediate command sequences. The best fine-tuned model (Qwen2.5-3B) achieved 69.3% accuracy (up from 58.8% baseline), reduced Chamfer Distance by 48.6%, and attained a volumetric IoU median of 0.9868 and F1 median of 0.9836. The work demonstrated clear scaling benefits: even the smallest model (124M parameters) reached higher accuracy with only 58 minutes of training compared to 2 days for the Text2CAD baseline. A GPT-4.1 mini fine-tuned on 10k examples produced 0% invalid rate but did not exceed accuracy of the 3B model, highlighting that larger LLMs improve executability but require sufficient task-specific data to improve 3D generation accuracy (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000013).

**TCADGen + CADLLM** (2025) combined a Transformer-based CAD sequence generator with LLM-based refinement (Llama 3.2 3B), achieving a sequence-level accuracy of 0.864 and dramatically reducing Chamfer Distance from 120.99–169.93 (baseline transformers) down to approximately 3.12, representing orders-of-magnitude geometric improvement (pqac-00000012).

**CADCodeVerify** (2025) introduced iterative visual verification for LLM-generated CAD code. GPT-4 with few-shot prompting and CADCodeVerify achieved a median point-cloud distance of 0.127 and 96.5% success rate, outperforming Gemini 1.5 Pro (0.147, 85.0%) and CodeLlama (0.185, 73.5%) (pqac-00000014).

**Seek-CAD** (2025) pioneered the use of locally deployed open-source inference LLMs (DeepSeek-R1) for training-free CAD parametric model generation with visual and chain-of-thought feedback in a self-refinement loop.

**CAD-Coder** (2025) fine-tuned DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B to generate CAD script codes with geometric annotations, achieving CD = 0.88 with comments versus 1.31 without, demonstrating the importance of annotation quality for LLM-based CAD generation (pqac-00000010).

### 4.3 Multimodal and Diffusion-Based Approaches

Diffusion models are increasingly applied to CAD generation. **RECAD** (AAAI 2025) employs dual diffusion networks generating raster sketches and extrusion boxes, offering advantages in continuous latent space interpolation and intuitive user control. **GenCAD-3D** (2025) uses multimodal latent space alignment with contrastive learning and latent diffusion models, augmented by synthetic dataset balancing (SynthBal), to improve reconstruction accuracy on high-complexity geometries (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000024).

A structured review identified GANs as the most common architecture for direct design generation (9/14 studies in a systematic sample), with conditional GANs incorporating physical parameters (stress fields, aerodynamic conditions) to produce more physically informed outputs. However, GAN outputs suffer from pixelation, aliasing, and fuzzy edges, and are generally used as starting points for downstream processing rather than final geometries (pqac-00000030, pqac-00000032). Diffusion models are predicted to be adopted in engineering GD as they mature, though a representational mismatch persists between neural pixel/voxel formats and engineering-standard CAD formats (pqac-00000031).

### 4.4 Agentic CAD Pipelines

A framework for automating CAD generation using LLM agents with function calling and agent workflows evaluated five LLMs across four agent workflows. The agent workflow incorporating automated visual feedback outperformed others, especially with multimodal LLMs like ChatGPT-4o. A case study demonstrated its application to topology optimization and additive manufacturing with minimal human input (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000021).

---

## 5. Constraints and Limitations

### 5.1 Manufacturability

Across all approaches, manufacturability remains a critical under-addressed challenge. Commercial TO/GD tools can encode manufacturing constraints (e.g., AM build directions, minimum feature sizes), but outputs typically require extensive post-processing before they are production-ready (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000003). AI-generated CAD models fundamentally "overlook key aspects such as manufacturability or the practical application of each geometry," with frameworks that focus on raw geometry creation lacking built-in DfM checks (pqac-00000017). Current AI approaches "reason about geometry only, without validation from a physical simulation," limiting confidence for functional and manufacturing requirements (pqac-00000018). Research suggests integrating AI-based manufacturability checkers (e.g., surrogate models acting as MLP-based DfM filters) into late-stage optimization to improve producibility (pqac-00000031).

### 5.2 Editability and Parametric Controllability

A fundamental tension exists between AI-generated outputs and traditional parametric CAD workflows. Many generative models produce non-parametric representations (point clouds, meshes) that "require manual adjustments to meet exact design intent" and lack design history or CAD interoperability features essential for downstream modification (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021). Tokenization and quantization approaches used in sequence-based CAD generation produce coordinate precision loss and unstable predictions, causing misaligned or disconnected components (pqac-00000018). Current methods do not explicitly capture high-level geometric constraints such as symmetry, orthogonality, or parallelism, so edits can break design intent (pqac-00000018). The code-generation paradigm (e.g., Text-to-CadQuery) partially addresses editability by outputting executable parametric scripts, but this approach is currently limited to the sketch-and-extrude subset of CAD operations (pqac-00000024).

### 5.3 Scriptability and CI/CD Integration

Code-based frameworks like CadQuery and build123d offer the strongest CI/CD integration due to their Python-native, headless operation and excellent scripting ecosystem support (pqac-00000008). OpenSCAD provides deterministic reproducibility but limited Python integration. Commercial TO/GD tools are largely GUI-bound with API access varying by vendor. The emerging Text-to-CadQuery paradigm bridges AI generation with CI/CD-friendly code output, leveraging the fact that LLMs already excel at Python generation (pqac-00000009).

### 5.4 Data and Benchmark Limitations

A critical limitation across AI-driven CAD is dataset imbalance: the standard DeepCAD dataset contains 178,238 models but only covers sketch-and-extrude programs, a small subset of real engineering geometries. High-complexity CAD programs are underrepresented, causing models to "excel on simple shapes but struggle with more intricate ones" (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000024). Standard evaluation metrics are "dominated by results on simple examples," potentially masking failures on complex industrial parts (pqac-00000020). Surrogate models face high data generation costs (reported in 56.8% of surveyed articles) and accuracy concerns (30% of articles), requiring trade-offs between training investment and performance (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000033).

---

## 6. Summary and Outlook

The generative CAD landscape in 2025 spans a spectrum from mature commercial TO/GD platforms delivering proven mass-reduction results (>40–60% savings) through simulation-driven optimization, to rapidly advancing AI/LLM methods that are beginning to generate valid, editable parametric CAD from natural language. Code-based frameworks like CadQuery and build123d occupy a critical bridging role, enabling both traditional parametric modeling and emerging LLM-driven generation within CI/CD-compatible pipelines. Key remaining challenges include closing the gap between generated geometry and manufacturable, fully parametric CAD; expanding training data beyond simple sketch-and-extrude vocabularies; integrating physics-based validation into generative loops; and developing foundation surrogate models that generalize across engineering domains (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000031). The convergence of diffusion models, agentic LLM pipelines, and structured CAD representations represents the most promising frontier for achieving production-grade AI-assisted design automation."}

answer.answer:
# State-of-the-Art Landscape of Generative CAD and Generative Design Tools and Research (2025)

## 1. Introduction

The convergence of topology optimization (TO), generative design (GD), parametric code-based CAD, and AI/LLM-driven generation is reshaping how engineers and designers create 3D models. This landscape review covers four major pillars: (a) commercial TO/GD products, (b) parametric/code-based CAD frameworks, (c) AI/LLM-driven CAD generation research, and (d) cross-cutting constraints and limitations. A structured review of over 14,000 publications found that 72.7% of relevant AI-in-GD papers appeared after 2021, reflecting rapid growth driven by advances in generative AI and computational resources (peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 26-28, peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 1-3).

---

## 2. Commercial Topology-Optimization and Generative-Design Products

Commercial GD and TO tools encode physical rules and optimization algorithms to generate many candidate forms from user-specified constraints, helping avoid designer fixation and producing multiple design embodiments rapidly (watson2023generativedesignof pages 2-4). The following table summarizes the major platforms:

| Tool Name | Vendor | Core Method (TO/GD/both) | Key Features | Manufacturing Constraints Support | Output Format | Notable Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Autodesk Fusion Generative Design | Autodesk | Both | Generates 3D part forms from geometric + force parameters; explores many alternatives; supports simulation-based evaluation and filtering of outcomes; widely used for lightweighting and AM-oriented redesign (souvanhnakhoomman2025acomprehensivereview pages 5-6, nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 4-8) | Strong relative to mainstream CAD GD tools; users can apply manufacturing constraints to bias solutions toward manufacturable outputs, especially AM processes (nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 4-8) | Native Fusion geometry; exportable CAD/B-rep workflows | Outputs often skew toward AM-style organic forms; many alternatives still require manual interpretation and downstream styling; engineer-centric workflow (nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 8-11, nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 4-8) |
| nTopology / nTop | nTop | Both | Field-driven implicit modeling; strong lattice generation/manipulation; integrates FEA-informed lattice workflows; well suited to AM and performance-driven geometry (nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 8-11) | Strong for AM/DFAM, especially lattice and powder-bed workflows (nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 8-11) | Implicit/mesh/B-rep export depending workflow | Best fit is advanced AM rather than conventional subtractive manufacturing; requires expertise to convert performance forms into broader production-ready product geometry (watson2023generativedesignof pages 2-4, nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 8-11) |
| Siemens NX Topology Optimizer (+ Simcenter HEEDS) | Siemens | Both | NX geometry + Simcenter/HEEDS can automatically generate and compare hundreds to thousands of optimized options; integrates CAD and CAE; targeted at high-end engineering workflows and AM-ready exploration (souvanhnakhoomman2025acomprehensivereview pages 5-6) | Good support through NX/Simcenter manufacturing-aware setup and AM-targeted workflows (souvanhnakhoomman2025acomprehensivereview pages 5-6) | NX CAD geometry / CAE-linked optimized models | Higher workflow complexity and enterprise cost; still subject to classic TO sensitivity to loads/boundary conditions and post-processing burden for manufacturable CAD (souvanhnakhoomman2025acomprehensivereview pages 5-6, watson2023generativedesignof pages 2-4) |
| PTC Creo Generative Design | PTC | Both | Integrated parametric CAD + simulation-driven concept generation; intended to produce editable Creo-native concepts and support engineering redesign | Moderate-to-strong in principle via process constraints, preserved geometry, loads, materials, and manufacturing method setup | Creo-native parametric geometry / exportable CAD | Fewer independently reported benchmark-style public results than Autodesk/Altair; manufacturability and editability depend heavily on problem setup and post-processing; organic outputs still need engineering cleanup (watson2023generativedesignof pages 2-4) |
| ANSYS Discovery | ANSYS | Both | Real-time simulation-guided design exploration; topology optimization linked to fast physics feedback; strong CAE-centric concept screening | Good for simulation-constrained design; manufacturability can be introduced through setup constraints, but typically less design-history-centric than full parametric CAD | Simulation-derived geometry / CAD handoff | More analysis-centric than end-to-end editable CAD authoring; translating optimized shapes into robust production CAD can require extra remodeling (watson2023generativedesignof pages 2-4) |
| Altair Inspire (OptiStruct) | Altair | Both | Early and influential commercial TO platform; removes material from a 3D CAD model to meet goals such as maximum stiffness with minimum mass; strong concept-lightweighting workflow (nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 4-8) | Moderate; supports engineering constraints and process-aware setup, but manufacturable finalization often needs redesign/refinement (nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 4-8) | Optimized concept geometry / export to downstream CAD | TO outputs can be hard to convert directly into final parametric CAD; workflows are predominantly engineer-focused; raw results may require significant remodeling and styling (watson2023generativedesignof pages 2-4, nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 4-8) |
| Rhino + Grasshopper | Robert McNeel + Associates | GD-oriented (with TO via plugins) | Flexible visual parametric/algorithmic modeling; strong for custom rule-based exploration, geometry scripting, and integration with plugins such as Kangaroo, Karamba3D, Millipede, Wallacei, and AM workflows | Variable; can encode manufacturing rules and custom constraints, but depends on user-authored definitions/plugins rather than turnkey solver workflows | NURBS/mesh/parametric Grasshopper definitions; broad export support | Powerful but less turnkey for certified engineering optimization; requires substantial user expertise, plugin selection, and manual validation; manufacturability and performance assurance are not automatic (watson2023generativedesignof pages 2-4) |


*Table: This table compares major commercial topology-optimization and generative-design platforms on method, workflow strengths, manufacturing support, outputs, and practical limitations. It is useful for distinguishing simulation-centric optimization tools from more editable parametric or algorithmic design environments.*

**Autodesk Fusion Generative Design** generates 3D part forms from geometric and force parameters, exploring many alternatives that users can visualize, simulate, and filter. In case studies it achieved significant mass reductions (e.g., 705 g to 586 g on drone components). Users can apply manufacturing constraints to bias solutions toward AM or subtractive processes (souvanhnakhoomman2025acomprehensivereview pages 5-6, nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 4-8).

**nTopology (nTop)** employs field-driven implicit modeling and FEA-informed lattice structures, particularly suited to powder-based AM workflows. It can generate and manipulate lattice structures driven by simulation data, providing strong DFAM (Design for Additive Manufacturing) support (nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 8-11).

**Siemens NX + Simcenter HEEDS** automatically generates and compares hundreds to thousands of optimized options, integrating NX CAD geometry with Simcenter CAE for solutions targeted at additive manufacturing. The combined workflow enables large-scale design-space exploration but carries higher enterprise workflow complexity (souvanhnakhoomman2025acomprehensivereview pages 5-6).

**Altair Inspire (OptiStruct)** is among the earliest commercial TO platforms. It removes material from a 3D CAD model to meet goals such as maximum stiffness with minimum mass. Practical challenges include translating voxelized TO outputs into manufacturable parametric CAD (watson2023generativedesignof pages 2-4, nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 4-8).

**PTC Creo Generative Design** and **ANSYS Discovery** offer integrated simulation-driven concept generation, with PTC targeting Creo-native parametric geometry and ANSYS providing real-time physics feedback. Both face the common limitation that optimized shapes often require significant remodeling for production readiness (watson2023generativedesignof pages 2-4).

**Rhino + Grasshopper** provides a flexible visual parametric/algorithmic modeling environment with plugins for structural analysis (Karamba3D, Kangaroo) and evolutionary optimization (Wallacei, Galapagos). While extremely versatile, it requires substantial user expertise and does not provide turnkey certified engineering optimization (watson2023generativedesignof pages 2-4).

Across all commercial TO/GD tools, common limitations include sensitivity to input definitions (loads/boundaries), risk of converging to local optima, high computational cost for high-resolution problems, and difficulty translating voxel-based density fields into manufacturable parametric CAD (B-splines, contours) (watson2023generativedesignof pages 2-4). Additive manufacturing remains best suited to small-batch or niche production, which limits practical manufacturing options for some GD/TO outputs (nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 4-8). A structured review found that light-weighting studies using GD/TO report >40–60% mass savings versus expert designs (peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 1-3).

---

## 3. Parametric / Code-Based CAD Frameworks

Code-based CAD frameworks enable programmatic, reproducible, and version-controllable 3D modeling, making them particularly valuable for CI/CD pipelines and automated design workflows. The following table compares the major frameworks:

| Framework | Language | License/Type | Kernel | GUI Required? | Python Ecosystem Integration | CI/CD Suitability | Key Strengths | Key Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CadQuery | Python | Open-source library | OpenCascade (via OCCT/OCP) | No | Excellent; strong Jupyter and scripting workflow, with no separate CAD software required (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 12-15) | High | Parametric, programmable solid modeling; readable Python API; good for automation, testing, batch generation, and export pipelines; strong fit for reproducible engineering code (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 12-15) | Less interactive than mainstream GUI CAD; advanced surfacing/assemblies can require more coding expertise; manufacturability rules are not automatic |
| OpenSCAD | OpenSCAD DSL | Open-source application/language | CGAL-based CSG pipeline | Typically yes for normal use; headless CLI possible | None/very limited; not Python-native and requires separate software (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 12-15) | Medium | Extremely deterministic and reproducible; simple declarative CSG; excellent for scripted fixtures, enclosures, and parameterized hobbyist/mechanical parts | Weaker ecosystem interoperability; limited direct Python integration; less suited to complex fillets/freeform industrial geometry; editability is code-centric rather than feature-tree-centric (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 12-15) |
| build123d | Python | Open-source library | OpenCascade (OCCT) | No | Excellent | High | Modern Pythonic parametric CAD; close to CadQuery but with explicit algebra/mode-building APIs; strong type hints and testability; good for CI/CD and reusable CAD components | Smaller ecosystem/community than older tools; still code-first, so discoverability and interactive editing are weaker than GUI CAD |
| Onshape FeatureScript | FeatureScript | Proprietary cloud CAD language/platform | Onshape Parasolid-based cloud kernel | Yes, via Onshape cloud environment | Limited/indirect; accessible through APIs rather than native Python modeling | Medium-High | Native extension mechanism inside a production CAD system; robust parametric features; strong collaboration, versioning, and enterprise governance; good for custom feature authoring in real engineering workflows | Cloud-only and vendor-tied; proprietary language/platform; less suitable for offline/local builds; CI/CD depends on SaaS/API workflows rather than pure local execution |
| JSCAD | JavaScript | Open-source library/toolchain | CSG/B-rep-style JS geometry engine | No | Limited direct Python integration; strong JavaScript/Node ecosystem instead | High for JS-centric teams | Browser- and Node-friendly; easy web deployment; good for lightweight parametric CSG and custom configurators; accessible for full-stack developers | Less mature for heavy mechanical CAD than OCCT-based tools; complex fillets/shells/industrial geometry can be harder; manufacturability and engineering semantics are mostly user-enforced |


*Table: This table compares major parametric and code-driven CAD frameworks on language, kernel, scripting ergonomics, and CI/CD suitability. It is useful for distinguishing pure code-first CAD stacks from cloud-native feature authoring and browser-oriented geometry tools.*

**CadQuery** is a Python library for parametric 3D modeling built on the OpenCascade (OCCT) kernel. It requires no separate software, uses pure Python, has low modeling difficulty, and offers "excellent" integration with the Python ecosystem (Jupyter, scripting). Its headless operation makes it highly suited for CI/CD pipelines and automated batch geometry generation (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 12-15). Recent work on Text-to-CadQuery has demonstrated that CadQuery provides the best trade-off between usability and flexibility for programmatic shape synthesis, and is "well-suited for programmatic shape synthesis" (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 12-15).

**OpenSCAD** uses its own domain-specific language (DSL) with a CGAL-based CSG pipeline. It requires separate software for execution, has medium modeling difficulty, and shows no direct Python ecosystem integration (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 12-15). While deterministic and reproducible, its limited interoperability constrains its use in modern Python-centric automation workflows.

**build123d** is a modern Python library also built on the OCCT kernel. It serves as a spiritual successor to CadQuery with explicit algebra/mode-building APIs and strong type hints, making it well-suited for testable, CI/CD-integrated CAD generation.

**Onshape FeatureScript** is a proprietary language embedded within the Onshape cloud CAD platform. It enables custom feature authoring within a production-grade parametric system with robust collaboration and versioning. However, it is cloud-only and vendor-tied, limiting offline/local CI/CD workflows.

**JSCAD** provides JavaScript-based parametric CSG modeling that runs in browsers and Node.js environments, making it accessible for web-based configurators and full-stack teams. It is less mature for heavy mechanical CAD than OCCT-based tools.

Key limitations across code-based frameworks include: (i) manufacturability rules are not automatically enforced—users must encode process constraints manually; (ii) interactive visual editing is weaker compared to GUI CAD systems; and (iii) complex freeform/NURBS surfacing can require substantial coding expertise.

---

## 4. AI/LLM-Driven CAD Generation Research

The past three years have seen an explosion of research applying deep learning, LLMs, and diffusion models to automated CAD generation. The following table summarizes benchmark results for representative methods:

| Model | Year | Input Modality | Architecture | Coverage (COV%) | MMD | JSD | Valid% | Chamfer Distance (CD) | Other Key Metrics |
|---|---:|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| DeepCAD (ICCV) | 2021 | CAD command sequences / unconditional CAD generation | Autoregressive deep generative network for sketch-and-extrude CAD programs | 65.46 | 1.29 | 1.67 | 46.1 | Median CD 0.787 (autoencoder); CD 4.25 in reconstruction benchmark | Novel 87.4%, Unique 89.3%; ACCcmd 99.36, ACCparam 97.47, Invalid Ratio 3.30%; NC 0.49, IR 7.14 in reconstruction table (lin2025asurveyon pages 16-18, lin2025asurveyon pages 14-16, lin2025asurveyon pages 18-19) |
| SkexGen | 2022 | Unconditional CAD construction sequences | Autoregressive generation with disentangled latent space for sketch-and-extrude CAD | 84.74 | 1.02 | 0.90 | — | MSLCD 0.81 | Novel 99.1%, Unique 99.8%, Human realism 46.9; sketch FID 18.56, Unique 96.02%, Novel 83.54%; cmd 83.6, μSLparam 1.48 (yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 10-11, xu2023hierarchicalneuralcoding pages 6-7, xu2023hierarchicalneuralcoding pages 13-20) |
| HNC-CAD | 2023 | Incomplete or unconditional CAD models | Hierarchical neural coding with VQ-VAE + cascaded autoregressive transformers | — | — | — | — | CD 1.09 | HD 20.23, ECD 5.94, NC 0.75, IR 0.32; emphasizes controllable conditional generation/completion (lin2025asurveyon pages 14-16) |
| BrepGen (CVPR) | 2024 | Unconditional B-rep CAD generation | B-rep generative model | 71.26 | 1.04 | 0.09 | 62.9 | — | Novel 99.8%, Unique 99.7%; outperforms DeepCAD on unconditional generation benchmark (lin2025asurveyon pages 16-18, lin2025asurveyon pages 18-19) |
| Text-to-CadQuery | 2025 | Natural language text | Fine-tuned LLMs generating executable CadQuery Python directly | — | — | — | — | Median CD 0.191; mean CD 10.229×10^3 | Best accuracy 69.3%; 48.6% CD reduction; volumetric IoU median 0.9868; F1 median 0.9836; best model Qwen2.5-3B; Mistral-7B IR 1.32; GPT-4.1 mini fine-tuned achieved 0% invalid rate (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 1-3, xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 8-10, xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 6-8) |
| TCADGen + CADLLM | 2025 | Text descriptions with parameters/appearance cues | Transformer CAD sequence generator + LLM refinement | — | — | — | — | 3.12 | Sequence accuracy 0.864; average LCSratio 0.983; command-level metrics ≈0.966/0.947/0.962; large CD improvement versus baselines (e.g., 120.99–169.93 down to ~3–4) (liao2025automatedcadmodeling pages 7-8) |
| CADCodeVerify + GPT-4 | 2025 | Natural language prompts (+ visual verification loop) | Vision-language iterative verification/refinement of CAD code | — | — | — | 96.5 success rate | Median point-cloud distance 0.127 | GPT-4 few-shot + CADCodeVerify; zero-shot 0.132 / 94.0% success; uses Chamfer-style point-cloud distance, Hausdorff distance, IoGT (alrashedy2024generatingcadcode pages 7-9) |
| GenCAD-3D | 2025 | Point clouds / meshes / multimodal geometry-to-CAD | Multimodal latent alignment + latent diffusion + synthetic data balancing | — | — | — | — | — | Reported to improve reconstruction accuracy, reduce invalid CAD generation, and improve high-complexity geometry performance, but excerpt did not provide numeric benchmark values (yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 1-2, yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 14-15) |
| RECAD (AAAI) | 2025 | Unconditional and conditional CAD generation | Dual diffusion networks over raster sketches + extrusion boxes | — | — | — | — | — | Strong unconditional generation and effective conditional generation/editing reported, but excerpt did not provide numeric benchmark values (liao2025automatedcadmodeling pages 7-8) |


*Table: This table compares representative AI/deep-learning CAD generation models through 2025 using the benchmark values available in the gathered evidence. It highlights where metrics are directly comparable and where recent papers report qualitative gains but not standardized numeric results.*

### 4.1 Foundational Generative CAD Models

**DeepCAD** (ICCV 2021) pioneered autoregressive deep generative networks for sketch-and-extrude CAD program synthesis. On unconditional generation benchmarks, DeepCAD achieved Coverage (COV) = 65.46%, MMD = 1.29, JSD = 1.67, with 87.4% novel and 89.3% unique outputs, but only 46.1% of generated models were valid sealed B-rep solids. In autoencoder reconstruction, it reports command accuracy (ACCcmd) = 99.36 and parameter accuracy (ACCparam) = 97.47 with a median Chamfer Distance (CD) of 0.787 and an invalid ratio of 3.30% (lin2025asurveyon pages 16-18, lin2025asurveyon pages 14-16, lin2025asurveyon pages 18-19).

**SkexGen** (2022) introduced autoregressive generation of CAD construction sequences with a disentangled latent space separating sketch and extrusion properties. It substantially improved upon DeepCAD with COV = 84.74%, MMD = 1.02, JSD = 0.90, 99.1% novel and 99.8% unique outputs, and a human-perceived realism score of 46.9. For sketch generation, it achieved FID = 18.56 (xu2023hierarchicalneuralcoding pages 6-7, xu2023hierarchicalneuralcoding pages 13-20).

**HNC-CAD** (2023) extended controllable generation via hierarchical neural coding with VQ-VAE and cascaded autoregressive transformers, achieving CD = 1.09 (versus DeepCAD's 4.25) and an invalid ratio of only 0.32 in reconstruction benchmarks, enabling novel interaction capabilities for conditional generation and completion tasks (lin2025asurveyon pages 14-16).

**BrepGen** (CVPR 2024) addressed direct B-rep generation with COV = 71.26%, MMD = 1.04, JSD = 0.09, and 62.9% valid outputs—substantially improving validity over DeepCAD while maintaining 99.8% novelty and 99.7% uniqueness (lin2025asurveyon pages 16-18, lin2025asurveyon pages 18-19). More recent work by **BrepGPT** (2025) introduced a single-stage autoregressive framework using Voronoi Half-Patch representation for unified B-rep generation.

### 4.2 Text-to-CAD and LLM-Driven Generation

**Text-to-CadQuery** (2025) proposed generating executable CadQuery Python code directly from natural language, bypassing intermediate command sequences. The best fine-tuned model (Qwen2.5-3B) achieved 69.3% accuracy (up from 58.8% baseline), reduced Chamfer Distance by 48.6%, and attained a volumetric IoU median of 0.9868 and F1 median of 0.9836. The work demonstrated clear scaling benefits: even the smallest model (124M parameters) reached higher accuracy with only 58 minutes of training compared to 2 days for the Text2CAD baseline. A GPT-4.1 mini fine-tuned on 10k examples produced 0% invalid rate but did not exceed accuracy of the 3B model, highlighting that larger LLMs improve executability but require sufficient task-specific data to improve 3D generation accuracy (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 1-3, xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 8-10, xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 6-8).

**TCADGen + CADLLM** (2025) combined a Transformer-based CAD sequence generator with LLM-based refinement (Llama 3.2 3B), achieving a sequence-level accuracy of 0.864 and dramatically reducing Chamfer Distance from 120.99–169.93 (baseline transformers) down to approximately 3.12, representing orders-of-magnitude geometric improvement (liao2025automatedcadmodeling pages 7-8).

**CADCodeVerify** (2025) introduced iterative visual verification for LLM-generated CAD code. GPT-4 with few-shot prompting and CADCodeVerify achieved a median point-cloud distance of 0.127 and 96.5% success rate, outperforming Gemini 1.5 Pro (0.147, 85.0%) and CodeLlama (0.185, 73.5%) (alrashedy2024generatingcadcode pages 7-9).

**Seek-CAD** (2025) pioneered the use of locally deployed open-source inference LLMs (DeepSeek-R1) for training-free CAD parametric model generation with visual and chain-of-thought feedback in a self-refinement loop.

**CAD-Coder** (2025) fine-tuned DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B to generate CAD script codes with geometric annotations, achieving CD = 0.88 with comments versus 1.31 without, demonstrating the importance of annotation quality for LLM-based CAD generation (he2505cadcodertextguidedcadfiles pages 11-12).

### 4.3 Multimodal and Diffusion-Based Approaches

Diffusion models are increasingly applied to CAD generation. **RECAD** (AAAI 2025) employs dual diffusion networks generating raster sketches and extrusion boxes, offering advantages in continuous latent space interpolation and intuitive user control. **GenCAD-3D** (2025) uses multimodal latent space alignment with contrastive learning and latent diffusion models, augmented by synthetic dataset balancing (SynthBal), to improve reconstruction accuracy on high-complexity geometries (yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 1-2, yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 14-15).

A structured review identified GANs as the most common architecture for direct design generation (9/14 studies in a systematic sample), with conditional GANs incorporating physical parameters (stress fields, aerodynamic conditions) to produce more physically informed outputs. However, GAN outputs suffer from pixelation, aliasing, and fuzzy edges, and are generally used as starting points for downstream processing rather than final geometries (peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 20-21, peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 23-24). Diffusion models are predicted to be adopted in engineering GD as they mature, though a representational mismatch persists between neural pixel/voxel formats and engineering-standard CAD formats (peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 24-26).

### 4.4 Agentic CAD Pipelines

A framework for automating CAD generation using LLM agents with function calling and agent workflows evaluated five LLMs across four agent workflows. The agent workflow incorporating automated visual feedback outperformed others, especially with multimodal LLMs like ChatGPT-4o. A case study demonstrated its application to topology optimization and additive manufacturing with minimal human input (schupbach2025fromtextto pages 7-8, schupbach2025fromtextto pages 1-2).

---

## 5. Constraints and Limitations

### 5.1 Manufacturability

Across all approaches, manufacturability remains a critical under-addressed challenge. Commercial TO/GD tools can encode manufacturing constraints (e.g., AM build directions, minimum feature sizes), but outputs typically require extensive post-processing before they are production-ready (watson2023generativedesignof pages 2-4, nemme2022integratinggenerativedesign pages 4-8). AI-generated CAD models fundamentally "overlook key aspects such as manufacturability or the practical application of each geometry," with frameworks that focus on raw geometry creation lacking built-in DfM checks (schupbach2025fromtextto pages 7-8). Current AI approaches "reason about geometry only, without validation from a physical simulation," limiting confidence for functional and manufacturing requirements (liu2508breplerlanguageguidedediting pages 8-9). Research suggests integrating AI-based manufacturability checkers (e.g., surrogate models acting as MLP-based DfM filters) into late-stage optimization to improve producibility (peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 24-26).

### 5.2 Editability and Parametric Controllability

A fundamental tension exists between AI-generated outputs and traditional parametric CAD workflows. Many generative models produce non-parametric representations (point clouds, meshes) that "require manual adjustments to meet exact design intent" and lack design history or CAD interoperability features essential for downstream modification (yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 1-2, schupbach2025fromtextto pages 1-2). Tokenization and quantization approaches used in sequence-based CAD generation produce coordinate precision loss and unstable predictions, causing misaligned or disconnected components (liu2508breplerlanguageguidedediting pages 8-9). Current methods do not explicitly capture high-level geometric constraints such as symmetry, orthogonality, or parallelism, so edits can break design intent (liu2508breplerlanguageguidedediting pages 8-9). The code-generation paradigm (e.g., Text-to-CadQuery) partially addresses editability by outputting executable parametric scripts, but this approach is currently limited to the sketch-and-extrude subset of CAD operations (yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 14-15).

### 5.3 Scriptability and CI/CD Integration

Code-based frameworks like CadQuery and build123d offer the strongest CI/CD integration due to their Python-native, headless operation and excellent scripting ecosystem support (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 12-15). OpenSCAD provides deterministic reproducibility but limited Python integration. Commercial TO/GD tools are largely GUI-bound with API access varying by vendor. The emerging Text-to-CadQuery paradigm bridges AI generation with CI/CD-friendly code output, leveraging the fact that LLMs already excel at Python generation (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 1-3).

### 5.4 Data and Benchmark Limitations

A critical limitation across AI-driven CAD is dataset imbalance: the standard DeepCAD dataset contains 178,238 models but only covers sketch-and-extrude programs, a small subset of real engineering geometries. High-complexity CAD programs are underrepresented, causing models to "excel on simple shapes but struggle with more intricate ones" (yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 1-2, yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 14-15). Standard evaluation metrics are "dominated by results on simple examples," potentially masking failures on complex industrial parts (yu2026gencadthreedimensionalcomputeraideddesign pages 1-2). Surrogate models face high data generation costs (reported in 56.8% of surveyed articles) and accuracy concerns (30% of articles), requiring trade-offs between training investment and performance (peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 26-28, peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 21-23).

---

## 6. Summary and Outlook

The generative CAD landscape in 2025 spans a spectrum from mature commercial TO/GD platforms delivering proven mass-reduction results (>40–60% savings) through simulation-driven optimization, to rapidly advancing AI/LLM methods that are beginning to generate valid, editable parametric CAD from natural language. Code-based frameworks like CadQuery and build123d occupy a critical bridging role, enabling both traditional parametric modeling and emerging LLM-driven generation within CI/CD-compatible pipelines. Key remaining challenges include closing the gap between generated geometry and manufacturable, fully parametric CAD; expanding training data beyond simple sketch-and-extrude vocabularies; integrating physics-based validation into generative loops; and developing foundation surrogate models that generalize across engineering domains (peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 1-3, peckham2025artificialintelligencein pages 24-26). The convergence of diffusion models, agentic LLM pipelines, and structured CAD representations represents the most promising frontier for achieving production-grade AI-assisted design automation.
