# State-of-the-Art Landscape of Generative CAD and Generative Design Tools and Research (2025)

## 1. Introduction

The convergence of topology optimization (TO), generative design (GD), parametric code-based CAD, and AI/LLM-driven generation is reshaping how engineers and designers create 3D models. This landscape review covers four major pillars: (a) commercial TO/GD products, (b) parametric/code-based CAD frameworks, (c) AI/LLM-driven CAD generation research, and (d) cross-cutting constraints and limitations. A structured review of over 14,000 publications found that 72.7% of relevant AI-in-GD papers appeared after 2021, reflecting rapid growth driven by advances in generative AI and computational resources (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029).

---

## 2. Commercial Topology-Optimization and Generative-Design Products

Commercial GD and TO tools encode physical rules and optimization algorithms to generate many candidate forms from user-specified constraints, helping avoid designer fixation and producing multiple design embodiments rapidly (pqac-00000001). The following table summarizes the major platforms:

| Tool Name | Vendor | Core Method (TO/GD/both) | Key Features | Manufacturing Constraints Support | Output Format | Notable Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Autodesk Fusion Generative Design | Autodesk | Both | Generates 3D part forms from geometric + force parameters; explores many alternatives; supports simulation-based evaluation and filtering of outcomes; widely used for lightweighting and AM-oriented redesign (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000003) | Strong relative to mainstream CAD GD tools; users can apply manufacturing constraints to bias solutions toward manufacturable outputs, especially AM processes (pqac-00000003) | Native Fusion geometry; exportable CAD/B-rep workflows | Outputs often skew toward AM-style organic forms; many alternatives still require manual interpretation and downstream styling; engineer-centric workflow (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003) |
| nTopology / nTop | nTop | Both | Field-driven implicit modeling; strong lattice generation/manipulation; integrates FEA-informed lattice workflows; well suited to AM and performance-driven geometry (pqac-00000002) | Strong for AM/DFAM, especially lattice and powder-bed workflows (pqac-00000002) | Implicit/mesh/B-rep export depending workflow | Best fit is advanced AM rather than conventional subtractive manufacturing; requires expertise to convert performance forms into broader production-ready product geometry (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002) |
| Siemens NX Topology Optimizer (+ Simcenter HEEDS) | Siemens | Both | NX geometry + Simcenter/HEEDS can automatically generate and compare hundreds to thousands of optimized options; integrates CAD and CAE; targeted at high-end engineering workflows and AM-ready exploration (pqac-00000000) | Good support through NX/Simcenter manufacturing-aware setup and AM-targeted workflows (pqac-00000000) | NX CAD geometry / CAE-linked optimized models | Higher workflow complexity and enterprise cost; still subject to classic TO sensitivity to loads/boundary conditions and post-processing burden for manufacturable CAD (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001) |
| PTC Creo Generative Design | PTC | Both | Integrated parametric CAD + simulation-driven concept generation; intended to produce editable Creo-native concepts and support engineering redesign | Moderate-to-strong in principle via process constraints, preserved geometry, loads, materials, and manufacturing method setup | Creo-native parametric geometry / exportable CAD | Fewer independently reported benchmark-style public results than Autodesk/Altair; manufacturability and editability depend heavily on problem setup and post-processing; organic outputs still need engineering cleanup (pqac-00000001) |
| ANSYS Discovery | ANSYS | Both | Real-time simulation-guided design exploration; topology optimization linked to fast physics feedback; strong CAE-centric concept screening | Good for simulation-constrained design; manufacturability can be introduced through setup constraints, but typically less design-history-centric than full parametric CAD | Simulation-derived geometry / CAD handoff | More analysis-centric than end-to-end editable CAD authoring; translating optimized shapes into robust production CAD can require extra remodeling (pqac-00000001) |
| Altair Inspire (OptiStruct) | Altair | Both | Early and influential commercial TO platform; removes material from a 3D CAD model to meet goals such as maximum stiffness with minimum mass; strong concept-lightweighting workflow (pqac-00000003) | Moderate; supports engineering constraints and process-aware setup, but manufacturable finalization often needs redesign/refinement (pqac-00000003) | Optimized concept geometry / export to downstream CAD | TO outputs can be hard to convert directly into final parametric CAD; workflows are predominantly engineer-focused; raw results may require significant remodeling and styling (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000003) |
| Rhino + Grasshopper | Robert McNeel + Associates | GD-oriented (with TO via plugins) | Flexible visual parametric/algorithmic modeling; strong for custom rule-based exploration, geometry scripting, and integration with plugins such as Kangaroo, Karamba3D, Millipede, Wallacei, and AM workflows | Variable; can encode manufacturing rules and custom constraints, but depends on user-authored definitions/plugins rather than turnkey solver workflows | NURBS/mesh/parametric Grasshopper definitions; broad export support | Powerful but less turnkey for certified engineering optimization; requires substantial user expertise, plugin selection, and manual validation; manufacturability and performance assurance are not automatic (pqac-00000001) |


*Table: This table compares major commercial topology-optimization and generative-design platforms on method, workflow strengths, manufacturing support, outputs, and practical limitations. It is useful for distinguishing simulation-centric optimization tools from more editable parametric or algorithmic design environments.*

**Autodesk Fusion Generative Design** generates 3D part forms from geometric and force parameters, exploring many alternatives that users can visualize, simulate, and filter. In case studies it achieved significant mass reductions (e.g., 705 g to 586 g on drone components). Users can apply manufacturing constraints to bias solutions toward AM or subtractive processes (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000003).

**nTopology (nTop)** employs field-driven implicit modeling and FEA-informed lattice structures, particularly suited to powder-based AM workflows. It can generate and manipulate lattice structures driven by simulation data, providing strong DFAM (Design for Additive Manufacturing) support (pqac-00000002).

**Siemens NX + Simcenter HEEDS** automatically generates and compares hundreds to thousands of optimized options, integrating NX CAD geometry with Simcenter CAE for solutions targeted at additive manufacturing. The combined workflow enables large-scale design-space exploration but carries higher enterprise workflow complexity (pqac-00000000).

**Altair Inspire (OptiStruct)** is among the earliest commercial TO platforms. It removes material from a 3D CAD model to meet goals such as maximum stiffness with minimum mass. Practical challenges include translating voxelized TO outputs into manufacturable parametric CAD (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000003).

**PTC Creo Generative Design** and **ANSYS Discovery** offer integrated simulation-driven concept generation, with PTC targeting Creo-native parametric geometry and ANSYS providing real-time physics feedback. Both face the common limitation that optimized shapes often require significant remodeling for production readiness (pqac-00000001).

**Rhino + Grasshopper** provides a flexible visual parametric/algorithmic modeling environment with plugins for structural analysis (Karamba3D, Kangaroo) and evolutionary optimization (Wallacei, Galapagos). While extremely versatile, it requires substantial user expertise and does not provide turnkey certified engineering optimization (pqac-00000001).

Across all commercial TO/GD tools, common limitations include sensitivity to input definitions (loads/boundaries), risk of converging to local optima, high computational cost for high-resolution problems, and difficulty translating voxel-based density fields into manufacturable parametric CAD (B-splines, contours) (pqac-00000001). Additive manufacturing remains best suited to small-batch or niche production, which limits practical manufacturing options for some GD/TO outputs (pqac-00000003). A structured review found that light-weighting studies using GD/TO report >40–60% mass savings versus expert designs (pqac-00000029).

---

## 3. Parametric / Code-Based CAD Frameworks

Code-based CAD frameworks enable programmatic, reproducible, and version-controllable 3D modeling, making them particularly valuable for CI/CD pipelines and automated design workflows. The following table compares the major frameworks:

| Framework | Language | License/Type | Kernel | GUI Required? | Python Ecosystem Integration | CI/CD Suitability | Key Strengths | Key Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CadQuery | Python | Open-source library | OpenCascade (via OCCT/OCP) | No | Excellent; strong Jupyter and scripting workflow, with no separate CAD software required (pqac-00000008) | High | Parametric, programmable solid modeling; readable Python API; good for automation, testing, batch generation, and export pipelines; strong fit for reproducible engineering code (pqac-00000008) | Less interactive than mainstream GUI CAD; advanced surfacing/assemblies can require more coding expertise; manufacturability rules are not automatic |
| OpenSCAD | OpenSCAD DSL | Open-source application/language | CGAL-based CSG pipeline | Typically yes for normal use; headless CLI possible | None/very limited; not Python-native and requires separate software (pqac-00000008) | Medium | Extremely deterministic and reproducible; simple declarative CSG; excellent for scripted fixtures, enclosures, and parameterized hobbyist/mechanical parts | Weaker ecosystem interoperability; limited direct Python integration; less suited to complex fillets/freeform industrial geometry; editability is code-centric rather than feature-tree-centric (pqac-00000008) |
| build123d | Python | Open-source library | OpenCascade (OCCT) | No | Excellent | High | Modern Pythonic parametric CAD; close to CadQuery but with explicit algebra/mode-building APIs; strong type hints and testability; good for CI/CD and reusable CAD components | Smaller ecosystem/community than older tools; still code-first, so discoverability and interactive editing are weaker than GUI CAD |
| Onshape FeatureScript | FeatureScript | Proprietary cloud CAD language/platform | Onshape Parasolid-based cloud kernel | Yes, via Onshape cloud environment | Limited/indirect; accessible through APIs rather than native Python modeling | Medium-High | Native extension mechanism inside a production CAD system; robust parametric features; strong collaboration, versioning, and enterprise governance; good for custom feature authoring in real engineering workflows | Cloud-only and vendor-tied; proprietary language/platform; less suitable for offline/local builds; CI/CD depends on SaaS/API workflows rather than pure local execution |
| JSCAD | JavaScript | Open-source library/toolchain | CSG/B-rep-style JS geometry engine | No | Limited direct Python integration; strong JavaScript/Node ecosystem instead | High for JS-centric teams | Browser- and Node-friendly; easy web deployment; good for lightweight parametric CSG and custom configurators; accessible for full-stack developers | Less mature for heavy mechanical CAD than OCCT-based tools; complex fillets/shells/industrial geometry can be harder; manufacturability and engineering semantics are mostly user-enforced |


*Table: This table compares major parametric and code-driven CAD frameworks on language, kernel, scripting ergonomics, and CI/CD suitability. It is useful for distinguishing pure code-first CAD stacks from cloud-native feature authoring and browser-oriented geometry tools.*

**CadQuery** is a Python library for parametric 3D modeling built on the OpenCascade (OCCT) kernel. It requires no separate software, uses pure Python, has low modeling difficulty, and offers "excellent" integration with the Python ecosystem (Jupyter, scripting). Its headless operation makes it highly suited for CI/CD pipelines and automated batch geometry generation (pqac-00000008). Recent work on Text-to-CadQuery has demonstrated that CadQuery provides the best trade-off between usability and flexibility for programmatic shape synthesis, and is "well-suited for programmatic shape synthesis" (pqac-00000008).

**OpenSCAD** uses its own domain-specific language (DSL) with a CGAL-based CSG pipeline. It requires separate software for execution, has medium modeling difficulty, and shows no direct Python ecosystem integration (pqac-00000008). While deterministic and reproducible, its limited interoperability constrains its use in modern Python-centric automation workflows.

**build123d** is a modern Python library also built on the OCCT kernel. It serves as a spiritual successor to CadQuery with explicit algebra/mode-building APIs and strong type hints, making it well-suited for testable, CI/CD-integrated CAD generation.

**Onshape FeatureScript** is a proprietary language embedded within the Onshape cloud CAD platform. It enables custom feature authoring within a production-grade parametric system with robust collaboration and versioning. However, it is cloud-only and vendor-tied, limiting offline/local CI/CD workflows.

**JSCAD** provides JavaScript-based parametric CSG modeling that runs in browsers and Node.js environments, making it accessible for web-based configurators and full-stack teams. It is less mature for heavy mechanical CAD than OCCT-based tools.

Key limitations across code-based frameworks include: (i) manufacturability rules are not automatically enforced—users must encode process constraints manually; (ii) interactive visual editing is weaker compared to GUI CAD systems; and (iii) complex freeform/NURBS surfacing can require substantial coding expertise.

---

## 4. AI/LLM-Driven CAD Generation Research

The past three years have seen an explosion of research applying deep learning, LLMs, and diffusion models to automated CAD generation. The following table summarizes benchmark results for representative methods:

| Model | Year | Input Modality | Architecture | Coverage (COV%) | MMD | JSD | Valid% | Chamfer Distance (CD) | Other Key Metrics |
|---|---:|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| DeepCAD (ICCV) | 2021 | CAD command sequences / unconditional CAD generation | Autoregressive deep generative network for sketch-and-extrude CAD programs | 65.46 | 1.29 | 1.67 | 46.1 | Median CD 0.787 (autoencoder); CD 4.25 in reconstruction benchmark | Novel 87.4%, Unique 89.3%; ACCcmd 99.36, ACCparam 97.47, Invalid Ratio 3.30%; NC 0.49, IR 7.14 in reconstruction table (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006) |
| SkexGen | 2022 | Unconditional CAD construction sequences | Autoregressive generation with disentangled latent space for sketch-and-extrude CAD | 84.74 | 1.02 | 0.90 | — | MSLCD 0.81 | Novel 99.1%, Unique 99.8%, Human realism 46.9; sketch FID 18.56, Unique 96.02%, Novel 83.54%; cmd 83.6, μSLparam 1.48 (pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027) |
| HNC-CAD | 2023 | Incomplete or unconditional CAD models | Hierarchical neural coding with VQ-VAE + cascaded autoregressive transformers | — | — | — | — | CD 1.09 | HD 20.23, ECD 5.94, NC 0.75, IR 0.32; emphasizes controllable conditional generation/completion (pqac-00000005) |
| BrepGen (CVPR) | 2024 | Unconditional B-rep CAD generation | B-rep generative model | 71.26 | 1.04 | 0.09 | 62.9 | — | Novel 99.8%, Unique 99.7%; outperforms DeepCAD on unconditional generation benchmark (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000006) |
| Text-to-CadQuery | 2025 | Natural language text | Fine-tuned LLMs generating executable CadQuery Python directly | — | — | — | — | Median CD 0.191; mean CD 10.229×10^3 | Best accuracy 69.3%; 48.6% CD reduction; volumetric IoU median 0.9868; F1 median 0.9836; best model Qwen2.5-3B; Mistral-7B IR 1.32; GPT-4.1 mini fine-tuned achieved 0% invalid rate (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000013) |
| TCADGen + CADLLM | 2025 | Text descriptions with parameters/appearance cues | Transformer CAD sequence generator + LLM refinement | — | — | — | — | 3.12 | Sequence accuracy 0.864; average LCSratio 0.983; command-level metrics ≈0.966/0.947/0.962; large CD improvement versus baselines (e.g., 120.99–169.93 down to ~3–4) (pqac-00000012) |
| CADCodeVerify + GPT-4 | 2025 | Natural language prompts (+ visual verification loop) | Vision-language iterative verification/refinement of CAD code | — | — | — | 96.5 success rate | Median point-cloud distance 0.127 | GPT-4 few-shot + CADCodeVerify; zero-shot 0.132 / 94.0% success; uses Chamfer-style point-cloud distance, Hausdorff distance, IoGT (pqac-00000014) |
| GenCAD-3D | 2025 | Point clouds / meshes / multimodal geometry-to-CAD | Multimodal latent alignment + latent diffusion + synthetic data balancing | — | — | — | — | — | Reported to improve reconstruction accuracy, reduce invalid CAD generation, and improve high-complexity geometry performance, but excerpt did not provide numeric benchmark values (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000024) |
| RECAD (AAAI) | 2025 | Unconditional and conditional CAD generation | Dual diffusion networks over raster sketches + extrusion boxes | — | — | — | — | — | Strong unconditional generation and effective conditional generation/editing reported, but excerpt did not provide numeric benchmark values (pqac-00000012) |


*Table: This table compares representative AI/deep-learning CAD generation models through 2025 using the benchmark values available in the gathered evidence. It highlights where metrics are directly comparable and where recent papers report qualitative gains but not standardized numeric results.*

### 4.1 Foundational Generative CAD Models

**DeepCAD** (ICCV 2021) pioneered autoregressive deep generative networks for sketch-and-extrude CAD program synthesis. On unconditional generation benchmarks, DeepCAD achieved Coverage (COV) = 65.46%, MMD = 1.29, JSD = 1.67, with 87.4% novel and 89.3% unique outputs, but only 46.1% of generated models were valid sealed B-rep solids. In autoencoder reconstruction, it reports command accuracy (ACCcmd) = 99.36 and parameter accuracy (ACCparam) = 97.47 with a median Chamfer Distance (CD) of 0.787 and an invalid ratio of 3.30% (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006).

**SkexGen** (2022) introduced autoregressive generation of CAD construction sequences with a disentangled latent space separating sketch and extrusion properties. It substantially improved upon DeepCAD with COV = 84.74%, MMD = 1.02, JSD = 0.90, 99.1% novel and 99.8% unique outputs, and a human-perceived realism score of 46.9. For sketch generation, it achieved FID = 18.56 (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027).

**HNC-CAD** (2023) extended controllable generation via hierarchical neural coding with VQ-VAE and cascaded autoregressive transformers, achieving CD = 1.09 (versus DeepCAD's 4.25) and an invalid ratio of only 0.32 in reconstruction benchmarks, enabling novel interaction capabilities for conditional generation and completion tasks (pqac-00000005).

**BrepGen** (CVPR 2024) addressed direct B-rep generation with COV = 71.26%, MMD = 1.04, JSD = 0.09, and 62.9% valid outputs—substantially improving validity over DeepCAD while maintaining 99.8% novelty and 99.7% uniqueness (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000006). More recent work by **BrepGPT** (2025) introduced a single-stage autoregressive framework using Voronoi Half-Patch representation for unified B-rep generation.

### 4.2 Text-to-CAD and LLM-Driven Generation

**Text-to-CadQuery** (2025) proposed generating executable CadQuery Python code directly from natural language, bypassing intermediate command sequences. The best fine-tuned model (Qwen2.5-3B) achieved 69.3% accuracy (up from 58.8% baseline), reduced Chamfer Distance by 48.6%, and attained a volumetric IoU median of 0.9868 and F1 median of 0.9836. The work demonstrated clear scaling benefits: even the smallest model (124M parameters) reached higher accuracy with only 58 minutes of training compared to 2 days for the Text2CAD baseline. A GPT-4.1 mini fine-tuned on 10k examples produced 0% invalid rate but did not exceed accuracy of the 3B model, highlighting that larger LLMs improve executability but require sufficient task-specific data to improve 3D generation accuracy (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000013).

**TCADGen + CADLLM** (2025) combined a Transformer-based CAD sequence generator with LLM-based refinement (Llama 3.2 3B), achieving a sequence-level accuracy of 0.864 and dramatically reducing Chamfer Distance from 120.99–169.93 (baseline transformers) down to approximately 3.12, representing orders-of-magnitude geometric improvement (pqac-00000012).

**CADCodeVerify** (2025) introduced iterative visual verification for LLM-generated CAD code. GPT-4 with few-shot prompting and CADCodeVerify achieved a median point-cloud distance of 0.127 and 96.5% success rate, outperforming Gemini 1.5 Pro (0.147, 85.0%) and CodeLlama (0.185, 73.5%) (pqac-00000014).

**Seek-CAD** (2025) pioneered the use of locally deployed open-source inference LLMs (DeepSeek-R1) for training-free CAD parametric model generation with visual and chain-of-thought feedback in a self-refinement loop.

**CAD-Coder** (2025) fine-tuned DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B to generate CAD script codes with geometric annotations, achieving CD = 0.88 with comments versus 1.31 without, demonstrating the importance of annotation quality for LLM-based CAD generation (pqac-00000010).

### 4.3 Multimodal and Diffusion-Based Approaches

Diffusion models are increasingly applied to CAD generation. **RECAD** (AAAI 2025) employs dual diffusion networks generating raster sketches and extrusion boxes, offering advantages in continuous latent space interpolation and intuitive user control. **GenCAD-3D** (2025) uses multimodal latent space alignment with contrastive learning and latent diffusion models, augmented by synthetic dataset balancing (SynthBal), to improve reconstruction accuracy on high-complexity geometries (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000024).

A structured review identified GANs as the most common architecture for direct design generation (9/14 studies in a systematic sample), with conditional GANs incorporating physical parameters (stress fields, aerodynamic conditions) to produce more physically informed outputs. However, GAN outputs suffer from pixelation, aliasing, and fuzzy edges, and are generally used as starting points for downstream processing rather than final geometries (pqac-00000030, pqac-00000032). Diffusion models are predicted to be adopted in engineering GD as they mature, though a representational mismatch persists between neural pixel/voxel formats and engineering-standard CAD formats (pqac-00000031).

### 4.4 Agentic CAD Pipelines

A framework for automating CAD generation using LLM agents with function calling and agent workflows evaluated five LLMs across four agent workflows. The agent workflow incorporating automated visual feedback outperformed others, especially with multimodal LLMs like ChatGPT-4o. A case study demonstrated its application to topology optimization and additive manufacturing with minimal human input (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000021).

---

## 5. Constraints and Limitations

### 5.1 Manufacturability

Across all approaches, manufacturability remains a critical under-addressed challenge. Commercial TO/GD tools can encode manufacturing constraints (e.g., AM build directions, minimum feature sizes), but outputs typically require extensive post-processing before they are production-ready (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000003). AI-generated CAD models fundamentally "overlook key aspects such as manufacturability or the practical application of each geometry," with frameworks that focus on raw geometry creation lacking built-in DfM checks (pqac-00000017). Current AI approaches "reason about geometry only, without validation from a physical simulation," limiting confidence for functional and manufacturing requirements (pqac-00000018). Research suggests integrating AI-based manufacturability checkers (e.g., surrogate models acting as MLP-based DfM filters) into late-stage optimization to improve producibility (pqac-00000031).

### 5.2 Editability and Parametric Controllability

A fundamental tension exists between AI-generated outputs and traditional parametric CAD workflows. Many generative models produce non-parametric representations (point clouds, meshes) that "require manual adjustments to meet exact design intent" and lack design history or CAD interoperability features essential for downstream modification (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021). Tokenization and quantization approaches used in sequence-based CAD generation produce coordinate precision loss and unstable predictions, causing misaligned or disconnected components (pqac-00000018). Current methods do not explicitly capture high-level geometric constraints such as symmetry, orthogonality, or parallelism, so edits can break design intent (pqac-00000018). The code-generation paradigm (e.g., Text-to-CadQuery) partially addresses editability by outputting executable parametric scripts, but this approach is currently limited to the sketch-and-extrude subset of CAD operations (pqac-00000024).

### 5.3 Scriptability and CI/CD Integration

Code-based frameworks like CadQuery and build123d offer the strongest CI/CD integration due to their Python-native, headless operation and excellent scripting ecosystem support (pqac-00000008). OpenSCAD provides deterministic reproducibility but limited Python integration. Commercial TO/GD tools are largely GUI-bound with API access varying by vendor. The emerging Text-to-CadQuery paradigm bridges AI generation with CI/CD-friendly code output, leveraging the fact that LLMs already excel at Python generation (pqac-00000009).

### 5.4 Data and Benchmark Limitations

A critical limitation across AI-driven CAD is dataset imbalance: the standard DeepCAD dataset contains 178,238 models but only covers sketch-and-extrude programs, a small subset of real engineering geometries. High-complexity CAD programs are underrepresented, causing models to "excel on simple shapes but struggle with more intricate ones" (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000024). Standard evaluation metrics are "dominated by results on simple examples," potentially masking failures on complex industrial parts (pqac-00000020). Surrogate models face high data generation costs (reported in 56.8% of surveyed articles) and accuracy concerns (30% of articles), requiring trade-offs between training investment and performance (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000033).

---

## 6. Summary and Outlook

The generative CAD landscape in 2025 spans a spectrum from mature commercial TO/GD platforms delivering proven mass-reduction results (>40–60% savings) through simulation-driven optimization, to rapidly advancing AI/LLM methods that are beginning to generate valid, editable parametric CAD from natural language. Code-based frameworks like CadQuery and build123d occupy a critical bridging role, enabling both traditional parametric modeling and emerging LLM-driven generation within CI/CD-compatible pipelines. Key remaining challenges include closing the gap between generated geometry and manufacturable, fully parametric CAD; expanding training data beyond simple sketch-and-extrude vocabularies; integrating physics-based validation into generative loops; and developing foundation surrogate models that generalize across engineering domains (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000031). The convergence of diffusion models, agentic LLM pipelines, and structured CAD representations represents the most promising frontier for achieving production-grade AI-assisted design automation.

contexts.context: The excerpt contrasts shape (including topology) optimization with generative design and notes that Autodesk Fusion 360 includes both capabilities. Shape optimization is described as a late-stage process used to remove material and reduce weight/costs when material constraints exist; it typically starts from a rough design, rebuilds a mesh in a CAD system, uses simulation to predict performance, and automatically modifies geometry—producing a single optimized solution. Generative design, by contrast, requires explicit inputs for constraints, loads, and manufacturing/process specifications and generates a set of iterative candidate models across objectives like cost and materials. The cube group project example illustrates practical constraints (ABS, 40% volume, 1000 lbf) and the need to consider manufacturing (final parts 3D printed in PLA). Implicit limitations cited include the need to rebuild meshes for shape optimization, shape optimization yielding one solution (limiting exploration), and generative design’s dependence on well-specified constraints and manufacturability settings. The excerpt provides general capability/limitation themes but does not detail features or limits specific to nTopology, Siemens NX, PTC Creo, ANSYS Discovery, Altair Inspire, or Rhino+Grasshopper.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities, features, and limitations of commercial generative design and topology optimization tools including Autodesk Fusion, nTopology, Siemens NX, PTC Creo, ANSYS Discovery, Altair Inspire, and Rhino+Grasshopper?

snippets: Shape optimization typically occurs near the end of the design process when a final product needs weight and cost reductions due to material constraints [7].

Figure 4 shows the process for shape optimization, which uses simulation technology to predict the design performance, and automatically makes the changes to improve the design and accelerate product development processes.
As seen in the figure, the optimization process starts with a rough design which is used to rebuild a mesh model in a CAD system.
This is in contrary to generative design which requires constraints, loads, and manufacturing processes based on design specifications.
Thus, shape optimization leads to one design solution, while generative design provides a set of iterative models based on cost, materials, and other design specifications.
Autodesk Fusion 360, which includes generative design and shape optimization tools
Constraints included ABS plastic, 40% of volume and 1000 lbf load.
In the end, each group was required to use PLA 3D printing to make the final product as shown in Figure 6.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes general capabilities and limits of generative design and topology optimisation tools relevant to product design. Capabilities: they can automatically generate variants by iterating constraint combinations (load cases, mesh density, boundary envelopes), save concepts, and produce designs useful at early and later development stages; simulation outputs can inspire effective structural strategies. Features include integration with physical prototyping (3D printing) and compatibility with other fabrication methods (CNC), and some CAD tools offer smoothing of simulation outputs. Limitations: raw topology results are often ‘lumpy’ and unattractive for visible consumer products and usually require further stylistic refinement, sketching and manual CAD rework; more refined outputs may still need additional detailing, FEA and printing for production analysis. Other considerations: the tools may encourage design fixation or constrain divergent thinking if misused, can alienate designers unfamiliar with the methods, and should be taught in design education. Overall, these tools augment rather than replace traditional design practice and are most powerful when combined with manual refinement and consideration of materials, aesthetics and varied load/use cases.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities, features, and limitations of commercial generative design and topology optimization tools including Autodesk Fusion, nTopology, Siemens NX, PTC Creo, ANSYS Discovery, Altair Inspire, and Rhino+Grasshopper?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes capabilities, workflows, and limitations for several commercial generative-design tools. Fusion 360 uses topology optimization to identify optimal material distribution, generates many design iterations that users can visualize and validate via simulation, and in case studies achieved significant mass reduction (e.g., 705 g to 586 g). CogniCAD (introduced 2018) is focused on GD/AM generation but cannot create geometries natively—users must import STEP files from an external CAD system and configure loads/grid settings. Solid Edge’s generative tools are limited in the student edition; full functionality requires a licensed version and workflows typically start from a main-body sketch with geometry-preservation areas and cut-outs. Siemens NX, combined with Simcenter HEEDS, can automatically generate and compare hundreds to thousands of optimized options and integrate NX geometry with Simcenter CAE for solutions targeted at additive manufacturing. The table of algorithms highlights trade-offs: methods like genetic algorithms suit complex multi-objective optimization but can be computationally expensive and slow to converge; swarm approaches have fast convergence but risk local optima; AI approaches can learn and optimize complex dynamic systems yet demand significant compute and large datasets. These points summarize practical strengths and limitations observable in commercial GD/topology tools in the excerpt.

contexts.context: The excerpt outlines general capabilities, common features and key limitations of generative design (GD) and topology optimisation (TO) approaches relevant to commercial tools. GD methods encode rules to generate many candidate forms, helping avoid designer fixation and producing multiple embodiments rapidly. TO is performance-driven (often using FEM) and includes material-distribution methods such as SIMP and BESO; it excels at structural light-weighting but tends to produce voxelised density fields. Major limitations include sensitivity to input definitions (loads/boundaries), local optima, high computational cost for high-resolution problems, and difficulty capturing complex, multi-case design requirements within a single TO run. A practical challenge is translating voxel TO outputs into manufacturable parametric CAD (B‑splines, contours, polygons, or fitting to control points). For additive manufacturing, DFAM constraints must be integrated early, and GD/TO are recommended as exploratory tools to survey solution spaces rather than as turnkey net‑design solvers.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities, features, and limitations of commercial generative design and topology optimization tools including Autodesk Fusion, nTopology, Siemens NX, PTC Creo, ANSYS Discovery, Altair Inspire, and Rhino+Grasshopper?

contexts.context: The paper reports use of Autodesk Fusion 360 for topology optimisation within a product-design practice study. Fusion 360 was used to generate multiple design alternatives by varying envelope size, symmetry, force location/magnitude, mesh density and protected regions. The solver requires a user-entered mass-reduction target (authors note typical 30–40% and set 40%) and runs cloud-based simulations. Results are visualised with load-colour mapping (red high load, blue low) and the interface provides an on-screen slider to vary the mass-reduction interactively. Optimised outputs can be exported as polygon meshes and physically prototyped (authors printed models in ABS on an FDM UP Box). The study used steel as the material for designs and notes that, while metal additive manufacturing could produce complex forms, current 3D metal printing is not commercially viable for an MTB crank arm. The authors caution this work is a design-practice investigation rather than an engineering validation and recommend technical guidelines if used for engineering projects. They also highlight that topology tools aid iterative idea generation across constraint combinations but do not substitute detailed engineering assessment.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes capabilities and some practical constraints of topology optimisation and generative-design tools relevant to product design education. Tools like nTopology and Materialise 3-Matic can generate and manipulate lattice structures driven by FEA data, which is beneficial for powder-based additive manufacturing. Autodesk Fusion 360 was chosen for the authors' topology optimisation experiment because it is accessible to students and offers practical training; SolidWorks is noted as another viable option. Typical solver behaviour requires user inputs (e.g., a mass reduction percentage, often 30–40%) and then removes material from a shape envelope to maximize stiffness. Multiple design alternatives can be produced by varying envelope size, symmetry, force location/magnitude, and mesh density. Limitations highlighted include the need for manual interpretation of many generated alternatives for manufacturability and performance, the potential for designer fixation, and that most tools are primarily engineer-focused rather than tailored for product-design education. contexts.context: The excerpt describes Autodesk Fusion 360's generative design workflow and general shape/topology optimization concepts. Fusion 360 generative design requires a partial (rough) model plus boundary conditions (preserve/obstacle geometries, loads, materials, manufacturing processes, and objectives). It runs a series of calculations—a statistical method combined with stress analysis—using cloud-based compute to produce multiple candidate solutions. Users select or further constrain designs based on metrics like weight, cost, stiffness, and manufacturability; designs can be modified by adding constraints. Advantages cited include faster, lighter, and cheaper designs and support for manufacturing constraints. Limitations noted include the need for extensive cloud computational resources and reliance on correct input constraints and models. Shape/topology optimization is characterized as an algorithmic, material-removal process typically applied late in the design cycle to reduce weight/cost; it rebuilds a mesh in CAD and generally yields a single optimized solution rather than multiple explorations. The excerpt does not provide tool-specific capabilities or limitations for nTopology, Siemens NX, PTC Creo, ANSYS Discovery, Altair Inspire, or Rhino+Grasshopper, so no direct comparisons are available here.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes key capabilities and some limitations of contemporary generative design (GD) and topology optimisation (TO) tools. Autodesk Fusion 360's Generative Design module can "generate the three-dimensional form of a part given a set of geometric and force parameters," supports designer-guided filtering of results, and lets operators apply manufacturing constraints so outputs are more manufacturable; its exuberant results are often better suited to additive manufacturing (AM). GD more broadly enables users to "generate and explore (a multitude of) alternative design proposals" and to "analyse and evaluate them," and increasingly the software tools can be "relocated at the centre of the design process." Common GD algorithms include L-systems, shape grammars, cellular automata, genetic algorithms and swarm intelligence. On TO, Altair's Optistruct was an early commercial product; TO workflows typically "remove material volume from a three-dimensional CAD model until a goal is reached," e.g., to "find the stiffest possible design using the least material." The literature notes many TO tools exist but are predominantly engineer-focused. The authors also distinguish TO for efficiency goals from topology manipulation for aesthetic changes, and observe AM remains best suited to small-batch or niche production—limiting practical manufacturing options for some GD/TO outputs.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities, features, and limitations of commercial generative design and topology optimization tools including Autodesk Fusion, nTopology, Siemens NX, PTC Creo, ANSYS Discovery, Altair Inspire, and Rhino+Grasshopper?

contexts.context: The excerpt provides benchmark results for DeepCAD and a recent model BrepGen. In unconditional generation (Table 7), DeepCAD (ICCV’21) reports Coverage (COV) = 65.46, MMD = 1.29, JSD = 1.67, Novel = 87.4%, Unique = 89.3%, and Valid = 46.1%. BrepGen (CVPR’24) outperforms DeepCAD on these metrics with COV = 71.26, MMD = 1.04, JSD = 0.09, Novel = 99.8%, Unique = 99.7%, and Valid = 62.9%. For DeepCAD autoencoder variants (Table 8), DeepCAD + Aug achieves ACCcmd = 99.50, ACCparam = 97.98, Median CD = 0.752, Invalid Ratio = 2.72%; the base DeepCAD autoencoder reports ACCcmd = 99.36, ACCparam = 97.47, Median CD = 0.787, Invalid Ratio = 3.30%. The excerpt defines Coverage, Valid (sealed B-rep solid), and Chamfer Distance (CD), clarifying that CD is used to measure point-cloud similarity. snippets: Coverage (COV): The proportion of generated data points that have at least one reference data point that matches after assigning each generated data point to its nearest reference data point (based on chamfer distance).
Valid: The percentage of generated B-rep data that is a sealed solid.
Chamfer Distance (CD): Calculates the distance metric between two sets of points.

contexts.context: This excerpt defines the evaluation metrics and experimental setup used in the Drawing2CAD (DeepCAD) work but does not provide numerical benchmark results for DeepCAD, Text2CAD, SkexGen, Point2CAD, or other models. The paper uses Mean Chamfer Distance (MCD) to measure geometric discrepancy between predicted and real shapes and reports an Invalidity Ratio (IR) measuring the percentage of generated CAD operation sequences that fail to produce valid 3D shapes. Command-level metrics include Command Type Accuracy (ACCcmd) and Parameter Accuracy (ACCparam). For parameter prediction the authors introduce a softened Parameter Loss: a cross-entropy-style loss with smoothed target distributions (˜y_k) that decay with distance from the true discrete parameter category, using 𝛼=2.0 and tolerance=3 to permit small deviations. Implementation details relevant for reproducing or comparing results: command/parameter embeddings use 8-bit quantization to 257-d one-hot encodings, outputs are turned into CAD via an OpenCASCADE CAD kernel, and training ran on an NVIDIA RTX 4090 with batch size 256 for 200 epochs using Adam (lr=0.001) with linear warm-up (2k steps), dropout 0.1 and gradient clipping 1.0. No numeric Chamfer, IoU, coverage, or validity rates for the named baseline models appear in the provided excerpt.
contexts.question: What are the benchmark results and performance metrics for DeepCAD, Text2CAD, SkexGen, Point2CAD, and other AI-based CAD generation models? Include Chamfer distance, IoU, coverage, and validity rates.

Chamfer Distance (CD): Measures the geometric discrepancy between the generated and ground-truth 3D models [14].
Invalid Rate (IR): Captures the percentage of generated CadQuery scripts that fail during execution, due to syntax errors, incomplete expressions, or invalid operations.
Gemini 2.0 Flash Evaluation: Assesses the similarity between the generated and ground-truth shapes purely from a vision-language perspective [46, 55].

The fine-tuned Qwen2.5-3B model achieves the best overall results: a Chamfer Distance of 0.191 (median) and 10.229 (mean) after ×103, along with a Gemini 2.0 Flash accuracy of 69.30%, a 10.5% absolute improvement over the baseline.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes the programming-based CAD paradigm: models are defined by source code in a text editor, compiled, and rendered in a viewer. It lists OpenSCAD, CadQuery, and JSCAD among common examples. Key strengths noted are easy scripting of repetitive/automatic actions, precise explicit geometric definitions (dimensions and math expressions), support for complex programmatic geometries (e.g., via recursion), and advantages for versioning and collaborative workflows. Typical interfaces offer limited direct/manipulative interaction (mainly camera/view controls). Important limitations discussed are the learning barrier to programming and that few CAD tools fully adopt a programmatic workflow. The excerpt does not provide framework-specific technical details (API, CI/CD hooks, build123d, Onshape FeatureScript, or manufacturability considerations) nor does it discuss CI/CD integration, deployment, or manufacturing export pipelines. Thus it supports general statements about scriptability and precision but not the specific CI/CD or manufacturability limitations for the named frameworks.

snippets: Programming-based or programmatic interfaces [138] allow the creation of 3D models using a textual description in the form of source code.
Normally, programming-based applications provide a text editor (1) to describe a model with a programming language. The application compiles and transforms a visual representation in a viewer (2).
Some examples of programming-based CAD applications are OpenSCAD [160], Cadquery [35], JSCAD [159], BRL-CAD [38], ImplicitCAD [131], and IceSL [127].
Programming brings highly appreciated advantages to 3D design [41, 228]. Repetitive and automatic actions are easily scripted [89].

precision is an important advantage on programming-based CAD applications. Geometric properties can be defined with exact dimensions or expressed through mathematical expressions that assure exactitude in the designs.
Versioning and collaborative works are also remarkable advantages of designing with programming-based CAD compared to direct manipulation.

Commonly, programming-based applications have a text editor in which users create the code, a compile button, and a view where the applications display the visual representation of the model, which usually offers few or no interaction capabilities except to change the position and angle of the view to inspect the model (Figure 9).
One of the reasons for this is possibly the difficulties that learning to program presents, which can be an entry barrier for users [111].

contexts.context: The excerpt focuses mainly on OpenSCAD and general issues with programming-based CAD. OpenSCAD is described as the most popular open-source parametric CSG programming-based CAD application, using a functional declarative language edited in text, compiled and rendered in a viewer. It targets CAD (not artistic modeling), supports CSG and 2D outline extrusion, and offers preview and render modes; preview supports interactive mapping from rendered CSG parts back to the source code (right-click → menu → places the text cursor in the line of code). OpenSCAD includes modifier characters for debugging and has preferences for integrating with fabrication workflows (e.g., connecting with OctoPrint or exporting STL for 3D printing). The text lists other parametric/programming-based CAD systems (IceSL, JSCAD, BRL-CAD, ImplicitCAD, RapCAD) but provides no detailed feature descriptions for them. It also highlights recurring user challenges across programming-based CAD: structuring problems, finding features and documentation, reading and reusing code, aligning/selecting/refactoring parts, and issues related to 3D printing and transferring physical measurements into digital designs.

snippets: OpenSCAD is the most popular programming-based CAD application with no real competitor [148, 236].
OpenSCAD is an open-source parametric CSG programming-based CAD application.
Users can describe 3D models in its functional declarative programming language using the text editor, and the system compiles and renders the scripts in a viewer.
OpenSCAD preferences menu offers features such as connecting with OctoPrint [90], a web interface to control consumer 3D printers or export models into the standard STL format for 3D printing [43].
OpenSCAD is not an interactive modeler and does not focus on the artistic aspects of 3D modeling but on the CAD aspects [160].

In preview mode, OpenSCAD allows the user to right-click on the models in the view to display a menu of the CSG elements that create the clicked part. The user can click on a menu item while the system places the text cursor in the line of code that creates the CSG element.
OpenSCAD language includes modifiers5 for debugging.
such as IceSL [127], JSCad [159], BRL-CAD [38], ImplicitCAD [131], or RapCAD [20].

programming-based CAD users may present similar challenges, such as difficulties in structuring and breaking down the problem into smaller problems, difficulty finding the features the programs offer, or documentation problems.
These problems include difficulties reading code, re-using code, aligning objects, selecting parts, refactoring code, and 3D printing.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes distinctions relevant to programming-based parametric CAD tools. OpenSCAD is highlighted as an open-source, parametric, CSG-based programming CAD system: users write models in a functional/declarative text language which the system compiles and renders; it is 3D-printing oriented and supports export features (e.g., STL, OctoPrint integration). CSG is explained as a primitives-plus-boolean-operations paradigm (binary tree of primitives/operations), offering concise, mathematically well-formed, watertight models but with limited surface-detail (difficulty with smooth curves) and potentially long rendering times for complex CSG trees. By contrast, BREP (e.g., FreeCAD) explicitly stores faces/edges/vertices, enables operations like chamfer/blend/drafting, supports parametric history trees and spreadsheet-driven parameters, and exposes scripting (FreeCAD’s Python console) for automation. The text also notes the Topological Naming Problem (TNP) in parametric direct-manipulation CAD—history-dependent references can break when topology changes—and that direct modeling omits history entirely. These points imply trade-offs: CSG/scriptable systems like OpenSCAD suit reproducible, script-first 3D-print workflows but may lack manufacturability-focused surface/BREP ops and can be slower for complex parts; BREP-based systems better support manufacturing operations and richer scripting/automation but face history fragility (TNP) that can complicate robust CI/CD pipelines.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities and features of CadQuery, OpenSCAD, build123d, Onshape FeatureScript, and JSCAD as parametric code-based CAD frameworks? What are their limitations for CI/CD, scriptability, and manufacturability?

contexts.context: The excerpt reports user experiences with programming-based CAD (OpenSCAD). Users value parametric, code-based models because "everything is written down" and can be revisited later, and they create or import digital replicas of physical objects to ensure fit. However, many participants found spatial transformations difficult: relating code transformation parameters to on-screen spatial coordinates is challenging due to limited visual cues and the camera/view orientation. The OpenSCAD view only provides a widget for the canonical x, y, z axes, which is often insufficient when working with nested or non-canonical coordinate systems. Translates and rotations are noncommutative and hard to reason about, so users resort to trial-and-error, checkpoints, or module patterns to manage transforms. Calculating correct transformation parameters can require painful mathematical reasoning. The excerpt contains detailed observations about OpenSCAD’s usability strengths and weaknesses but does not discuss CadQuery, build123d, Onshape FeatureScript, JSCAD, CI/CD pipelines, or explicit scriptability and manufacturability workflows for those other frameworks.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities and features of CadQuery, OpenSCAD, build123d, Onshape FeatureScript, and JSCAD as parametric code-based CAD frameworks? What are their limitations for CI/CD, scriptability, and manufacturability?

I rotate first and translate later. It is easier because when I translate before rotation, sometimes the rotation center is not the same as the object’s center.

because everything is written down . . . if I pick up something one year later, I’ll remember ex- actly what I did.
if I’m making something to fit another object that already exists that (. . . ) I’d like to have a model of that object in my CAD software so that I can build around it or in it

contexts.context: The excerpt provides observations about code-based parametric CAD tools, with direct notes about OpenSCAD, JSCAD, IceSL, and RapCAD. OpenSCAD centralizes model definition in a single code editor and offers a backward search to jump from a view selection to code, but lacks visual confirmation cues so users must edit to verify changes. JSCAD and OpenSCAD commonly limit in-view interactions, preventing selection of parts to extract and reuse geometric information. RapCAD is given as an example that does allow extracting an object’s position into code variables for reuse. IceSL gives stronger visual highlighting of code-to-geometry correspondences but cannot discriminate between contributing statements in a CSG structure. Broader challenges reported include a disconnect between the editing space and the rendered view, difficulty navigating which code statements produce particular parts, problems understanding nested spatial transformations and coordinate systems, and fragmented parameter-definition workflows (e.g., multiple panels or spreadsheets in other parametric tools). The excerpt does not contain specific information about CadQuery, build123d, or Onshape FeatureScript, nor explicit discussion of CI/CD, scriptability pipelines, or manufacturability workflows.

snippets: so users have three different views for creating a parametric model, while in OpenSCAD, everything is defined in one place, the code editor.
users are forced to understand the connections between the code and the rendered model, but current tools barely try to help users in this task.

OpenSCAD provides a backward search (section 2.1.4) to place a cursor on a code statement based on a selected part in the view. However, it does not provide visual cues to confirm that the selected part is aimed, and users need to modify the code to seek visual confirmation.

IceSL [127] provides better visual cues to highlight code statements responsible for creating parts in the view but does not discriminate between statements involved, so it is impossible to navigate the CSG structure.
Applications such as OpenSCAD or JSCAD often limit interactions within the view, preventing users from selecting specific parts or re-using information, for example, the position of an object.

Certain applications such as RapCAD [20] permit such operations within the code. For instance, users can use functions to extract an object’s position as a parameter, store it in a variable, and later use this information to define another element.

Understanding 3D spaces on a 2D screen is a difficult task reported in direct manipulation [88]. Our interview revealed that this problem can be more difficult in programming-based CAD where the editing space is disconnected from the view, and no visual help to relate them.
These constraints are also stored as a list in a different panel.

contexts.context: The paper evaluates CAD-code generation using three geometric metrics: a point-cloud distance defined in Equation 8 (a Chamfer-style bidirectional nearest-neighbor average), Hausdorff distance, and Intersection over Ground Truth (IoGT). Generated meshes are aligned via ICP and normalized to fit in a unit cube; failures to compile are penalized by assigning the maximum point distance (√3) and IoGT = 0. Experiments use zero-shot and few-shot (five demonstrations) protocols across GPT-4, Gemini 1.5 Pro, and CodeLlama. Table 2 reports median Point Cloud (Chamfer-like) distances and success rates: GPT-4 few-shot with CADCodeVerify achieves 0.127 median point-cloud distance and 96.5% success; GPT-4 zero-shot CADCodeVerify 0.132 and 94.0% success. Gemini and CodeLlama show higher point-cloud distances and lower success rates (e.g., Gemini few-shot CADCodeVerify 0.147 / 85.0%; CodeLlama few-shot CADCodeVerify 0.185 / 73.5%). The geometric solver (accessing ground truth) is presented as an upper-bound for refinement. The excerpt does not report explicit "exact match" metrics nor any experiments involving fine-tuning of LLMs for CAD code generation; models were used via API in zero-/few-shot setups.

snippets: Point Cloud distance:  Point Cloud distance D(P, Q) is shown in Equation 8
We apply the Iterative Closest Point (ICP) algorithm to rotate and translate the generated 3D object to choose the best possible alignment with the ground truth object (Besl & McKay, 1992).
Finally, we normalize each point cloud such that it fits within a unit cube, as per prior work (Zheng et al., 2023).
In some cases, the generated CAD code does not compile, which prevents the rendering of a valid 3D object. We report the percentage of successfully compiled code as the “success rate.”
When computing these distances, we assign the maximum distance of two points within a unit cube, i.e.,   √3, and an IoGT value of zero to any data point where the code fails to compile.
The feedback from the geometric solver is a numerical value across thirteen categories of geometric information.
This feedback method serves as an upper limit for CAD code refinement, as it conveys the precise geometric differences between the generated 3D object and the ground truth.
In the few-shot setting, we provide five demonstration examples, with each example consisting of a 3D object description followed by the corresponding code.
We utilize CADPrompt to quantitatively evaluate the capabilities of CADCodeVerify approach across various VLMs.
Gemini: Few-shot      CADCodeVerify (Ours)   0.939 (0.052)      0.147 (0.160)        0.492 (0.358)       85.0%
CodeLlama: Few-shot   CADCodeVerify (Ours)   0.935 (0.957)      0.185 (1.620)        0.582 (1.366)       73.5%
GPT-4 exhibits the highest capacity to generate compile-able code on CADPrompt.
With regards to “success-rate,” GPT-4 exhibits superior performance 96.5% on the CADPrompt benchmark compared to Gemini 85% and CodeLlama 73.5%.

contexts.context: The Text-to-CadQuery benchmark reports state-of-the-art CAD generation results using CadQuery code output. A fine-tuned Qwen2.5-3B model achieved a Flash accuracy of 69.30%, a 10.5% absolute improvement over the baseline, and shows the best geometric scores (F1 median 0.9836, volumetric IoU median 0.9868). The paper evaluates geometric fidelity with a point-based F1 (10,000 sampled surface points, threshold τ = 0.02) and volumetric IoU (voxelization resolution 0.02). Larger models do not automatically yield better accuracy: Mistral-7B underperforms Qwen2.5-3B on accuracy metrics though it attains the lowest Invalid Rate (1.32), indicating better syntactic/executable code generation. A GPT-4.1 mini fine-tuned on 10k examples produced fully executable code (0% Invalid Rate) but its accuracy matched the 7B model, highlighting that larger or more capable LLMs improve executability but still require sufficient task data to improve 3D generation accuracy. The dataset contains 170,000 text–CadQuery pairs and the authors observe clear scaling benefits up to 3B, with potential underfitting for 7B due to limited data.

fine-tuned a commercial model, GPT-4.1 mini, on a 10k subset of our data. This model achieved a 0% Invalid Rate—producing fully executable code—but its accuracy matched that of the 7B model, confirming that advanced models are more reliable in execution but still require sufficient data to learn precise 3D generation.
we introduce two supplementary metrics, F1 Score and Volumetric IoU, to further evaluate the geometric fidelity of generated shapes.

We adopt a point-based F1 score to evaluate the alignment between predicted and ground-truth meshes. For each shape, we sample 10,000 surface points and compute the bidirectional recall and precision using a fixed distance threshold τ = 0.02 in normalized space.

Volumetric IoU: To further assess 3D shape similarity, we compute the volumetric Intersection over Union (IoU) between voxelized versions of the predicted and ground-truth meshes ... voxelized using a resolution of 0.02.
Qwen2.5-3B achieves the best performance across both F1 and IoU metrics, with an F1 median of 0.9836 and a volumetric IoU median of 0.9868.
This holds across multiple metrics, such as CD, F1 Score, and IoU, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance for CAD generation.
our dataset includes 170,000 samples

as model size increases, accuracy improves steadily from 124M to 3B. Similar to the previous observations, the 7B model (Mistral) does not further improve over Qwen2.5-3B, likely due to underfitting from limited data.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports benchmark results for text-to-CAD sequence generation showing large benefits from combining transformer predictors with an LLM-based refinement (CADLLM). Command-level prediction (Table 1) shows TCADGen+CADLLM achieving very high metrics (e.g., row: "TCADGen+CADLLM (Ours)  0.966  0.947  0.962 ..."). For sequence-level generation (Table 2), CADLLM + TCADGen attains accuracy 0.864 and average LCSratio 0.983, outperforming prompt-only and non-LLM combinations. Fine-tuning the Llama 3.2 3B model (SFT) improves performance versus prompt-only (0.621 vs 0.328 accuracy; LCSratio 0.883 vs 0.688). Chamfer Distance (Table 3) is dramatically reduced when adding CADLLM: baseline Transformer methods have CDs of 120.99–169.93, CADFusion 45.67, while LLM-refined combinations yield CD ≈ 3–4 (e.g., "TCADGen+CADLLM(ours)  3.12 (↓117.87)"). MMD and JSD also drop substantially with CADLLM. The excerpt does not explicitly report an "exact match" metric, but reports accuracy, F1, AUC, LCSratio, and geometric distances (CD/MMD/JSD) illustrating that LLM fine-tuning/refinement yields large improvements in both symbolic command accuracy and geometric fidelity.

contexts.context: The paper introduces Text-to-CadQuery, a paradigm that directly generates executable CadQuery Python code from natural language, removing intermediate command sequences. The authors expanded the Text2CAD/DeepCAD data to create roughly 170,000 text–CadQuery pairs for supervised fine-tuning. They fine-tuned six open-source LLMs spanning 124M to 7B parameters (CodeGPT-small 124M; GPT-2 medium 355M; GPT-2 large 774M; Gemma3-1B; Qwen2.5-3B; Mistral-7B with LoRA). Across these models they report consistent improvements and a clear scaling trend: larger models yield higher generation accuracy. Quantitatively, their best fine-tuned model attains a top-1 exact match of 69.3% (up from 58.8%) and reduces Chamfer Distance by 48.6%. They also note efficiency benefits: even the smallest model reached higher CAD generation accuracy with only 58 minutes of training compared to 2 days reported for Text2CAD. The authors attribute gains to leveraging pretrained models’ Python-generation and spatial reasoning strengths when trained on code-level CAD supervision.
contexts.question: Text-to-CadQuery benchmark results: exact match, Chamfer distance reduction, LLM fine-tuning for CAD code generation performance

snippets: To enable this, we augment the Text2CAD dataset with 170,000 CadQuery annotations.
This results in a new dataset of approximately 170,000 text–CadQuery pairs, enabling direct training for Text-to-CadQuery generation.
We fine-tune six open-source LLMs of varying sizes and observe consistent improvements. Our best model achieves a top-1 exact match of 69.3%, up from 58.8%, and reduces Chamfer Distance by 48.6%.
These include CodeGPT-small (124M), GPT-2 medium (355M), GPT-2 large (774M) [33], Gemma3-1B [40], Qwen2.5-3B [52], and Mistral-7B [21] (using LoRA).
we observe a clear scaling trend: as model size increases, so does generation accuracy.
even our smallest model achieves higher CAD generation accuracy with only 58 minutes of training—versus 2 days for Text2CAD.
We introduce a new paradigm for CAD generation that directly produces executable CadQuery code from natural language, bypassing intermediate command sequences.
This approach simplifies the modeling pipeline and improves accuracy by leveraging both the Python generation and spatial reasoning capabilities of pretrained language models.

contexts.context: The excerpt details the Text-to-CadQuery data annotation and model fine-tuning pipeline but does not report explicit benchmark metrics such as exact-match rates or Chamfer distance reductions. It describes a semi-automatic annotation pipeline that converted minimal JSON descriptions into CadQuery using Gemini 2.0 Flash, with a self-correction feedback loop that increased code execution success from 53% to 85%. The team annotated 170,000 CadQuery samples; GPT-4o produced >90% executable outputs on first attempt but was costlier (roughly 20× the API cost of Gemini 2.0 Flash). Gemini 2.0 Flash was chosen for annotation because it offered competitive quality, much lower cost, higher throughput (up to 2000 RPM and 4M tokens/min), and lower latency, allowing the full annotation to finish in ~9 hours for $170. For model selection and fine-tuning, Text2CAD Transformer architecture is described (340M encoder + 23M decoder = 363M total), and a range of decoder-only LLMs from 124M to 7B parameters were evaluated. Data splits were 90/5/5, and training used full-parameter SFT for small models and PEFT (LoRA with 4-bit quantization, r=16, α=32) for large models. Training times ranged from under an hour (124M) to ~33 hours (Mistral-7B) on available hardware. No explicit exact-match, Chamfer reduction, or LLM fine-tuning performance numbers are given in this excerpt.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes the Text-to-CadQuery work: the authors build on large foundational CAD datasets (DeepCAD: 178,238 command-sequence models; Text2CAD extends DeepCAD with multi-level natural language annotations, producing ~170k models and >600k prompts). They adopt text as the sole input modality and use CadQuery as the high-level output representation. The methodology centers on fine-tuning pretrained large foundation models (varying model scales) to generate executable CadQuery code. The paper claims that larger model capacity improves symbolic 3D generation and that they systematically evaluate models of different scales, with Section 4 covering evaluation metrics and experimental results. Prior symbolic-CAD datasets and baselines noted include Query2CAD (57 examples) and LLM4CAD (~5k CadQuery samples), motivating the need for a larger, diverse dataset. The excerpt, however, does not provide numeric benchmark results (no exact-match rates, Chamfer distance reductions, or concrete fine-tuning performance numbers). For precise values (exact match, Chamfer reduction, quantified effects of LLM fine-tuning), the full paper's results section (Section 4) must be consulted.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports benchmark and ablation results for a Text-to-CadQuery/CAD-Coder setup. Evaluation uses pass@k, APR, ACC-G/ACC-P/ACC-F and Chamfer Distance (CD) computed after converting DXF to point clouds (Comparecloud) and measuring CD with Open3D. Fine-tuning details: multi-round fine-tuning of DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B on 29,130 samples (~2.27M tokens) on one V100 (lr=0.0002, batch=4, seq=1048, 2 epochs). LoRA is described (layer increment equations shown) and the authors state: “we conclude that the LoRA method is more suitable for our task, as our dataset is not very large, and the LoRA method helps reduce the occurrence of overfitting.” Quantitative results show Chamfer distance improvements for the fine-tuned CAD-Coder variants: CAD-Coder with comments CD=0.88 vs without comments CD=1.31 (better when annotated); adding 3D training produced CD=0.72 in one variant. Compared to baselines, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B yields CD=0.88, Llama baseline CD=1.20, CodeLlama 1.76, Qwen-Coder 2.10, indicating Chamfer distance reduction after LLM fine-tuning and dataset/annotation choices. contexts.context: The excerpt reports Text-to-CadQuery evaluation setup and quantitative results for multiple fine-tuned LLMs. Training used token-level cross-entropy loss and a 1024-token context window; Mistral-7B was adapted with LoRA (4-bit quantization, rank r=16, α=32; ∆W = AB low-rank updates). Evaluation metrics are Chamfer Distance (CD), Invalid Rate (IR), and Gemini 2.0 Flash. The baseline Text2CAD Transformer has median CD 0.370 and mean CD 26.417 (×10^3), Gemini 58.80%, IR 3.5. Fine-tuned CadQuery-output models improve substantially: CodeGPT-small (124M, SFT) median CD 0.234, mean 13.520, Gemini 60.28%; Qwen2.5-3B (3B, SFT) achieves the best reported median CD 0.191 and mean 10.229, Gemini 69.30%, IR 6.5. Mistral-7B fine-tuned with LoRA reports median CD 0.219, mean 11.835, IR 1.32, Gemini 65.38%. The authors note CodeGPT-small’s advantage likely stems from Python-code pretraining. The excerpt does not report an "exact match" metric for generated code; geometry-focused CD, execution IR, and perceptual Gemini scores are provided instead.

contexts.context: The excerpt highlights several limitations and challenges relevant to AI/LLM-driven CAD generation. Many generative models output non-parametric representations ("non-parametric data such as point clouds and meshes") or other free-form shapes that lack CAD’s parametric structure and editability, so such outputs often "require manual adjustments to meet exact design intent," which is especially problematic where strict tolerances and precise fits are needed. Creating and editing CAD programs remains a specialist skill ("manually creating CAD programs is itself a complex skill that typically takes years to master"), and existing reverse-engineering tools still demand substantial expert input, "making the process expensive and time-consuming." A core data limitation is dataset imbalance: "High-complexity CAD programs... are underrepresented, causing models trained on this data to excel on simple shapes but struggle with more intricate ones." Standard evaluation metrics "are dominated by results on simple examples," which can mask failures on complex, high-complexity geometries. Together these factors lead to generated models that can be non-editable, insufficiently parametric for engineering needs, error-prone for complex designs, and often require human post-processing to be manufacturable or fit for downstream use.
contexts.question: What are the limitations of AI/LLM-driven CAD generation in terms of manufacturability, editability, parametric controllability? What are challenges with generated CAD models?

contexts.context: The excerpt identifies several limitations and challenges for AI/LLM-driven CAD generation relevant to manufacturability, editability, and parametric control. First, the framework primarily focuses on raw geometry creation and "overlooks key aspects such as manufacturability or the practical application of each geometry," suggesting a lack of built-in design-for-manufacturing (DfM) checks or process-specific constraints (e.g., for AM, injection molding, milling). Second, generated models suffer from accuracy and robustness issues: some LLMs repeatedly fail specific primitives ("failed to generate the right-angle geometry accurately") and less capable models can produce no viable geometries at all. Complex tasks (topology optimization, patterning, mirroring) lower success rates significantly. Third, error detection and iterative correction are limited by the workflow's visual capabilities — "reliance on a single perspective" hampers the LLM's ability to find and fix mistakes; the authors propose "multiple views with spatial annotations" and "visual aids, such as sketches" to improve interpretability. Finally, the paper recommends extending agent workflows with explicit DfM guidelines and validated case studies to improve manufacturability and practical application, implying current outputs may be hard to edit, validate, or control parametrically without further tooling and constraints.
contexts.question: What are the limitations of AI/LLM-driven CAD generation in terms of manufacturability, editability, parametric controllability? What are challenges with generated CAD models?

contexts.context: The paper highlights several limitations of AI/LLM-driven CAD generation relevant to manufacturability, editability, and parametric control. Diffusion- and LLM-based approaches "have limited ability to produce exact CAD models from engineering constraints" and "still struggle to adhere to precise requirements such as dimensional constraints or accurate positions of features," undermining reliable parametric controllability and tight tolerances needed for manufacturability. Generated geometry often "lack[s] typical features like design history or CAD interoperability," which reduces editability, downstream modification, and integration with CAD toolchains. Broader challenges include "model hallucination" and the "limited availability of specialized engineering training data," causing incorrect or fabricated features that can break manufacturability and require human correction. The reliance on text-only I/O and weaknesses in spatial reasoning ("current LLMs primarily rely on text input and output"; "limitations in spatial reasoning") further limit the system's ability to capture 3D constraints and produce robust, editable parametric models. The authors also note "prompt dependency" and "workflow adaptability" as constraints, and recommend future work on improving "design-for-manufacturing capabilities, visual tools, and evaluation benchmarking."
contexts.question: What are the limitations of AI/LLM-driven CAD generation in terms of manufacturability, editability, parametric controllability? What are challenges with generated CAD models?

contexts.context: The paper notes several limitations relevant to AI/LLM-driven CAD generation. First, synthetic augmentation (SynthBal) improves performance on existing benchmarks but "synthetic data may not fully capture the complexities inherent in industrial designs, potentially limiting generalization." Second, the training/evaluation data are narrow: "the current DeepCAD dataset only contains Sketch-and-Extrude CAD programs, a small subset of engineering geometries," which restricts exposure to commands, feature types, and real-world part variability. Third, practical parametric and editable CAD requires handling of more diverse CAD vocabularies and specialized program constructs: "it is important to collect and experiment on datasets with larger CAD vocabularies, and perhaps develop architectures better suited to handling special commands such as those that require references to compiled B-Rep geometries." Overall, the authors emphasize that despite performance gains, "certain limitations remain," pointing to dataset realism, limited CAD program types, and the need for architectures that support B-Rep references and richer CAD operations for better generalization, parametric controllability, and practical editability.
contexts.question: What are the limitations of AI/LLM-driven CAD generation in terms of manufacturability, editability, parametric controllability? What are challenges with generated CAD models?

contexts.context: The excerpt identifies several limitations and challenges of AI/LLM-driven CAD generation relevant to manufacturability, editability, and parametric control. Generated design data often lacks sufficient accuracy, making conversion into high-quality, editable CAD models difficult—especially where industrial precision is required. LLMs are computationally expensive and inefficient, and designers find it hard to guide them with simple text or visual prompts toward reasonable, manufacturable designs. Prior methods focus on data synthesis rather than producing robust modeling histories or parametric control; available datasets often lack modeling-process information and natural-language annotations needed for editable, parametric models. Specific model behaviors can harm editability and parametric controllability: for example, DeepCAD generates sketches before extrusion parameters, producing disconnected sequences and only supporting basic operations. The paper suggests hybrid approaches—using finely tuned small generative models to provide accurate task-level guidance combined with large models—to mitigate reasoning uncertainty, but overall challenges remain in ensuring manufacturability, maintaining parametric editability, and producing precise, connected modeling sequences that are practical for industrial workflows.
contexts.question: What are the limitations of AI/LLM-driven CAD generation in terms of manufacturability, editability, parametric controllability? What are challenges with generated CAD models?

Most current research on combining LLMs with CAD focuses on the data synthesis ability of these models, such as generating design information by processing large datasets.

DeepCAD uses a Transformer model to generate CAD sequences (Wu et al., 2021). It breaks down CAD modeling into sketches and extrusion steps. However, it generates sketches before extrusion parameters, often creating disconnected sequences. It also supports only basic modeling operations.

The small generative models can provide more accurate guidance for specific tasks, compensating for the limitations of the large models in detailed reasoning and thus improving the design synthesis capabilities of large models and reduce the uncertainties that often arise in the reasoning process (Fang et al., 2024).

contexts.context: The excerpt documents several practical limitations of AI/LLM-driven CAD generation and editing. Tokenization/quantization approaches (used by CAD-Editor) produce coordinate precision loss and unstable token predictions, causing misaligned or disconnected components and even split models. Sketch-and-extrude token representations limit scalability to complex primitives and datasets lacking construction history. Modeling B-rep latent spaces is challenging because they are variable-length; padding/unpadding introduces noise that degrades performance. The methods do not explicitly capture or enforce high-level geometric constraints (e.g., symmetry, orthogonality, parallelism), so edits can break design intent and reduce generalization to real-world CAD workflows. Models can also misinterpret textual prompts (notably counting-based instructions). Finally, current approaches are purely text- and geometry-driven and lack physical or functional validation — the model “reasons about geometry only, without validation from a physical simulation,” which limits confidence for functional/manufacturability requirements. Together these issues point to challenges in manufacturability, editability, parametric control, and downstream functional validation of generated CAD models.
contexts.question: What are the limitations of AI/LLM-driven CAD generation in terms of manufacturability, editability, parametric controllability? What are challenges with generated CAD models?
